title: A Unified Open Quantum System Investigation of Leakage Suppressed Transmon Qubit Dynamics Via Parametric Pulse Spectral Engineering, Purcell Limited Dissipation Control, and Dispersive Readout Phase Space Analysis
authors: Hilal Ahmad Bhat, Farooq A. Khanday
abstract: Superconducting transmon qubits form the backbone of today's leading quantum processors, where high field dispersive readout relies fundamentally on the shape and temporal structure of microwave control pulses. While transmon qubits offer improved robustness against charge noise, their performance is still strongly influenced by environmental coupling, particularly the Purcell effect and by the spectral characteristics of the readout pulses used for state preparation and logic operations. In this study, we present a rigorous comparative analysis of qubit coherence dynamics using measurement under four relevant configurations: a single-step rectangular pulse without Purcell decay, a single-step pulse subject to Purcell-induced relaxation, a two-step shaped pulse without Purcell effects, and a two-step shaped pulse incorporating Purcell decay. By solving the time-dependent Lindblad master equation for a three-level transmon system, we illustrate how readout pulse shaping and Purcell filtering independently and jointly influence qubit population dynamics, excited-state lifetime, and the final-state fidelity. The results demonstrate that two-step readout pulse shaping significantly improves coherence by suppressing overshoot and reducing higher-level population transfer, while the inclusion of Purcell mitigation further stabilizes the dynamics and enhances fidelity. The most notable observation is that the combined application of pulse shaping and Purcell filtering produces the most stable and highest fidelity qubit state discrimination. This unified perspective constitutes the central novelty of our work. Such a comparative framework, integrating pulse engineering with environmental decay modeling, has received limited explicit treatment in prior literature. The findings provide critical insights for optimizing both readout protocols and quantum hardware design in large scale superconducting quantum systems.

title: Real-Time Human Fall Detection from Video Using YOLOv11 with Pose Estimation: A Paradigm Shift Towards Efficient Transformer-Based Architectures
authors: Adel Benabdennour, Mahmoud Sameh, Bilal A. Khawaja, Arshad Karimbu Vallappil, Abdulmajeed M. Alenezi, Qammer H. Abbasi, Sameer Qazi
abstract: The increasing global aging population and the associated risk of falls necessitate the development of efficient and reliable automated human fall detection systems. This paper presents a novel two-stage, lightweight deep learning pipeline designed for real-time fall detection. The proposed architecture combines a state-of-the-art pose estimation model, You-Only-Look-Once (YOLO)v11-Pose, with a compact transformer network for temporal classification. The methodology includes a robust data preprocessing pipeline, careful data partitioning, and systematic ablation studies across architectural components, hyperparameters, and input features. Evaluation on a combined dataset of 6,988 videos demonstrates the model’s high performance. The best F1 model achieved an accuracy of 97.96%, an F1-score of 97.74%, a Precision of 97.74%, and a Recall of 97.74% on the held-out test set. Cross-dataset validation on the LE2I dataset achieves 91.65% F1-score without fine-tuning, demonstrating generalization capability. The full pipeline requires only 142MB VRAMand achieves 76.2 FPS on a Tesla P100, exceeding real-time requirements by 2.5×, confirming its suitability for deployment in real-time monitoring systems. The findings demonstrate the effectiveness and computational efficiency of the transformer-based approach for safety-critical fall detection applications.

title: Unplugged but Empowered: Investigating the Impact of 3Ps on Computational Thinking Activities
authors: Izzah Sakinah, Tze Ying Sim, Corrienna Abdul Talib, Sian Lun Lau
abstract: In the 21st century, while technical skills remain vital, employers increasingly prioritize critical thinking and problem-solving which signify core components of computational thinking (CT). The unplugged CT methodology does not require resources like computers, making it a viable method especially to even low-resourced communities. This study investigates the impact of unplugged CT activities on lower secondary school students’ CT concepts and to improve their programming skills and interest. The activities are structured around three key categories: puzzles, problem-solving, and programming concepts (3Ps), and are delivered through an unplugged approach. Using pre- and post-tests, the study evaluates the effectiveness of each category in enhancing students’ CT capabilities. The findings reveal statistically significant improvements (p < 0.05) across all three categories, indicating that unplugged CT activities can positively influence student learning. Additionally, the study identifies and categorizes suitable CT and programming concept activities by drawing from previous unplugged and plugged CT research. This paper also presents the discussions of 3Ps and students’ perceptions on CT understanding and programming concept improvement through correlation results. These discussions contribute to the field of CT education by demonstrating the efficacy and scalability of integrating 3P-based activities into unplugged learning environments, particularly in Malaysian secondary schools.

title: Toward Realistic Synthetic Ultrasound Images with Pose Vectors
authors: Khanabhorn Kawattikul, Kazuhiko Hamamoto
abstract: Synthesizing realistic and anatomically consistent B-mode ultrasound (US) images under arbitrary ultrasound probe (US-probe) motion remains a challenging problem due to the conflicting requirements of pose-dependent anatomical alignment and stochastic speckle texture modeling. Generative models must preserve structural consistency governed by six-degree-of-freedom (6-DoF) US-probe poses while simultaneously reproducing realistic ultrasound speckle statistics. This study investigate architectural decomposition strategies for pose-conditioned ultrasound image synthesis. We propose a Three-Generator conditional GAN (CGAN) that explicitly separates translation-aware anatomical layout, rotation-dependent structural refinement, and speckle-specific texture generation into dedicated generator branches. To evaluate the effectiveness of this design, we conduct extensive comparisons against Single- and Two-Generator CGAN variants, as well as a conditional diffusion baseline, evaluated under a unified evaluation protocol. Model performance is assessed using a comprehensive evaluation protocol covering standard image fidelity metrics, ultrasound-specific realism measures, anatomy-preservation metrics, pose–image alignment, and efficiency of computation. Experimental results on a phantom dataset demonstrate that architectural decomposition consistently improves anatomical fidelity, speckle realism, and pose consistency. In particular, the proposed Three-Generator pose-conditioned CGAN achieves the most balanced performance across all evaluation criteria while maintaining stable and efficient inference. The results indicate that explicit architectural separation provides an effective framework for high-fidelity, pose-aware ultrasound image synthesis.

title: Magnetic Soft Strain Sensor Based on Hall Device_supp1-3672232.docx
authors: Tingting Yu
abstract: Soft strain sensors are soft electronic devices capable of quantifying mechanical deformation, and they have been extensively explored in human health monitoring, and soft robotics. However, conventional resistive and capacitive soft strain sensors encounter a trade-off between sensing range and circuit complexity. To address this limitation, this work proposes a magnetic soft strain sensor based on a Hall device. The sensor consists of a silicone elastomer, a Hall sensor, a permanent magnet, and two rigid clamping fixtures. The fixtures are located at both ends of the sensor, with the Hall device embedded inside. Applying tensile strain increases the relative distance between the Hall device and the magnet embedded in the silicone, resulting in a reduced magnetic flux density at the Hall sensor and thereby enabling strain detection. This work investigates the influence of the magnet polarity and geometrical parameters on the sensing performance. In addition, the effect of the initial spacing between the Hall device and the magnet is also analyzed. Experimental characterization demonstrates that the proposed magnetic soft strain sensor achieves a gauge factor of 0.11 and exhibits excellent linearity (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.999). The sensor shows no signal drift under cyclic tensile loading, indicating stability. Finally, the feasibility of the magnetic soft strain sensor for human motion and health monitoring applications is demonstrated.

title: D2TriPO-DETR: Dual-Decoder Triple-Parallel-Output Detection Transformer
authors: Menghao Pu, Chaoqun Han, Zhiping Chai, Yifan Zhou, Pu Wen, Jihong Zhu, Chao Wang, Han Ding, Xuguang Lan, Zhigang Wu
abstract: Vision-based grasping, though widely employed for industrial and household applications, still struggles with object stacking scenarios. Current methods face three major challenges: limited inter-object relationship understanding; poor grasping adaptation across different viewpoints; and error propagation. To address the above challenges, we propose D2TriPO-DETR, a dual-decoder transformer with three outputs, of which are object detection, manipulation relationship, and grasp detection. Specifically, a distributed attention perception module and a rotation attention invariance module are designed to address limited interobject relationship understanding and poor grasping adaptation across different viewpoints. These two modules are respectively integrated into the two parallel decoders to output the triple results simultaneously, partly eliminating task-level error propagation. Experimental results on the visual manipulation relationship dataset indicate that D2TriPO-DETR outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods across all metrics, e.g., +6.1% object detection recall, +6.7% manipulation relationship image accuracy, and +1.5% grasp detection accuracy. Extensive real-world experiments and quantitative results validate D2TriPO-DETR’s effectiveness.

title: Distributed Games in Dynamic Systems: Theory, Learning, and Applications
authors: Shuai Liu, Longcheng Liu, Qing-Long Han, Lihua Xie, Xiuxian Li
abstract: Game theory has emerged as a fundamental framework for modeling and analyzing strategic interactions and decision-making among multiple agents, and has witnessed rapidly growing impact in cyber–physical systems over the past decade. Its integration with dynamic systems has driven major theoretical and technological advances in a wide range of applications, including smart grids, autonomous driving, robotic swarms, and networked control systems. In particular, distributed games in dynamic systems and their equilibrium learning mechanisms have attracted increasing attention due to their scalability, lightweight information exchange, and real-time implementability. This article provides a comprehensive survey of distributed games in dynamic systems, where agents interact only with local neighbors while collectively achieving global equilibrium and stability. First, the foundational theories of distributed dynamic games under three representative classes of systems: linear dynamic systems, nonlinear dynamic systems, and uncertain dynamic systems, are presented. Then, state-of-the-art distributed equilibrium learning and control methods are reviewed, including gradient-based dynamics, payoff-based learning, best-response dynamics, and learning-based approaches. To demonstrate the practical relevance and impact of distributed games in dynamic systems, representative application domains are discussed in detail. Finally, several promising future research directions are outlined, highlighting open challenges at the intersection of distributed games, learning, and dynamic systems.

title: SAFF: A Spatially-Aware Fusion Framework for Effective and Efficient Aerial Object Detection
authors: Hongru Xiao, Jiankun Zhuang, Bin Yang, Jinming Hu, Junze Zhu, Zhen Lian, Sijie Zhao, Yanmin Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Aerial-based object detection aims to accurately locate vehicles, personnel, and other targets, providing critical support for smart cities and traffic management. While some methods have made partial progress in addressing challenging detection scenarios by fusing infrared (IR) and visible (RGB) modalities, challenges remain in optimizing cross-modal fusion efficiency and balancing the computational cost of additional modalities with detection accuracy. These challenges represent significant barriers to the progress of real-time aerial multimodal detection. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a lightweight IR-RGB fusion framework designed to enhance cross-modal fusion efficiency and balance the disparity between computational cost and detection accuracy. Specifically, a Spatially-Aware Fusion Framework (SAFF) based on the characteristics between IR and RGB modalities is designed, enabling efficient processing of cross-modal and cross-scale feature interactions, thereby significantly reducing computational costs. Moreover, a Semantic-Spatial Fusion Module (S2FM) is proposed, which leverages spatial location information from the IR modality to enhance the single RGB-based detector, facilitating complementary improvements in both location accuracy and discrimination capabilities. Experimental validation on three multimodal detection datasets (DroneVehicle, VEDAI, and M3FD) using Yolov11 and Yolov12 as single-modal baseline detectors shows that the proposed multimodal framework achieves a 12.2%–19.2% reduction in FLOPs, along with 2.6%–5.3% and 2.7%–7.1% improvements in mAP and mAP50, respectively, demonstrating a superior balance between detection accuracy and computational efficiency.

title: Agents4PLC: Automating Closed-loop PLC Code Generation and Verification in Industrial Control Systems using LLM-based Agents_supp1-3667895.pdf
authors: Dongxia Wang
abstract: In industrial control systems, the generation and verification of Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) code are crucial for ensuring operational efficiency and safety. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have made strides in automated code generation, they fall short in providing correctness guarantees and specialized support for PLC programming (which has its own programming language and clear logical structures). To address these challenges, this paper introduces Agents4PLC, a novel framework that not only automates PLC code generation but also introduces code-level verification and repair built upon an LLM-based multi-agent system, which together is capable of directly producing operational PLC code without any human interaction. To comprehensively evaluate our framework, we first establish a new benchmark specially designed for the critical area of verifiable PLC code generation, which includes hundreds of natural language requirements, human-written and verified formal specifications, and finally reference PLC code. Then, we carefully designed a multi-agent workflow combining a set of expert agents responsible for different code generation tasks including planning, coding, validation and debugging towards generating correct PLC code. For each agent, we also incorporate optimization strategies such as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), advanced prompt engineering techniques, and Chain-of-Thought strategies which are shown to be effective to enhance the ability of these expert ‘agents’. Evaluation against the benchmark demonstrates that Agents4PLC significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving superior results across a series of increasingly rigorous evaluation metrics. This research highlights the potential of LLM agent-based code generation in real-world industrial control systems and the importance of code-level verification in generating correct code with formal guarantees.

title: Study on Aging Performance of DC Support Capacitors Based on MMC Converter Valve Full-Scale Test Platform
authors: Xiang Huang, Jiang Guo, Mengyi Xu, Jiao Zhou, Wenjing Zhang, Yankun Fan, Kai Chen
publish: 2025 4th Asia Power and Electrical Technology Conference (APET)
abstract: Flexible DC transmission employs voltage-source converters composed of fully controllable power electronic devices. Compared to thyristor-based HVDC transmission systems, it offers greater operational flexibility and enhanced system reliability. DC support capacitors serve as the core components of flexible DC converter valves. Currently, both domestic and international research on the performance of drytype DC capacitors under all operating conditions remains limited, with most studies focusing on testing capacitor films under pure AC or pure DC conditions. This paper establishes a full-scale test platform for MMC converter valves based on a power module-to-drag test circuit topology. By incorporating a full-bridge module array into the drag circuit and employing direct current control to impart current-source characteristics to the array, the platform reproduces the stress profiles and operational behavior of actual MMC single modules. Using this platform, long-term accelerated aging tests were conducted on DC support capacitors with different structures. Results indicate that when self-healing failures occur internally, DC leakage current typically exceeds 100mA. Critical conditions for capacitance degradation were identified for both high-resistancefilm and segmented-film DC support capacitors, providing valuable insights for enhancing their reliability.

title: A Single-Phase Grounding Fault Section Location Method for Distribution Networks Based on Modified Graph Autoencoder and Cluster Analysis
authors: Zijie Zheng, Zuli Jiang, Wei Gao, Xiaofang Li, Yitao Jiang
publish: 2025 4th Asia Power and Electrical Technology Conference (APET)
abstract: The secure and stable operation of power distribution networks largely depends on the rapid and accurate identification of faulty sections. However, existing deep learning methods exhibit limited generalization capability in handling topological changes and rely heavily on large amounts of labeled data for training. To address these issues, we proposed a fault section localization method for distribution networks based on a modified graph autoencoder. First, the adjacency matrix is used to represent the connection relationships between nodes in the distribution network, which, along with the node feature matrix, serves as the input to the model. Then, an improved graph autoencoder is designed to learn complex relational features between nodes in an unsupervised manner through an encoderdecoder structure. After the model training is completed, the reconstruction error of each node is calculated and normalized to obtain an anomaly score for each node. Finally, the Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) algorithm is employed to classify the anomaly scores, thereby identifying the faulty section. Simulation and field test results demonstrate that the proposed method exhibits excellent fault section localization capability and adaptability under various fault conditions, noise interference, and asynchronous sampling scenarios.

title: ScopeTags: Self-Knowledge Tokens for Reliable Retrieval-Augmented Generation in Multimodal Systems
authors: Muhammad Taimoor Hassan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: This paper presents a retrieval-augmented generation framework that uses model-internal "scope tokens" to decide when to retrieve, which evidence to keep, and how to reconcile multiple sources before answering. Three lightweight controls—retrieve gating, per-document relevance scoring, and cross-document consistency filtering—turn the LLM’s own knowledge boundary into a governance signal for retrieval and context curation. The approach slots into standard multimodal pipelines and reduces wrong answers from irrelevant or contradictory context while preserving helpful evidence for encyclopedic visual and textual queries. Across knowledge-intensive question-answering benchmarks, these controls improve accuracy and robustness over strong multimodal baselines without requiring external annotators or architectural overhauls. By elevating self-knowledge to a first-class signal, the method reframes RAG from passive grounding to active, token-level control of retrieval and fusion.

title: A Novel Authentication Scheme for Smart Homes
authors: Irfan Alam, Zubair Ashraf, Mohammad Muzakkir Hussain, Abdul Rahman, Jaluddin Khan, Mohd Asim Siddiqui
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Today’s life cannot be imagined without smart homes, which are designed to provide both convenience and automation. Unfortunately, the security gaps that exist in the infrastructure frameworks of smart homes can lead to significant loss of property and even life. These settings depend on devices on the Internet of Things (IoT), which have some of the most serious security issues, especially with respect to device authentication and key management. Although traditional cryptographic methods could work, they are often too slow and overly complicated for basic IoT gadgets. This article proposes a lightweight authentication scheme based on Shamir’s Secret Sharing (SSS), cryptographic hash functions (SHA-3), and an XOR based session key generation. The proposed system is capable of securely distributing the secrets of authentication and provides pseudonyms to the reconstructed secrets through Lagrange interpolation. The system exploits hashing and XOR to deliver fast authentication while being not vulnerable to MITM, replay, or key compromise attacks. The security evaluation proves the effectiveness and dependability of the protocol and its applicability in IoT settings with limited resources.

title: Advanced Spectrum Sensing in Cognitive Radio Networks: Riemannian Geometry Techniques
authors: Khaja Kamaluddin
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Innovations in Engineering, Science and Technology for Sustainable Development (ICEST)
abstract: In order to increase spectrum efficiency, Cognitive Radio Networks (CRNs) let secondary users (SUs) to access underutilised licensed bands without interfering with main users (PUs). Spectrum sensing is a fundamental feature of CRNs that guarantees the uninterrupted operation of PUs. When applied in noisy or low SNR situations, traditional detection techniques frequently fail. Modelling signal covariance matrices on manifolds using Riemannian geometry has improved detection robustness and accuracy in recent years. With a review of previous research, an explanation of the mathematical underpinnings, the proposal of a novel Riemannian Distance Detector (RDD), and simulation findings, this work offers a thorough investigation of the use of Riemannian geometry in spectrum sensing. This paper presents two experiments: the first one to detect PU in low SNR conditions; and the comparison of RDD with conventional detection approaches in the second experiment. Findings suggest that, compared to conventional detection methods, RDD outperformed them. It is found that computational complexity is moderate and offers excellent performance at high noise levels.

title: A Survey: Named Entity Recognition for Legal Documents
authors: Asmit Aditya, Aman Kumar, Sachin Kumar, Manju Verma, Kapil Kumar
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: Law files, such as judgments, contracts, and legislative documents, have a lot of value but can be extremely boring. intricate, and hard to understand. Manual extraction of useful name of cases, judges, courts and the petitioners etc. respondents, statutes, and dates is time-consuming and errorprone. Named Entity Recognition (NER), the solid foundation of Natural Processing (NLP), has been proved as an effective tool to resolve the challenge. NER approaches have established extensive advancements over the years, from rule-based and statistical techniques to neural network models and, more recently, transformer models. Rule-based methods and Conditional Random Fields were accurate but non-scalable, while neural architectures such as BiL STM and BiL STMCRF enhanced contextual understanding but struggled in extremely long legal texts. Transformer models such as BERT and its legal-domain counterparts, such as Legal-BERT, Ro BERTa-Legal, Big BERTa, and Indian Legal BERT, have published cutting-edge findings, with F1-measures greater than 90% in expert applications. However, there remain problems with multilingual processing, the availability of domain-specific data, computational cost, and handling low-resource legal systems. This survey summarizes earlier research, compares different approaches, and unearths research gaps, as well as outlines directions for matters to further advance NER in the legal domain.

title: AutoCluster, AutoTopicModeling, AutoTrendAnalysis: A Complete AutoML Pipeline for Predicting Emerging Trends
authors: Ahmed Abolfadl, Marwa Mahmoud Abla, Mervat Abu-Elkheir, Maggie Mashaly
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Predicting emerging trends is vital for businesses, researchers, and policymakers; yet traditional approaches often lack scalability and adaptability. This paper presents a trend prediction framework based on Automated Machine Learning (AutoML), designed to extract insights from textual datasets with temporal attributes. The system ingests subject-specific textual entries accompanied by a date field. The pipeline begins with preprocessing and embedding, followed by AutoClustering, which uses meta-learning to select the optimal clustering algorithm. AutoTopicModeling then applies successive halving to identify the best topic modeling method—Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA), BERTopic, or Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF)—based on the coherence score for each cluster. For trend forecasting, AutoTrendAnalysis evaluates multiple models—Facebook Prophet, AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA), Seasonal-Trend decomposition using Loess (STL), and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)—selecting the most accurate based on Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), either through successive halving or exhaustive comparison. Topics are classified as strong signals, weak signals, or noise based on forecasting outcomes, enabling the identification of emerging trends. By automating clustering, topic modeling, and time series forecasting, this research enhances trend prediction accuracy while reducing manual effort. The proposed system offers a scalable and user-friendly solution suitable for real-time applications and stakeholders with limited machine learning expertise. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed system’s best trial achieves a final RMSE of 7.099, indicating high predictive accuracy.

title: An Ethics-included Ontological Model for the Design and Evaluation of Digital Twins Technology for Spatial Planning
authors: John van Meerten, Koen Smit, Sam Leewis, Johan Versendaal
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 9th International Conference on Software and e-Business
