title: Informer-Based High-Order Geometrical Factor for Modeling Array Induction Logging in Layered Formations
authors: Ping Qiao, Lei Wang, Shaogui Deng, Zhen Chen, Honglian Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: This paper presents an Informer-based framework for efficient simulation of array induction logging (AIL) responses in layered isotropic and anisotropic formations. To overcome the limitations of computationally expensive pseudo-analytical methods and data-driven approaches with poor generalization, we propose a residual response learning scheme based on the geometrical factor (GF) theory. The method decomposes the total response into a low-order GF approximation and a high-order residual response learned through a physics-guided Informer-based framework. The Informer’s ProbSparse self-attention mechanism reduces time and memory complexity from O(L2) to O(LlogL), enabling efficient handling of long depth-series AIL data. Its multi-scale feature fusion and generative decoder further enhance the model’s ability to capture interlayer dependencies and avoid cumulative prediction errors. In addition, a custom loss function incorporating electromagnetic continuity and geological background constraints ensures physically consistent predictions. For layered anisotropic formations, the framework employs a two-level scheme involving an inversion-based equivalent isotropic resistivity transformation followed by learning of anisotropic residual response. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the method achieves 96% to 98% prediction accuracy while providing a computational speedup of nearly two to three orders of magnitude compared to conventional pseudo-analytical methods. The new method maintains robust performance across varying dipping angles and anisotropy coefficients, offering an accurate and efficient solution for real-time AIL response simulation.

title: Temporal Conditioning for Longitudinal Brain MRI Registration and Aging Analysis
authors: Bailiang Jian, Jiazhen Pan, Yitong Li, Fabian Bongratz, Ruochen Li, Daniel Rueckert, Benedikt Wiestler, Christian Wachinger
abstract: Longitudinal brain analysis is essential for understanding healthy aging and identifying pathological deviations. Longitudinal registration of sequential brain MRI underpins such analyses. However, existing methods are limited by reliance on densely sampled time series, a trade-off between accuracy and temporal smoothness, and an inability to prospectively forecast future brain states. To overcome these challenges, we introduce TimeFlow, a learning-based framework for longitudinal brain MRI registration. TimeFlow uses a U-Net backbone with temporal conditioning to model neuroanatomy as a continuous function of age. Given only two scans from an individual, TimeFlow estimates accurate and temporally coherent deformation fields, enabling non-linear extrapolation to predict future brain states. This is achieved by our proposed inter-/extrapolation consistency constraints applied to both the deformation fields and deformed images. Remarkably, these constraints preserve temporal consistency and continuity without requiring explicit smoothness regularizers or densely sampled sequential data. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TimeFlow outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both future timepoint forecasting and registration accuracy. Moreover, TimeFlow supports novel biological brain aging analyses by differentiating neurode-generative trajectories from normal aging without requiring segmentation, thereby eliminating the need for labor-intensive annotations and mitigating segmentation inconsistency. TimeFlow offers an accurate, data-efficient, and annotation-free framework for longitudinal analysis of brain aging and chronic diseases, capable of forecasting brain changes beyond the observed study period.

title: The bar-tip limit error in bar charts: Exploring its relationship to the within-the-bar bias
authors: Daniel Reimann
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: The present study suggests that the bar-tip limit error-assuming the raw data are limited by the bar-tip-assessed with one question, moderates the within-the-bar bias-rating values inside the bar as more likely than those outside the bar-and that this bias is resilient to reduction through explanation.

title: Dual-Branch Aesthetic Image Retouching Via Active Reinforcement Learning for Color Enhancement and Composition Optimization
authors: Dong Liang, Yifan Liu, Yuanhang Gao, Sheng-Jun Huang, Songcan Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Existing learning-based visual retouching primarily focuses on improving image quality through end-to-end objective mapping between input and retouched images. However, these approaches often overlook two critical aspects: the progressive nature of image retouching and the subjective aesthetic preferences, resulting in suboptimal visual outcomes. To address this, we introduce Automatic Aesthetic Image Retouching via active reinforcement learning (A <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{3}$</tex-math></inline-formula> RL) to enhance the visualization experience in two sub-tasks: color enhancement and composition optimization, which are formulated as a unified Markov Decision Process in the proposed A <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{3}$</tex-math></inline-formula> RL framework. In our approach, each pixel functions as an autonomous agent that determines optimal actions based on aesthetic guidance, engaging in online exploration through immediate pixel-wise and channel-wise feedback from the aesthetic environment. By leveraging a pretrained image aesthetic model, our method ensures that the A <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{3}$</tex-math></inline-formula> RL process aligns with human aesthetic preferences and adheres to subjective aesthetic principles. The framework integrates pixel-level retouching actions with image-level operations to achieve optimal image sequences through progressive iterations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method effectively recalibrates image aesthetics across multiple dimensions: low-level quality metrics (PSNR, SSIM), visual perception (LPIPS), and subjective visual experience (human survey). The results demonstrate high consistency with expert-retouched ground-truth images. Source code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/S-Ir-V/color_crop</uri>.

title: Development of a Black Carbon Aerosol Sensor Based on Multispectral Laser Scattering_supp1-3667931.pdf
authors: Hui Xiao
abstract: Black carbon (BC) aerosols, with their increasing emissions, have become a critical driver exacerbating global warming. However, the wide variety of atmospheric particulate matter poses substantial challenges to accurate BC detection. Traditional methods, such as offline sampling with black carbon analyzers and manual laboratory analysis, are plagued by high costs, time-consuming procedures, and operational complexity, limiting their applicability in real-time monitoring scenarios. To address these limitations, this study proposes an innovative BC detection method integrating microfluidic separation, sheath flow focusing, and multi-spectral laser scattering, aiming to achieve rapid and high-precision quantification of BC concentrations in samples. The microfluidic chip’s separation and focusing structure was rationally designed based on inertial impaction and bilateral sheath flow focusing principles, laying a foundation for effective BC isolation from complex matrices. A multi-spectral laser scattering detection platform was constructed to capture characteristic optical signals of BC, and a lightweight on-chip analysis model was further developed to realize qualitative identification and quantitative calculation of BC. Experimental results showed that the effective separation efficiency of the microfluidic chip in this system reached 95%, and the detection results were highly consistent with the calibration data from a black carbon mass spectrometer, with a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.9823 and a detection accuracy of 0.01 mg/m³. Compared to traditional BC detection methods, the sensor designed in this paper offers advantages such as low cost, high efficiency, and rapid response. Primarily targeting high-emission areas like factories and power plants, it holds broad potential for engineering applications and market promotion.

title: Toward a Machine Learning-Driven Digital Twin for Real-Time Hormone Biosensing in Personalized Infertility Care_supp1-3674340.pdf
authors: Anastasiia Gorelova

title: A Novel Low-Dimensional Sparse and Low-Rank Representation Method for Single-Cell RNA Sequencing Data Clustering
authors: Zhenduo Zhang, Junliang Shang, Lingyun Dai, Juan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: The advancement of single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) technology has enabled researchers to capture cellular heterogeneity at the individual cell level, driving progress in diverse fields such as developmental biology, immunology, and cancer research. Accurate cell clustering is a crucial step for researchers utilizing scRNA-seq data; however, inherent characteristics like high dimensionality and sparsity pose significant challenges to obtaining precise clustering results. To achieve accurate clustering, this paper proposes a novel approach that integrates dimensionality reduction, self-representation matrix construction, and the clustering process into an end-to-end model termed LDSLRR (Low-Dimensional Sparse and Low-Rank Representation). Specifically, the original gene expression matrix first undergoes dimensionality reduction via projection. Subsequently, low-rank representation combined with a sparsity constraint facilitates the learning of the self-representation matrix. Finally, the cluster assignment matrix is acquired using graph-regularized non-negative matrix factorization (NMF). These three modules are simultaneously optimized, enhancing the accuracy of the clustering results. Comparative experiments against multiple state-of-the-art clustering methods on various scRNA-seq datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed LDSLRR method.

title: MATCH: Multi-Agentic Evidence Grounding for Explainable Hate Video Detection_supp1-3672052.pdf
authors: Fan Zhou
abstract: The growing prevalence of hate videos promoting intolerance, bigotry, and discrimination presents significant psychosocial threats to both individuals and society. Current detection methods often rely on black-box models, which lack interpretability — a crucial factor for fostering more reliable content moderation and trustworthy AI. To bridge this gap, we propose MATCH, the first attempt to achieve interpretable hate video detection via multiple Large Multimodal Model (LMM) agent collaboration. Our method facilitates a novel diversely generate-then-verify paradigm, where LMM agents work in tandem to generate diverse clues and verify them to yield more faithful explanations. MATCH begins by proposing a new Dual-Perspective Proposing paradigm, where two LMM agents are regarded as Proposers to independently identify evidential clues from opposing angles – hate and non-hate. Leveraging these comprehensive clues, we introduce an innovative Spatiotemporal Evidence-Grounded Verification mechanism. In this mechanism, a third LMM agent acts as a Verifier, rigorously validating and reconciling the proposed clues against spatiotemporal evidences directly extracted from video content, yielding coherent and faithful explanations. Finally, these explanations are integrated with video features, enabling accurate identification of complex and ambiguous hateful content. Extensive experiments conducted on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that MATCH not only achieves state-of-the-art performance, but also provides reliable and trustworthy rationales for the predictions. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/MATCH-HVD.

title: 4DGS-Craft: Consistent and Interactive 4D Gaussian Splatting Editing
authors: Lei Liu, Can Wang, Zhenghao Chen, Zhenhua Liu, Dong Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Recent advances in 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) editing still face challenges with view, temporal, and non-editing region consistency, as well as with handling complex text instructions. To address these issues, we propose 4DGS-Craft, a consistent and interactive 4DGS editing framework. We first introduce a 4D-aware InstructPix2Pix model to ensure both view and temporal consistency. This model incorporates 4D VGGT geometry features extracted from the initial scene, enabling it to capture underlying 4D geometric structures during editing. We further enhance this model with a multi-view grid module that enforces consistency by iteratively refining multi-view input images while jointly optimizing the underlying 4D scene. Furthermore, we preserve the consistency of non-edited regions through a novel Gaussian selection mechanism, which identifies and optimizes only the Gaussians within the edited regions. Beyond consistency, facilitating user interaction is also crucial for effective 4DGS editing. Therefore, we design an LLM-based module for user intent understanding. This module employs a user instruction template to define atomic editing operations and leverages an LLM for reasoning. As a result, our framework can interpret user intent and decompose complex instructions into a logical sequence of atomic operations, enabling it to handle intricate user commands and further enhance editing performance. Compared to related works, our approach enables more consistent and controllable 4D scene editing.

title: Frame-wise Detection of Fake Bitrate VVC Videos Based on Hierarchical Feature Mapping in Coding Domain
authors: Qiang Xu, Hao Wang, Tanfeng Sun, Xinghao Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: A prevalent video manipulation technique involves upsampling the bitrate parameter without modifying the underlying video content, often implemented under the pretext of improving viewer appeal and monetization potential. Such deceptive practices, which substitute fake bitrate specifications for genuine ones, mislead audiences and platforms while directly infringing copyright protections, constituting a form of manipulation that forensic experts classify as fake bitrate video manipulation. Addressing the issue of detecting fake bitrate Versatile Video Coding (VVC) videos, an algorithm based on hierarchical feature mapping in the coding domain is proposed. We first analyze the Coding Unit (CU) partitioning and the deblocking filtering of fake bitrate VVC videos during multiple encoding processes. Then, CU partitioning and deblocking decision-mode information in the coding domain are extracted during the decoding process. By combining the positional information, the CU Difference feature map (FCD) and the Deblocking Filtering Difference feature map (FDD) are obtained through a hierarchical mapping of the encoding feature, which are further enhanced by calculating the eccentric covariance matrix. After that, the enhanced feature maps are fed into a Dual-branch Difference Perception (DDP) module to obtain frame-level detection results. By comparing with existing algorithms, the experimental results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves superior detection accuracy in different scenarios, validating its effectiveness and robustness.

title: DuFANet: A Multi-Source Remote Sensing Image Classification Method Integrating Frequency-Domain Attention and Cross-Modal Transformers
authors: Yong Mei, Xiangsuo Fan, Jinlong Fan
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Joint classification of hyperspectral imagery with LiDAR and SAR multimodal remote sensing data holds significant value for enhancing feature recognition accuracy. However, it still faces challenges such as severe spectral interference, modal imbalance, and insufficient feature fusion. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a multi-source fusion network-DuFANet. This approach introduces a frequency-domain attention module (FreqAttnBlock) in the hyperspectral branch to explicitly decompose and enhance spectral-spatial features. In the LiDAR/SAR branch, a depthwise convolutional self-attention module (DWConvSelfBlock) is designed to efficiently model modal local geometric features and global contextual information. A parallel Transformer encoder is employed after both hyperspectral and LiDAR/SAR branches to capture long-range dependencies and global semantics. The fusion stage introduces a dual-branch cross-modal fusion attention module (DuFuseAttention), achieving stable complementarity through dual-layer mechanisms at both the channel and spatial dimensions. Experiments on five datasets-Berlin, Muufl, Houston2013, Augsburg, and Trento, DuFANet achieves overall classification accuracies of 79.29%, 92.31%, 99.44%, 97.59%, and 99.75% respectively, significantly outperforming existing methods. Furthermore, the model demonstrates exceptional performance in complex urban scenes and under few-shot conditions, achieving a favorable balance between accuracy and computational cost. The source codes of DuFANet will be publicly available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/zwyxmy/DuFANet.git</uri>.

title: Impact of Inter-Modality Misalignment on Multispectral Object Detection
authors: Muhammad Usama, Imad Ali Shah, Roshan George, Enda Ward, Fiachra Collins, William O'Grady, Martin Glavin, Brian Deegan, Edward Jones
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: Object Detection significantly benefits from combining complementary spectral modalities; however, its performance suffers greatly from misregistration between sensor streams due to spatial misalignments, viewpoint differences between sensors, and inconsistent annotations, a common feature of multi-modal datasets used in object detection system development. In this work, we address the critical challenge of modality misregistration. We propose a comprehensive alignment framework based on object-aware local homography estimation, anchored on Long-Wave Infrared Radiation (LWIR) annotations, to generate a spatially aligned training dataset for improved model development. For our experiments, we utilise the Multispectral Object Detection (MOD) dataset, which includes RGB, Short-Wave Infrared Radiation (SWIR), Mid-Wave Infrared Radiation (MWIR), and Long-Wave Infrared Radiation (LWIR) imagery. The proposed alignment pipeline ensures a consistent field of view, resolution normalization, and accurate cross-modality annotation projection. To quantify the impact of alignment, we benchmark four state-of-the-art fusion architectures (DEYOLO, TarDAL, CMTFusion, and U2Fusion) on both the original and the aligned versions of the MOD dataset. Experimental results demonstrate significant improvements of 10 to 30 percentage points in mAP@0.5 after training on the aligned MOD data, with enhanced localization accuracy and reduced false detections. We further verified the generalizability of the proposed alignment framework on a different dataset (FLIR ADAS), observing 3.54 percentage points improvement in mAP@0.5 after training on the aligned RGB-LWIR image pairs. U2Fusion emerged as the architecture most tolerant to misregistration in initial experiments, and this architecture is used to conduct a further sensitivity analysis to evaluate model robustness against real-world misalignments, in a controlled way. The results of this sensitivity analysis could be useful for designers of practical systems, where small misalignment errors between image modalities commonly occur.

title: Analysis and Design of Compact Pin-Loaded Endfire Leaky-Wave Antenna with Stable Beam Pattern
authors: Junbing Duan, Zhuo-Ya Li, Lei Zhu, Ting Shi, Cheng Liao, Sheng Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: The beam patterns of endfire leaky-wave antennas (LWAs) are generally changed with frequencies from the split dual-beam to the narrow single-beam. This article presents and analyzes a pin-loaded leaky-wave antenna (LWA) with stable endfire beam pattern in the operation band, based on the negative group delay (NGD) effects introduced by the coupled resonators on the two sides of shorting pin loaded double-sided parallel strip line (DSPSL). An equivalent model of the proposed endfire LWA is firstly established to explore the optimal condition for the realization of abnormal phase constant analytically. The effects of shorting-pin as well as the coupled resonators are extracted numerically to accomplish the mapping between equivalent circuit values and physical sizes. Then, the intrinsic properties are analyzed detailly to explain the relation between the maximum bandwidth of anomalous phase constant and maximum attenuation constant. After that, the chosen strategy of anomalous propagation constants for the proposed endfire LWA are explored by considering the realization of stable radiation gains and efficiencies in the operation band. Finally, a designed prototype LWA is fabricated to verify the predicted endfire radiation performances, through good agreement between simulation and measurement.

title: A Comprehensive Water Absorption-Rebound Estimation Method for Uniaxial Compressive Strength of Red-Bed Soft Rock under Groundwater Influence
authors: Wentong Chang, Gang Wang, Yang Wang, Wei Yang, Yupei Wang

title: A Full-Load Range Soft-Switching Method for Full Bridge Converter Based on Small Magnetizing Inductance and Segmented Fixed-Frequency Control
authors: Baojian Ji, Wenjie Mei, Ziyang Zhang, Song Ding, Feng Hong
abstract: The rapid advancement of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), military applications, and industrial technologies has placed higher demands on power sources: further enhancement of power density, coupled with the realization of high-efficiency conversion over the entire load range. In this paper, a soft-switching method is proposed for a full-bridge converter based on a small magnetizing inductance. The method does not require any auxiliary circuits. The reduction in magnetizing inductance can decrease the size of magnetic components, which is conducive to the enhancement of power density. The reduction in transformer magnetizing inductance poses new challenges for achieving soft-switching over the entire load range. This paper further proposes a segmented fixed-frequency PWM strategy, along with a corresponding dynamic dead-time adjustment method, to achieve soft-switching across the entire load range. The proposed method remains PWM-based, retaining its simplicity and fast dynamic response, without increasing the size of magnetic components. The paper analyzes the operating modes of the proposed converter under phase-shift control. It derives the coupling relationships among soft-switching conditions, leakage and magnetizing inductance, switching frequency, and dead time. Parameter design guidelines are provided, and the impact of the segmented fixed-frequency PWM strategy on power density is also discussed. A prototype with a power rating of 1kW and a rated switching frequency of 500kHz has been developed. Experimental validation has confirmed the effectiveness of the theoretical analysis. The prototype achieves a power density of 8.3kW/L, a peak efficiency of 95.5%, and an efficiency of 91.1% at light loads.

title: PBH: A Periodic Binary Head with Two-Stage Training for Efficient Text Classification
authors: Yanbai Wang, Mengyu Wang, Yingqi Pei, Xueying Jiang, Lu Tan
abstract: Quantized neural networks significantly reduce storage requirements and computational complexity by lowering the numerical precision of weights and activations. Among these, binary neural networks (BNNs) represent an extreme form of quantization, using only 1-bit weights. However, due to the non-differentiability of the sign function during quantization and the resulting distortion in gradient approximation, directly applying binary weights in classification models often leads to unstable training and substantial performance degradation.To address these challenges, this paper introduces a Periodic Binary Head for text classification tasks. Specifically, we employ a periodic function to binarize weights while utilizing a Straight-Through Estimator (STE) for approximate gradient propagation, thereby mitigating quantization mismatch and vanishing gradient issues. In addition, a two-stage training strategy is adopted: first, warm-up training is performed on a full-precision classification head while freezing the Transformer backbone parameters to stabilize feature representations, which further enhances the trainability and final performance of the binarized model.Experimental results on multiple text classification datasets, including AGNews and DBpedia, demonstrate that the proposed method achieves classification accuracy comparable to, or slightly higher than, that of full-precision classification heads while incurring minimal parameter overhead. Further ablation experiments confirm that both the periodic function design and the two-stage training strategy play crucial roles in performance improvement. This work provides an effective approach for integrating lightweight binary classification heads into pre-trained language models.

title: Bridging Datasets and Hyperparameters: GCN-Based Link Prediction for Recommendation
authors: Liping Deng, MingQing Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Hyperparameter recommendation through meta-learning (HPR-MtL) has proven effective in a wide range of studies. At its core, HPR-MtL constructs a recommendation model using metadata extracted from historical learning tasks, such as dataset characteristics and the empirical performance of hyperparameter configurations. Existing approaches-typically based on k-nearest neighbors (KNN), linear regression, or collaborative filtering-focus primarily on modeling interactions between datasets and hyperparameters, i.e., performance observations. However, they often overlook important interactions within datasets or within hyperparameter configurations, such as similarity relationships, which are critical components embedded in the metadata. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel hyperparameter recommendation framework formulated as a link prediction problem on a bipartite graph. In our approach, each historical dataset and each hyperparameter configuration is represented as a node, and the links between them are weighted by the observed performance. When a new dataset becomes available, it is added as a node, and the model predicts its potential links to hyperparameter nodes. To enhance prediction accuracy, we introduce a graph convolutional network (GCN) that simultaneously captures both homogeneous interactions (within datasets and within hyperparameters) and heterogeneous interactions (between datasets and hyperparameters)-a capability not found in existing methods. We evaluated our method on two popular deep learning models, ResNet and Vision Transformer (ViT), across 105 real-world classification datasets. Extensive experiments using multiple evaluation metrics demonstrate the superior performance of our approach compared to state-of-the-art hyperparameter recommendation baselines.

title: A Distributed Communication-Noise-Resistant $\boldsymbol{k}$WTA Network With Its Applications
authors: Kewei Zhang, Erchao Li, Jiazheng Zhang, Long Jin
abstract: Owing to the ability to model competitive behavior, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-winners-take-all (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex-math></inline-formula>WTA) networks play a crucial role in swarm control. However, most existing <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex-math></inline-formula>WTA networks rely on centralized architectures and focus primarily on model noise, while largely neglecting the inevitable communication noise. To address these limitations, this article proposes a distributed communication-noise-resistant <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex-math></inline-formula>WTA (DC-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex-math></inline-formula>WTA) network and applies it to the multirobot task allocation. Due to the communication noise, the classical methods for deterministic convergence analysis based on LaSalle’s invariance principle are no longer applicable. Therefore, the stochastic stability theory and Itô’s formula are employed to analyze the convergence of the proposed DC-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex-math></inline-formula>WTA network under additive and multiplicative noise, respectively. Finally, the superiority and effectiveness of the proposed DC-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{k}$</tex-math></inline-formula>WTA network are demonstrated through simulations and physical experiments.

title: Prescribed-Time Control of Nonlinear Systems With Unknown Coefficients: An Event-Triggered Approach
authors: Zeqiang Li, Yujuan Wang, Jason J. R. Liu, Yongduan Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: This work considers the prescribed-time control problem of uncertain nonlinear systems with unknown control coefficients via an event-triggering strategy. In contrast to existing event-based prescribed-time works where the control coefficients are supposed to be known, in this work, not only the magnitude but also the sign of the control coefficient is allowed to be unknown. Based on a newly established Nussbaum-type lemma, the underlying problem is addressed by proposing an adaptive prescribed-time control scheme, which is characterized by the joint design of the triggering condition and actual controller. Moreover, the developed control scheme enables the execution time to be extended to infinity, distinguishing itself from those where the valid execution time can only be finite (ended at the prescribed time). It is proved that the closed-loop signals are globally bounded and system states converge to the origin within a prespecified time. Besides, the Zeno-free behavior is ensured. A robotic manipulator is exploited to demonstrate the validity of the theoretical findings.

title: RS-SSEDet: A Semantic Structure-Enhanced Self-Supervised Framework for Composite Target Detection in Remote Sensing
authors: Yi Liu, Zhongchao Guo, Chenhao Sun, Shiyin Liu, Tianze Su
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: High-resolution remote sensing imagery often contains complex composite targets, such as nuclear power plants and chemical factories, which consist of multiple discrete components arranged in variable layouts. These targets exhibit intricate structural patterns and ambiguous boundaries, making manual annotation costly and highly dependent on expert knowledge. Existing detection methods mainly extract visual features and model spatial relationships among components, using coarse grained category labels for supervision. However, they overlook fine-grained semantic structures such as attribute-orientation relations, limiting their semantic understanding. To tackle these issues, we propose RS-SSEDet, a Semantic Structure-Enhanced Self-Supervised framework designed to improve composite target detection without requiring manual annotations. We first construct a semantic structural instruction dataset and fine-tune a multimodal large model to generate structured descriptions of composite targets. Then, we enhance a vision-language detector to align visual features with semantic structures. Finally, we introduce a self-supervised iterative strategy that generates pseudo-labels to guide the detector in localizing regions referred to by semantic descriptions. Experiments on nuclear power plant and sewage treatment plant datasets demonstrate that RS SSEDet achieves 44.7% and 25.9% mAP respectively, outperforming all unsupervised methods by 12.3–13.4% over VLDet. Crucially, it matches supervised Faster R-CNN performance on nuclear facilities with 44.7% mAP compared to Faster R-CNN's 44.1% mAP, while exceeding supervised baselines on sewage treatment plants with 25.9% mAP compared to Faster R-CNN's 17.0% mAP, validating the effectiveness of our method.

title: FDN-VINS: Fault Detection Neural Network Aided Visual-Inertial SLAM
authors: Yilin Zhao, Ziyue Fan, Long Zhao
abstract: Multi-sensor fusion is widely adopted for resilient positioning, navigation, and timing (PNT) services. Visual-Inertial navigation system (VINS) is a critical solution for achieving pose estimation in GNSS-denied environments as one of the low-cost fusion approaches. However, vision-based methods depend on accurate depth reconstruction which is highly susceptible to environmental conditions. When the environment degrades, the system is prone to failure. It is worth noting that it is difficult to maintain highly accurate and robust localization because of the absence of an effective fault detection method in VINS. This paper proposes a fault detection scheme for VINS based on a data-driven method. It enables the detection of fault conditions of VINS by using IMU data as a reference while simultaneously inputting the state of VINS. Furthermore, a delayed marginalization strategy is employed to incorporate the temporally-sequenced fault status into the optimization thread, allowing the system to circumvent fault conditions. Comparative experiments based on extensive public datasets and self-collected data demonstrate that the proposed fault detection network can effectively identify fault conditions of VINS, significantly enhancing both the accuracy and robustness of VINS. It exhibits universal applicability across different platforms and scenarios. The code has been open-sourced for learning and usage at https://github.com/ColeZhao/FDN-VINS.

title: Criticality-Aware Gen-AI Inference via Dynamic Step Control in Resource-Efficient Vehicular Computing_supp1-3668982.pdf
authors: Jinmei Shu
abstract: Integrating Generative AI (Gen-AI) into vehicular computing can significantly enhance road safety and driving experience. The performance of Gen-AI inference in vehicular computing is highly sensitive to the number of inference steps, where even minor adjustments can disrupt the balance between delay and inference quality. This raises a critical question: how can inference steps be optimally determined for diverse tasks, given the inherent delay-quality trade-offs? Existing approaches fail to offer an optimal solution due to the lack of flexible inference services and efficient memory bandwidth allocation strategies. To address these challenges, we develop a <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">C</u>riticality-<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">a</u>ware <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">R</u>esource-efficient <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">In</u>ference (CARIN) framework, where inference steps are dynamically adjusted to balance delay and quality for multi-criticality tasks. Leveraging an accurate inference model, CARIN fully exploits in-vehicle resources to accelerate both parameter loading and task computing during inference. The joint optimization problem of step control, memory bandwidth allocation, and compute resource scheduling is formulated as a mixed-integer nonlinear programming (MINLP) problem, and solved by a novel learning-to-optimize (L2O) algorithm efficiently. Experimental results demonstrate that, for high-criticality tasks, the proposed approach achieves <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$28.4\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> latency reduction and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$36\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> quality improvement over baseline methods.

title: Dual Context-Conditioned Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation in Remote Sensing
authors: Sungjin Lim, Dong Un Kang, Yeongmok Cho, Se Young Chun
abstract: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) aims to assign pixel-level labels to unseen categories during training, offering a flexible and scalable solution for real-world applications. Although vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP have achieved remarkable success in natural images, the extension of OVSS to remote sensing remains challenging due to heterogeneous landscapes, large-scale variations, and small objects in complex backgrounds. To preserve spatial details for improving the segmentation performance on small objects in remote sensing images, existing methods typically adopt tile-based inference with sliding windows rather than downscaling entire scenes. However, processing each tile independently without considering surrounding contexts often leads to incorrect predictions due to the absence of global scene understanding and the discontinuities at tile borders. Here, we propose training-free dual context-conditioned OVSS specifically tailored for remote sensing. Our proposed dual context conditions consist of inter-region context integration and global context integration and are incorporated into the last attention layer of the CLIP image encoder via our frequency-aware gating mechanism, enabling joint refinement of attention weights and value representations to balance between fine details and scene-level structures. Specifically, the inter-region context integration employs vision foundation models (VFMs) to measure patch-level similarities between a target tile and its neighboring tiles, providing complementary local neighboring cues and the global context integration leverages a downsampled whole image to capture structural cues via VFMs, enabling the model to incorporate global information cues. Since the reliability of local and global cues differs across scenes, our frequency-aware gating mechanism adaptively modulates their relative importance based on the image’s frequency composition. Extensive experiments on 17 benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance for training-free OVSS in remote sensing, consistently outperforming prior arts on all benchmarks.

title: Quantum-Resistant Privacy and Auditing for Blockchain: A Lattice-Based Approach with Composable Security
authors: Chen Sun, Nurshazwani Muhamad Mahfuz, Yali Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Privacy-preserving blockchain systems in regulated financial environments must simultaneously satisfy transaction confidentiality and regulatory compliance requirements. Existing schemes predominantly rely on elliptic curve cryptography, rendering them vulnerable to Harvest Now, Decrypt Later (HNDL) attacks posed by quantum computing advances. This paper proposes L-HCPM (Lattice-based Hybrid Cryptographic Protocol Model), a privacy-preserving blockchain protocol based on lattice cryptography that achieves both post-quantum security and regulatory auditability. The core contribution is the LT-Share (Lattice Trapdoor Shared Randomness) mechanism, which leverages GPV08/MP12 lattice trapdoor sampling to generate shared randomness between users and regulators, enabling regulators to independently decrypt transactions without user cooperation. To address the non-reproducibility of Peikert randomized sampling under the UTXO model, we introduce an off-chain caching architecture to ensure sampling consistency. We further extend the Universal Composability (UC) framework by introducing a hybrid adversary model, formally proving the secure composability of quantum-secure and classical-secure components. Experimental evaluation based on a complete prototype system demonstrates that post-quantum cryptographic overhead accounts for only 0.17% of end-to-end latency, with Gas consumption ranging from 337 K to 686 K, and regulatory decryption achieving 100% success rate, validating the practical feasibility of post-quantum security upgrades.

title: A Comprehensive Study on the Stability and Implementation of the Conformal LCDI-FDTD Method
authors: Lingpu Zhang, Chenshu Liu, Kaihang Fan, Juan Chen, Chunhui Mou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Compared with the conventional finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) method, the conformal FDTD (C-FDTD) method incorporates additional geometric parameters such as edge lengths and surface areas, which significantly complicates its three-dimensional (3D) stability analysis. The stability of current unconditional conformal algorithms has not been fully analyzed theoretically, with many validations limited to parameter-based numerical experiments. This paper presents a systematic investigation into the stability of the conformal leapfrog complying divergence implicit FDTD (C-LCDI-FDTD) method. By integrating von Neumann analysis with the Jury criterion, a comprehensive theoretical proof of the method’s unconditional stability in 3D is established. Then, the detailed implementation procedure is elaborated. Finally, the stability is validated through three representative numerical examples, demonstrating excellent agreement with the theoretical stability analysis and further confirming the robustness of the C-LCDI-FDTD method.

title: Compact High-Gain Dual-Circularly Polarized Antenna Based on High-Order Mode Microstrip Patch
authors: Dieu Nguyen-Khanh, Dat Nguyen-Tien, Nguyen Tran, Thai Nguyen-Dinh, Niamat Hussain, Hung Tran-Huy
abstract: This paper presents a method to design a high-gain dual circularly polarized (CP) antenna with compact size based on high-order mode patch structure. For high-gain radiation, a square patch is excited to operate in high-order TM03 mode. To suppress grating lobes and reduce the overall size, four open slots are etched on the edges of the patch. For dual-CP realization, a 90 hybrid coupler is employed as the feeding network. By changing the feeding port, either right-hand CP (RHCP) or left-hand CP (LHCP) with high isolation can be produced. An antenna prototype with overall dimensions of 1.49 1.49 0.04 at 5.6 GHz is fabricated and measured for validation. The measurements demonstrate that the proposed design achieves good dual-CP performance around 5.6 GHz with a peak realized gain of approximately 12 dBi. In comparison with the high-gain dual-CP antennas using array or Fabry-Perot structures, the proposed approach has the advantage of achieving high gain with a compact and simple configuration.

title: Intelligent Learning Methods for Battery State of Charge Estimation for Electric Vehicles: a Review
authors: Ranvijay Parmar, D. K. Sakravdia
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: The increasing dependence on Li-ion batteries has amplified the importance of accurately estimating the State of Charge (SoC). A dependable SoC prediction enables BMS to maintain operational safety and improve overall efficiency. Conventional estimation methods, such as OCV and Coulomb counting, remain popular for their simplicity and low computational requirements, but often struggle to deliver reliable results under dynamic or fluctuating load conditions. In recent years, intelligent learning algorithms - including ML, DL, and hybrid models - have achieved notable improvements in accuracy and adaptability. Among them, deep architectures such as LSTM and CNN-LSTM have demonstrated under-1% RMSE in benchmark analyses. Despite these advances, such models continue to face challenges related to data dependency, computational demand, and poor generalization across battery chemistries and working situations. This study offers a consolidated review of SoC estimation methodologies, encompassing traditional, ML, and DL techniques, and presents structured comparison tables along with their integration into modern BMS frameworks. The paper further outlines existing research gaps involving data efficiency, realtime applicability, uncertainty quantification, and benchmarking standards. Through comparative evaluation and critical insight, the work aims to direct future research toward lightweight, generalizable, and uncertainty-aware SoC estimation strategies capable of supporting large-scale deployment in electric mobility and energy storage applications.

title: Spike-Based Time-Domain ECG Wave Delineation for Low-Power VLSI Implementation
authors: Priya K, Binsu J Kailath
publish: 2026 39th International Conference on VLSI Design &amp; 25th International Conference on Embedded Systems (VLSID)
abstract: Accurate delineation of ECG waves is critical for cardiac diagnosis, particularly under baseline wander, high-frequency noise, and pathological conditions. This paper presents a fully digital, adaptive ECG delineation system operating in the time domain using spike outputs from adaptive exponential integrate-and-fire (AdEx) neuron encoding. Four complementary slope detection methods—ISI-based differences, spike-rate estimation, ISI summation, and ISI grouping—are integrated using temporal consensus logic to capture sharp R-peaks, gradual P/T waves, and wide morphologies. Validation on QT and MIT-BIH arrhythmia databases demonstrates low mean error <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(<10 ~\text{ms}$</tex> for QRS, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<12 ~\text{ms}$</tex> for T) across fiducial points. A hardware implementation in SCL 180 nm CMOS achieves 0.252 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> area and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$28.8 \mu W$</tex> power at 50 kHz, confirming suitability for VLSI and wearable biomedical applications.

title: Exploring the Resilience Nature of AlN Cap Layer in AlGaN/GaN HEMT Under Thermal Conditions
authors: Ellapu Bhanu Prakash, Ashok Ray, Sushanta Bordoloi
publish: 2026 39th International Conference on VLSI Design &amp; 25th International Conference on Embedded Systems (VLSID)
abstract: This study explores the effectiveness of an aluminum nitride (AlN) cap layer that can be suppress self-heating effects (SHE) and improve the performance of AlGaN/GaN high electron mobility transistors (HEMTs). Using calibrated Sentaurus TCAD simulations, three device configurations are compared such as without a cap, with a GaN cap, and with an AlN cap. Results demonstrated that the hotspot lattice temperature decreases from 321 K (no cap) to 314 K (GaN cap) and further to 309 K (AlN cap) owing to AlN's superior thermal conductivity. The AlN cap also improves electron confinement which raises the 2DEG density to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.32 \times 10^{13} ~\text{cm}^{-2}$</tex> compared to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.11 \times 10^{13} ~\text{cm}^{-2}$</tex> in the conventional device. Performance improvements include a 110 % increase in drain current (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{I}_{d s}$</tex>), 73% higher transconductance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$g_{m}$</tex>, and a 22 % reduction in peak electric field (EFP) at the gate edge. These results emphasize that the AlN cap layer is an effective technique for mitigating SHE and enhancing reliability in high-power, high-frequency GaN-based electronics.

title: Hybrid Time Series and Sentiment Analysis for Stock Forecasting on Distributed Big Data Platforms
authors: Aswathnarayan Muthukrishnan Kirubakaran, Sumit Saha, Abhirup Mazumder, Ravi Kiran Kodali, Lokesh Butra, Dinesh Eswararaj
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Forecasting stock returns from prices alone often fails to capture event–driven moves that first surface in public discourse. We present a scalable hybrid framework that fuses time–series modeling with social–media sentiment and is designed for both batch and streaming operations. The system, implemented in Scala on Apache Spark, ingests price and tweet streams, constructs windowed sentiment aggregates, and trains two complementary forecasters: (i) a streaming friendly ARIMA with a post hoc sentiment adjustment and (ii) an ARIMAX that treats sentiment features as exogenous regressors. Evaluation on S&P 500 subsets uses expanding window backtests with strict causal joins to avoid leakage, and reports directional accuracy, RMSE, MAE, and MASE with paired significance tests. Sentiment augmented models improve directional accuracy by approximately 7–10% points over ARIMA and reduce RMSE by 12–18% on average, with the largest gains around earnings and other high attention events. A fee and slippage aware simulation indicates higher risk adjusted returns than a price only baseline. The end-to–end streaming path achieves 2–5 s latency and writes versioned forecasts, features, and metrics to Elasticsearch for auditable dashboards in Kibana. We release configuration details (tickers, spans, seeds and grids) to support reproducibility and discuss limitations from sentiment noise, regime shifts, and API constraints.

title: Control Strategy for Two-Phase Operation of Two-Terminal Fractional Frequency Transmission System
authors: Min Zhou, Yonghuan Liu, Zhida Kong, Yi Chen, Yongwen Chen, Yang Huang, Qingxin Wang, Tiantian He
publish: 2025 4th Asia Power and Electrical Technology Conference (APET)
abstract: This paper proposes a control strategy for twophase operation on the low-frequency(LF) side of two-terminal fractional frequency transmission system (TTFFTS). The control strategy is designed using a double closed-loop control based on dual dq transformation and voltage equalization control between bridge arms, supplemented by the instantaneous symmetrical component decomposition method for asymmetric phase quantities. The voltage and power characteristics of the LF side under different two-phase operation modes are analyzed, confirming the feasibility of a two-phase operation mode with a 90° phase difference. The mathematical model of modular multilevel matrix converter (M3C) under two-phase operation is derived, and a corresponding control strategy is designed. Finally, a twophase operation model is constructed in PSCAD/EMTDC, and the results demonstrate that the output power during two-phase operation stabilizes at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sqrt{2} / 3$</tex> times the rated power of normal operation, verifying the feasibility of the two-phase operation mode.

title: IoT-Driven Retail: A Smart Grocery Shopping Experience Revolution
authors: Hussain Al-Aqrabi, Nedaa Al Barghuthi, Hend Al Ali, Khaloud Al Kaabi, Amna Al Dahmani
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: The retail sector is poised for substantial transformation in the context of rapid technological advancements. This research presents the Smart Grocery Shopping System, an advanced cloud-based solution designed to optimize grocery shopping experiences at educational institutes. This innovative system effectively addresses the common challenges associated with traditional grocery shopping methods by harnessing state-of-the-art IoT and cloud computing technologies. The Smart Grocery Shopping System has the potential to revolutionize campus grocery shopping experiences and establish an efficient model for deployment in other educational and retail environments. As such, this initiative can potentially reshape retail technology strategies across the broader retail landscape. Serving as a pioneering model, the Smart Grocery Shopping System provides valuable insights into the future of retail technology strategies in diverse educational and retail contexts. Consequently, this initiative aims to elevate grocery shopping experiences within HCT campuses and drive innovation and transformation throughout the broader retail landscape. This research paper explores the innovative features and the potential impact of this trailblazing system, offering a glimpse into the exciting possibilities in the ever-evolving world of retail technology. Through its innovative approach and potential for widespread adoption, the Smart Grocery Shopping System promises to play a pivotal role in shaping the future of retail experiences in higher education and beyond. smart campus, smart grocery, retail, IoT, Cloud.

title: Dynamic Cloud Scene Image Simulation Based on a Multi-Scale Superposition Method with Temporal Smoothing
authors: Jiaxin Jiang, Zhichao Xu, Shikai Jiang, Jinnan Gong, Shuang Wu, Xiyang Zhi
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer Science, and Information Processing (AICSIP)
abstract: Addressing the challenge of real-time simulation for large-scale scenarios, which is often hindered by the high computational complexity and memory consumption of traditional dynamic cloud simulation methods, this paper introduces an efficient dynamic cloud simulation approach that integrates Multi-Scale Superposition (MSS) and Inter-Frame Interpolation (IFI). Initially, the method employs the MSS algorithm to generate discrete interval frame cloud images along the time axis, thereby significantly reducing the computational load compared to conventional methods. Subsequently, IFI technology is utilized to complete continuous frames from the interval frames, producing a dynamic cloud sequence. This resolves the issue of low efficiency in modeling continuous processes within traditional simulations. Experimental results demonstrate that this method achieves a more than tenfold increase in computational efficiency compared to conventional methods across large-scale scenarios ranging from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10 \mathrm{k} \times 10 \mathrm{k}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$100 \mathrm{k} \times 100 \mathrm{k}$</tex> pixels. The advantage of this method becomes more pronounced with increasing image dimensions, providing an effective solution for large-scale dynamic cloud simulation.

title: Black-Box Model Transfer for Bacterial-Cellulose-Based Sensors
authors: Luca Patanè, Francesca Sapuppo, Maria Gabriella Xibilia
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Green Technologies, Renewable Energy and Ecological Sustainability (MetroGREENST)
abstract: This work explores the use of black-box modeling techniques for characterizing Bacterial Cellulose (BC)-based electro-mechanical sensors functionalized with different ionic liquids. By leveraging nonlinear models, specifically Nonlinear AutoRegressive with eXogenous inputs (NARX) and Nonlinear Finite Impulse Response (NFIR), the dynamic behavior of BC-based sensors is identified from experimental data. Two ionic liquids, EMIM BF4 and EMIM TMFS, are compared to assess the impact of material composition on model accuracy. The study addresses the challenge of model generalization by applying transfer learning strategies. Both direct model reuse and fine-tuning are evaluated, showing that fine-tuning, even on limited data, significantly improves prediction performance. Results indicate that transfer learning can effectively reduce the experimental and computational burden associated with sensor modeling, supporting scalable and adaptive deployment of sustainable sensing technologies.

title: From Dynamic Pathways to Size Control: Tunable Formation of DNA Condensates via Microfluidics
authors: Zhitai Huang, Tomoya Maruyama, Masahiro Takinoue, Hiroaki Suzuki
publish: 2025 IEEE 18th International Conference on Nano/Molecular Medicine &amp; Engineering (NANOMED)

title: Federated Multi-Agent Fuzzy Q-Learning Framework for Intelligent Charging Schedule in Wireless Rechargeable Sensor Networks
authors: Israa Hasan, Omar Banimelhem
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Communication, Networking and Services (ICCNS)

title: A Polynomial Time Routing Algorithm for Meda Biochips Based on Dijkstra's Algorithm
authors: Issei Nakamura, Shigeru Yamashita, Hiroyuki Tomiyama, Ankur Guputa
publish: 2026 39th International Conference on VLSI Design &amp; 25th International Conference on Embedded Systems (VLSID)

title: An Enhanced CT-Based Lung Cancer Classification Framework Using a Hybrid EfficientNetV2, CBAM, and Transformer Architecture
authors: Aanchal Vij, Kuldeep Singh Kaswan, Anand Nayyar

title: Rail-SDet: Identification of Rail Head Surface Defects Using Gaussian Adaptive Thresholding (GAT) Based Segmentation Approach
authors: Laxman Singh, Bhuvnesh Malik, Nagendra Nath Dubey, Surya Prakash Sharma, Priya Singh, Gaurav Kumar
