title: A Policy Driven Geo Adaptive Framework for Data Privacy Residency and Cross Border Controls in CRM Platforms
authors: Karthik Bodducherla, Sitaram Srivatsavai, Vijaya Sai Munduru
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Today's CRM is devoid of these permits and in a fractious, fragmented world, it's going to be very difficult to manage data privacy, residency and cross border controls." This paper presents a new technical architecture, the "Geo-Adaptive Privacy Control Framework" (GAP-CF), to dynamically enforce contradictory regulatory policies across the boundaries of one centralized CRM database. This paper provides a quantitative, simulation-oriented assessment of the framework. A synthetic dataset was generated consisting of 446 unique customer instances, corresponding to profiles from three regulatory zones. It was a simulation developed in Python and the Pandas library was used to wrangle data with 10,000 simulated receptions of access requests. Performance indicators like percentage compliance, request response time and processing overhead were quantified. The results, which are visualized with Matplotlib and Seaborn show that GAP-CF is able to comply within 99.8% of the perfect compliance levels at a minimal over-provisioning ratio and predictable impact on the system latency. The model provides a workable answer for multinational companies wanting to reconcile data drive marketing with restrictive global compliance.

title: Ship Classification Based on Multi-Channel Pointnet with LiDAR Ring ID and Reflected Light Intensity
authors: Hao Jiang, Jianying Zheng, Yong Luo, Yanyun Tao, Xiang Wang, Qian Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Micro/Nano Sensors for AI, Healthcare, and Robotics (NSENS)
abstract: Ships represent a fundamental element of water transport traffic scenarios and are the primary focus in waterway traffic monitoring. Shipping transportation, as a predominant mode within the realm of transportation, has witnessed rapid development in recent times. However, it is now in urgent need of highquality progress as well. The automated classification of inland river ships constitutes the fundamental basis for the digitalization and intellectualization of shipping transportation, and it holds crucial importance for facilitating the high-quality development of the shipping industry. The predominant approach for inland ship classification relies on visual sensors and Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), which are limited in providing detailed 3D geometric information and are affected by varying weather and lighting conditions. To address this issue, we propose a LiDAR-based ship classification method for inland waterways. This method involves background filtering and target detection on the original point cloud, generating a point cloud dataset of inland ships, and utilizing PointNet to learn and classify the features of the ship point cloud model. Moreover, we propose for the first time to fuse LiDAR ring ID features, reflected light intensity features and geometric features, and to use the fused point cloud data for feature extraction and classification using an improved model. This fused point cloud data will be utilized for feature extraction and classification through an enhanced model. Our method achieves an accuracy of 97.83%, surpassing the geometric information-only method by 1.03%. This result effectively demonstrates the method's efficacy in feature extraction and classification of LiDAR point cloud ships.

title: Improving the Accuracy of Polygenic Prediction for Precision Medicine: The Stacked Clumping and Thresholding Method
authors: Krishna Sai Penumarthi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Communication, Networking and Services (ICCNS)
abstract: Polygenic scores hold promise for advancing precision medicine. This research introduces Stacked Clumping and Thresholding (SCT), a novel method designed to enhance the accuracy of polygenic prediction. SCT builds upon the C+T framework by optimizing key hyper-parameters and employing a stacking strategy to combine multiple <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{T}$</tex> scores. The efficacy of SCT is demonstrated through simulations and applications to UK Biobank data, where it consistently outperforms standard <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{C} \boldsymbol{+} \mathbf{T}$</tex> in predicting disease risk.

title: Deep Learning-Based Ultrasound Image Classification for Early Diagnosis of Neonatal Biliary Atresia
authors: Kuo Chin Cheng, Fang Rong Hsu, Sheng Yang Huan, Chia-Man Chou, Wei-Cheng Yeh
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer Science, and Information Processing (AICSIP)
abstract: Biliary atresia (BA) is a life-threatening hepatic disorder in newborns that requires prompt diagnosis and surgical intervention to improve long-term outcomes. Although ultrasound imaging is commonly used for BA screening due to its non-invasiveness, diagnostic accuracy is often limited by operator experience and image quality. In this study, we present a deep learning-based framework that enhances diagnostic performance by combining advanced image preprocessing techniques-such as masking, contrast enhancement, and speckle noise reduction-with state-of-theart neural network models. Using a dataset of ultrasound images from clinically verified neonatal cases, we evaluated multiple convolutional and transformer-based architectures. Performance was assessed using classification accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, specificity, and inference latency, based on 5-fold cross-validation stratified at the patient level. Among the tested models, Swin Transformer achieved the highest classification accuracy (96.77%) and F1 score (97.85%), demonstrating its suitability for clinical integration. Our findings confirm the potential of deep learning models to assist in the early, image-based diagnosis of BA with high efficiency and reliability.

title: Analyzing the Effect of Center of Mass Shift on the Dynamics of a Mobile Robot
authors: Pushpendra Kumar, Jyoti Joshi, Mohit Makkar, Inderjeet Singh, Jitendra Yadav
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: Mobile robots are extensively used in the area of field robotics. Specifically, wheeled mobile robots are widely applied in many non-factory applications like in agriculture, container ports, defense, etc. In the designing phase of a mobile robot, creating a precise mathematical representation is crucial to capture the dynamic characteristics of the robot, which should be accurate enough to realize the dynamic behavior for its analysis in various conditions. This paper develops a dynamic behavioral model of a wheeled mobile robot using the bond graph modeling approach, which is an energy-based graphical tool for dynamic modeling of multi-domain systems. Further, the developed model is exploited for simulation studies to verify the model, and to analyze the dynamic variations caused by disturbances in the robot's center of mass location.

title: The Genome as a Quantum Error-Correcting Code
authors: Sultan Alam, Anish Pawar, Irfan Alam
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: DNA replication upholds genomic integrity with remarkable precision, rectifying inaccuracies that emerge from quantum-level phenomena. This paper introduces a novel framework that models this biological process using the formalism of quantum error correction .We propose that genetic information encoded in DNA base pairs can be represented as the codespace of a Biological Stabilizer Code , where cellular proofreading and mismatch repair mechanisms function as an active error correction protocol.We provide comprehensive proofs that illustrate the code’s capacity to preferentially rectify the most likely types of mutations, thereby guaranteeing elevated logical fidelity. We establish a biological equivalent of the quantum threshold theorem, demonstrating that high-fidelity replication can be achieved if the inherent mutation rate falls below a critical threshold. The performance of the BSC is corroborated through simulations conducted in Qiskit, which reveal its superior efficacy compared to symmetric codes under biologically plausible noise conditions. This research establishes a quantitative relationship between the principles of fault-tolerant quantum computation and the mechanisms that uphold the stability of life’s genetic code.

title: Simulation Based Optimization of Unit Load Multi Aisle Automated Storage and Retrieval System Design
authors: Yousef Adel, Atef Afifi, Lamia Shihata
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems play a crucial role in modern warehousing, yet most existing analytical models rely on simplified motion profile. This work developed an integrated simulation-based optimization framework for an aisle-changing crane-based system using transfer car mechanism under non-zero acceleration and deceleration. A FlexSim model is integrated with an energy calculation module adapted from recently developed analytical models to use energy consumption and cycle time as response variables to investigate five design variables: horizontal speed, vertical speed, acceleration/deceleration, number of aisles, and rack shape factor using Response Surface Methodology (RSM). The results show that horizontal speed and acceleration affect cycle time the most, while vertical speed and shape factor have the strongest impact on energy consumption. Increasing the number of aisles significantly increases both responses due to added inter-aisle movements. Nonlinear and interaction effects highlight the necessity of integrating design geometry and equipment settings. The response analysis shows that minimizing cycle time and energy consumption require conflicting parameter regions, showing the importance of a multi-objective design approach.

title: The Role of Smart Contracts and Blockchain in Developing Financial Trust
authors: Pranabananda Rath, Manupriya Gaur, Deeksha Agrawal, Ankit Garg, Neeta Sahu, Mohit Tiwari
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: This paper does an in-depth analysis of how blockchain technology and smart contracts instill public confidence in the banking industry. For a very long time now, trust in monetary transactions has been cemented through honest businesses and a regulated intermediary. As it would later turn out, blockchain technology provides an un-hackable, transparent, and decentralized basis upon which trust is built. In this bid to cut the involvement of third parties, this study analyzes to what extent blockchain technology and smart contracts facilitate a shift in trust to programmable contracts. Basically, this essay goes into detail about how blockchain technology and smart contracts can be applied with a view to reconsidering the very concept of trust. This study adopted a mixed-method approach in order to determine useful use cases and evaluate their contribution toward data security, transaction speed, and making sure of confidence. Case studies that are included in this study include but are not limited to JP Morgan's Quorum blockchain technology. According to many studies, blockchain technology cuts transaction costs by enhancing security and transparency, hence encouraging innovation in new financial services and products. This page contributes to the growing debate on decentralized technology through the provision of relevant insight into the legislators, regulators, and financial institutions leading this technological revolution.

title: Design of Resonance Tracking Control Method for Wireless Power Transfer Systems Using Asymmetric Square Waves
authors: Chengyi Mao, Jiwei Huang
publish: 2025 4th Asia Power and Electrical Technology Conference (APET)
abstract: The performance of wireless power transfer (WPT) systems can be significantly degraded by detuning. This paper proposes a control method that utilizes asymmetric square waves to achieve resonance tracking to mitigate this issue. Asymmetric square wave signals possess rich harmonic components, while WPT systems inherently exhibit bandpass frequency selectivity. The combination of these two characteristics generates the maximum harmonic current component near the resonant frequency. Identifying the peak frequency point in the current spectrum allows the input signal frequency to be adjusted accordingly to achieve resonance tracking. Compared to traditional methods, this approach provides simpler computation, faster response, and lower circuit design complexity. The experimental results validate the feasibility of the proposed method.

title: A PSO-Based STATCOM Parameter Optimization Strategy for Mitigating Commutation Failures in a SLCC-HVDC System
authors: Lingjie Kong, Ao Zhao, Ruisheng Diao, Haifeng Li, Zhiwei Wang
publish: 2025 4th Asia Power and Electrical Technology Conference (APET)
abstract: Conventional line-commutated converter-based high-voltage direct current (LCC-HVDC) systems are prone to commutation failures, which pose significant risks to power system security and stability. The self-adaptive STATCOM and line commutation converter (SLCC) technology mitigates this issue by connecting a static synchronous compensator in parallel at the valve side to provide fast reactive power support. This paper proposes a particle swarm optimization (PSO)-based method for STATCOM parameter optimization to enhance commutation failure immunity. The operation principle and mathematical model of the SLCC are first established. A commutation failure evaluation index is then formulated using multi-scenario time-domain simulations, and the PSO algorithm is adopted to achieve global optimization of STATCOM control parameters. Finally, simulations based on the Jiangsu provincial AC/DC hybrid grid model are conducted. The results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly reduces the probability of commutation failures and improves the operational reliability of AC/DC systems.

title: Numerical Design of a Hybrid SPR Sensor for CO Detection Using Porous Silicon in Kretschmann Configuration
authors: Yassine Bouazzi, Mohamed Saleh Esmail, Sayed Elshahat, Foued Selmi, Mounir Kanzari
publish: 2025 Global Conference on Sustainable Energy and Net-Zero Emissions (SENZE)
abstract: In this study, we propose a hybrid surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensor for detecting carbon monoxide (CO) under the Kretschmann configuration. The proposed structure comprises a BK7 prism, a TiO2 layer, a copper film, three graphene layers, and a porous silicon layer with 80% porosity. Reflectance spectra at 633 nm were simulated using the transfer matrix method. The effective PSi index was determined using the Bruggeman model. The results show a resonance angle shift of 1.3° for an index change of 0.005 RIU. The sensor achieves an angular sensitivity of 130°/RIU, a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 1.7°, a detection accuracy of 0.58<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">°−1</sup>, and a figure of merit of 76.47 RIU<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>. These performances confirm its potential for real time, label-free, and high-sensitivity CO detection.

title: Parametric Analysis of Infill Geometry and Density for Thermally Efficient 3D-Printed Concrete
authors: Akram Joda, Abraham Mansouri, Ahmed Abdullahi, Elgaali Elgaali, Bahaa Mourad
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: D-printed concrete (3DPC) is an innovative construction method that offers great design flexibility, material and labor efficiency, compared to conventional construction techniques. Enhancing the insulation of the 3DPC walls is essential for energy-efficient buildings. This study aims to investigate the combined effect of infill pattern (shape) and infill count (number of internal repeating units across the wall width) on the thermal performance of 3DPC walls. A parametric analysis is conducted to study the effect of using four different infill shapes and varying infill numbers on the heat flux through 3DPC walls. The results reveal that both infill geometry and repetition count significantly affect thermal transmittance, with optimized designs reducing heat loss effectively.

title: Agentic AI for Autonomous Defense in Software Supply Chain Security: Beyond Provenance to Vulnerability Mitigation
authors: Toqeer Ali Syed, Mohammad Riyaz Belgaum, Salman Jan, Asadullah Abdullah Khan, Saad Said Alqahtani
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Software supply chain attacks increasingly target trusted development and delivery processes, making conventional post-build integrity mechanisms insufficient. Existing frameworks like SLSA, SBOM, and in-toto mainly provide provenance and traceability but cannot actively identify or remove vulnerabilities during production. This paper presents an agentic AI approach for autonomous software supply chain security, combining large language model (LLM) reasoning, reinforcement learning (RL), and multi-agent coordination. The system employs specialized security agents that interact with CI/CD environments, collaborate intelligently to identify and mitigate threats, and document actions for integrity and auditing. RL enables adaptive mitigation strategies balancing security effectiveness and operational overhead, while LLMs provide semantic vulnerability analysis and explainable decisions. The framework is evaluated on simulated pipelines and actual CI/CD integrations, including injection attacks, insecure deserialization, access control violations, and configuration errors. Results show improved detection accuracy, shorter mitigation latency, and reasonable build-time overhead compared to rule-based, provenance-only, and RL-only baselines, demonstrating that agentic AI can enable proactive, self-defending software supply chains.

title: A Hybrid Chaotic GWO–PSO Algorithm for Improved MPPT in Photovoltaic Arrays
authors: Salem Saidi, Wrida Khriji, Emna Horchani, Abdulaziz J. Alateeq, Rabeh Abbassi
publish: 2025 Global Conference on Sustainable Energy and Net-Zero Emissions (SENZE)
abstract: With the acceleration of the global energy transition to net-zero emissions, the penetration of solar photovoltaic (PV) systems is growing worldwide. For PV applications, the efficiency of the integrated DC-DC converter is paramount to optimize the energy yield. It requires an efficient Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) strategy to extract the maximum energy from PV panels for optimal energy conversion. In an autonomous PV system, the MPPT control ensures optimal performance without grid support, maintains system stability, minimizes power losses, and responds rapidly to fluctuations in solar irradiance. Moreover, an advanced MPPT strategy increases energy harvest and ensures high power quality with minimal harmonic distortion. In this context, a hybrid MPPT method using a chaotic approach for autonomous PV systems with a DC-DC boost converter is proposed, combining particle swarm optimization (PSO) and the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO). The method is validated through MATLAB/Simulink 2022b simulations for a 1 kW PV array operating independently with a boost converter. The simulation results demonstrate that the chaotic GWO-PSO (CGWOPSO) algorithm reduces overshoot, shortens rise and settling times, and improves the dynamic response. Comparisons with standalone PSO and GWO algorithms show that the proposed chaotic algorithm ensures faster, more stable, and more efficient energy extraction for autonomous DC-DC converterbased PV systems.

title: An Energy Efficient Pulse Accumulator Circuit (PAC) Based Multi-Bit Time Domain Compute in-Memory Architecture
authors: Subhradip Chakraborty, Dinesh Kushwaha
publish: 2026 39th International Conference on VLSI Design &amp; 25th International Conference on Embedded Systems (VLSID)
abstract: In this paper, we present an area and energyefficient multi-bit time domain compute in memory (TDCIM) architecture for AI inference applications. A novel 8T time domain (TD) based bit-cell is proposed to perform the multiplication operation, which is used to generate the necessary delay. To convert the time domain signal into a digital form, we propose a pulse accumulator circuit (PAC) and a single reference line 4-bit time to digital converter (TDC). The designed architecture has been simulated using commercial 45 nm CMOS technology, resulting in the development of a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$64 \times 64$</tex> TDCIM macro. This work demonstrates normalized energy efficiency and throughput of 1.46 POPS/W and 1.59 TOPS at VDD = 0.8 V, which is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.7 \times$</tex> higher compared to the state-of-the-art. Furthermore, the system attained a test accuracy of 89.35% and 97.58% when tested on the CIFAR-10 and MNIST datasets with 4-bit precision in inputs and weights.

title: Online Evaluation for Primary Frequency Regulation Capability of Supercritical Units Based Dynamic Modeling
authors: Dejun Shao, Jun Liu, Mengxuan Shi, Deyu Li, Lei Chen, Fei Xu, Qun Chen, Ling Hao, Yajun Wu, Xiaojie Pan
publish: 2025 4th Asia Power and Electrical Technology Conference (APET)
abstract: This paper proposes a dynamic-simulation-based method for online evaluation of the primary frequency regulation (PFR) capability of supercritical coal-fired units under high renewable-energy penetration. An integrated dynamic model covering the boiler-turbine system, DEH, and CCS is developed, explicitly incorporating key physical characteristics and control constraints such as valve flow properties, boiler heat-transfer inertia, fuel-water coupling, and rate limits. Based on this model, a set of grid-oriented PFR capability indicators is established to enable real-time capability assessment and early warning. Four scenarios with different frequency deviations are simulated, and the results show that the model accurately reproduces the unit's dynamic response, reveals the asymmetry between up- and down-regulation, and identifies how PFR contribution and valve-power sensitivity vary with disturbance magnitude. The proposed method provides an effective tool for monitoring PFR capability, identifying constraint mechanisms, and supporting secure operation of coal-fired units in renewable-rich systems.

title: Screen-Printed Electrochemical Sensor Platform for Nitrate and Nitrite Detection in Water
authors: Roberta Farina, Giuseppe E. Capuano, Serena C.R. Reina, Domenico Corso, Giuseppe A. Screpis, Silvia Scalese, Maria Anna Coniglio, Giuseppe D'Arrigo, Sebania Libertino
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Green Technologies, Renewable Energy and Ecological Sustainability (MetroGREENST)
abstract: Monitoring the concentration of nitrate (NO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−</sup>) and nitrite (NO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−</sup>) ions in water is essential due to their considerable impact on both environmental and human health. In this work, screen-printing technology was employed to fabricate electrochemical sensors with high reproducibility and reliable analytical performance. For nitrate detection, copper (Cu) was chosen as the electroactive material owing to its high electrical conductivity (5.8×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</sup>S/m), which enhances charge transfer kinetics. Well-defined copper microcrystals were electrodeposited onto carbon screen-printed electrodes (SPCEs) via cyclic voltammetry, resulting in excellent catalytic activity toward NO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−</sup>electroreduction. The developed sensor demonstrated a sensitivity of 44.71 µA/mM, a detection limit (LoD) of 0.87 µM, and a linear dynamic range between 50 µM and 3 mM. It also showed good reproducibility (maximum relative standard deviation, RSD, of 4%) and repeatability (RSD of 5.5% after ten consecutive measurements). For nitrite detection, a mixture of copper and manganese oxides (CuO and MnO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>) was electrodeposited onto a SPCE. These oxides act synergistically as electrocatalysts for the oxidation of N 02- to NO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−</sup>, leveraging their complementary redox properties. The resulting sensor exhibited high catalytic efficiency, with a sensitivity of 10.83 µA/µM, an LoD of 0.071 µM, and a linear response across the 0.2-60 µM range. Reproducibility (maximum RSD of 2.91%) and repeatability (up to three uses with a maximum RSD of 6.5%) tests were performed. Furthermore, simultaneous monitoring of NO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−</sup>and NO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−</sup>ions is viable by employing portable multichannel potentiostats, which allow independent multiple measurements. This approach enables efficient, real-time, and on-site multiplexed water quality analysis.

title: AI-Driven Sentiment Analysis for Stakeholder Feedback in Large Industrial Projects
authors: Mohammed J. Altooq, Baraa T. Sharef
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: This project presents a multilingual system designed to analyze stakeholder feedback and classify messages according to sentiment, topic, and urgency. The system was created to support organizations that handle frequent communication involving concerns, safety issues, service requests, and general observations. By combining transformer based language models with rule-based reasoning, the system aims to interpret feedback in a way that feels both accurate and practical in real operational environments. Overall, the project demonstrates how modern natural language processing techniques can contribute to safer, more informed, and more responsive decision-making within complex industrial settings.

title: Mock Circulatory Platform Simulating Cardiovascular Physiology for Validation of Arm-Wearable Blood Pressure Measurement Systems
authors: Zexu Yang, Chentao Du, Mengkang Deng, Ningxu Yuan, Xinci Gong, Xinge Yu, Tingrui Pan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Micro/Nano Sensors for AI, Healthcare, and Robotics (NSENS)
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases (CVD) rank among the foremost causes of mortality globally, with arteriosclerosis serving as a pivotal pathophysiological foundation. A healthy aorta exerts a robust buffering capacity, safeguarding the microvascular system against potentially harmful fluctuations in pressure and blood flow. With aging and various pathological states, aortic stiffening poses a severe threat to cardiovascular health. Wearable devices facilitate the prediction of cardiovascular disease risks and support early intervention efforts. These devices continuously monitor physiological parameters such as blood pressure and heart rate non-invasively, allowing individuals to manage their cardiovascular health in real-time without the presence of medical professionals. Circulatory simulators play a crucial role in the validation of wearable blood pressure monitoring devices. By emulating real hemodynamic environments, these simulators enable researchers to assess the accuracy and stability of devices under laboratory conditions. This study introduces a cardiovascular simulator based on the WINDKESSEL model, which mimics the physiological structure of the human cardiovascular system. It precisely controls parameters such as heart rate, cardiac output, aortic compliance, and peripheral resistance to reproduce human blood pressure waveforms (BPW). Measurement of BPW at the brachial and radial/ulnar arteries can be taken from the surface of an artificial arm, providing a rich and stable testing scenario for arm-worn blood pressure monitoring devices to analyze blood pressure, pulse wave velocity, augmentation index and aortic compliance.

title: Research on a Time-Series Simulation Method for Carbon Emissions in Power Systems Considering the Carbon Storage Characteristics of Energy Storage
authors: Baodi Ding, Rong Pan, Yanping Xv, Mingxin Zhao, Jie Bai, Xiaohui Qin
publish: 2025 4th Asia Power and Electrical Technology Conference (APET)
abstract: Energy storage plays an increasingly important role in new power systems, but the changes in carbon emission responsibility it brings have not been fully accounted for. This paper proposes a time-series simulation method for power system carbon emissions by considering the carbon storage characteristics of energy storage. Firstly, based on time-series production simulation calculations, the output of various types of units and tielines in each time period is clarified. Secondly, a carbon emission storage model for the energy storage system is constructed to clarify the changes in the total carbon emissions within the model. Furthermore, time-series carbon emissions for the power system are revised period by period. Finally, by constructing a virtual system including thermal power, gas power, tie-lines, and energy storage, the changes in system time-series carbon emissions and electricity carbon emission factors before and after considering the energy storage system's carbon storage are compared, verifying the feasibility and advancement of the proposed method.

title: AL-CNN: A Unified Framework for Image Classification and Captioning
authors: Monika Chawla, Rashmi Agrawal
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: In order to improve semantic and contextual comprehension in visual analysis, we present an innovative hybrid deep learning architecture which incorporates picture categorization and image captioning. Our new AL-CNN model, which is a fusion of AlexNet and LeNet architectures, achieves more than 91% accuracy on CIFAR10 and Fashion-MNIST datasets. A transformer decoder and ResNet-101 encoder are used for captioning, producing contextually rich captions evaluated using BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR, and CIDEr scores. Excellent generalization, perturbation robustness, and domain scalability are demonstrated by the system in many different applications, such as digital accessibility, monitoring, medical diagnostics, and autonomous driving. Fairness-aware training and privacy-preserving computation ensure ethical deployment, making the framework a dependable multimodal AI application solution.

title: A Short-Term Wind Power Prediction Model Based on PSO-SVMD-GRU
authors: Jinlin Chu, Changhong Lin, Yunpeng Cai
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer Science, and Information Processing (AICSIP)
abstract: Presently, the wind turbines managed by domestic wind farm systems are frequently utilized to maintain regular power generation efficiency through power transmission, which ensures that the turbine blades continue to rotate. Consequently, an accurate wind power prediction model is crucial for the effective operation of these turbines and optimal resource allocation. Firstly, variations in power generation often occur irregularly in frequency, making it difficult to parameterize this frequency accurately. Secondly, while historical generation data influences future output, environmental factors also play a critical role that cannot be overlooked; thus complicating the modeling of interactions between frequency and ecological variables. In this paper, we propose employing the particle swarm optimization algorithm to decompose parameters related to frequency decomposition effectively. This approach addresses the issue of noninterpretability associated with the frequency domain decomposition of traditional time series data. Secondly, we apply feature fusion techniques to integrate both time-series effects and environmental impacts, thereby mitigating challenges related to insufficient predictive accuracy when relying solely on single variables. Finally, we utilize a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) model for learning purposes and conduct comparative experiments designed to validate the performance of our proposed model.

title: Energy Performance Analysis and Optimization of Air Conditioning System in Commercial Buildings Using Advanced Energy Modeling
authors: Mohamed A. Jouma, Polina Matesha, Mohammad Kamil, Muhammad Zubair
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: Commercial cooling is surging worldwide, stressing electricity grids, water supplies, and carbon budgets-especially in hot climates. We show that the key to scalable relief is not a refrigerant swap but co-optimization of the working fluid and heatrejection strategy. Using hourly, year-long simulations for a retail system across ten cities spanning hot-arid, hot-humid, temperate, and cold climates, we test ammonia, R22, R134a, and propane paired with air-cooled, water-cooled, cooling-tower, cooling-tower with heat recovery, and hybrid condensers. With the baseline aircooled condenser, ammonia and R134a deliver the highest coefficients of performance, most notably in colder regions where lower ambient temperatures reduce condensing temperature and pressure ratio. Crucially, holding propane fixed, condenser choice overturns conventional rankings. CT/HR delivers the largest energy reduction (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 21 \%$</tex>) with low water use; CT saves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 15 \%$</tex> but requires substantially more water. The hybrid option yields only marginal gains (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 1 \%$</tex>) with very high water use, and the water-cooled system increases energy use (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 1 \%$</tex>) while being the most water-intensive. COPs peak in colder cities, but absolute annual savings are greatest in high-load, near-equatorial markets because savings scale with operating hours. Together, the results establish a climate-robust rule: co-optimize refrigerant and heat rejection-ideally pairing a high-efficiency fluid (or even R290) with CT/HR-to secure reliable reductions in energy use and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> while moderating water demand.

title: 3D Printing Application in Developing Advanced Polymer-Metal Hybrid Structures Built by Fused Deposition Modeling
authors: Mazhar Azeem, Muaad Hussain Saleh Mohamed Al-Ali, Tarak Amine, Ahmed Najeeb Ahmed Abdalla Amiri
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: Polymer-metal hybrid materials have grown significantly in recent years due to their exceptional mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties. These hybrid materials play a significant role in industrial applications and are usually used in complex assembly parts. In this study, 3D printing technique was employed to make a lap joint between Polymers (PLA and ABS) and pre-punched Aluminum Al 5052 sheets. Tensile and bending tests were conducted to investigate the impact of Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) process parameters on the mechanical properties of the resulting Polymer-Metal Hybrid Structures. The results obtained from the tests were found to be satisfactorily acceptable, indicating improvements in the methodology for designing and manufacturing polymer/metal hybrid structures.

title: Machine Learning Power Side-Channel Attack on SNOW-V
authors: Deepak, Rahul Balout, Anupam Golder, Suparna Kundu, Angshuman Karmakar, Debayan Das
publish: 2026 39th International Conference on VLSI Design &amp; 25th International Conference on Embedded Systems (VLSID)
abstract: This paper demonstrates a power analysis-based Side-Channel Analysis (SCA) attack on the SNOW-V encryption algorithm, which is a 5 G mobile communication security standard candidate. Implemented on an STM32 microcontroller, power traces captured with a ChipWhisperer board were analyzed, with Test Vector Leakage Assessment (TVLA) confirming exploitable leakage. Profiling attacks using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) and Fully Connected Neural Networks (FCN) achieved efficient key recovery, with FCN achieving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>5 \times$</tex> lower minimum traces to disclosure (MTD) compared to the state-of-the-art Correlational Power Analysis (CPA) assisted with LDA. The results highlight the vulnerability of SNOW-V to machine learning-based SCA and the need for robust countermeasures.

title: Modeling and Simulation of Staircase Wear in Ancient Buildings
authors: Jingyi Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer Science, and Information Processing (AICSIP)
abstract: This study develops a comprehensive mathematical model to analyze staircase wear in ancient buildings by integrating physical modeling, agent-based simulations, and statistical analysis. I employ Archard's wear equation to account for natural and human-induced wear and utilize Agent-Based Modeling (ABM) with Poisson processes and Markov chains to reflect user behavior patterns. The model effectively simulates staircase wear patterns, providing insights into historical usage.

title: Highly Efficient Solid State Organic Electrochemical Transistor for Ion Sensing
authors: Aaishiki Saha, Rupesh Kumar, Akshay Moudgil
publish: 2025 IEEE 18th International Conference on Nano/Molecular Medicine &amp; Engineering (NANOMED)
abstract: Wearable solid-state organic electrochemical transistors (SSOECTs) represent a promising approach for non-invasive, real-time biochemical sensing in healthcare. These devices enable multiplexed ion detection, such as sodium <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{Na}^{+})$</tex> and potassium <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{K}^{+})$</tex>, directly from sweat, providing continuous physiological monitoring without invasive sampling. The flexible, miniaturized design of SSOECT incorporates solid-contact architecture, which simplifies sample handling and enhances device robustness. Integration of ion-selective membranes (ISMs) with a biocompatible semiconducting channel (PEDOT:PSS/[MTEOA][MeOSO <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }_{3}$</tex>] ) achieves high selectivity and sensitivity while operating at low voltages. The extended gate configuration improves signal transduction, offering fast response times suitable for dynamic sweat analysis. Collectively, these features address critical challenges in wearable biosensors, such as stability, specificity, and user comfort, making SSOECTs an attractive platform for point-ofcare diagnostics and personalized health monitoring applications.

title: A Performance Evaluation Approach for Hardware System Based on Neural Networks
authors: Bijing Liu, Li Zhao, Haiteng Hang, Yong Yang, Xiaosong Zhu
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer Science, and Information Processing (AICSIP)
abstract: With the rapid development of information technology, the accuracy of hardware system performance evaluation has become increasingly important. This paper proposes a hardware system performance testing system and its methodology, innovatively integrating the neural network evaluation method with the fuzzy comprehensive evaluation method to effectively address the complexity and uncertainty issues in the hardware system performance evaluation process. Specifically, automated testing tools are first used to collect relevant data of the hardware system, and then a neural network is trained to learn the system performance indicators. On this basis, the fuzzy comprehensive evaluation method is adopted to conduct a comprehensive and objective performance assessment of the system. The evaluation system constructed in this paper can efficiently extract key features from massive data, significantly improving the accuracy and reliability of evaluation. The proposed evaluation method not only provides a new idea for hardware system performance evaluation but also offers theoretical basis and decision-making references for the safe operation and optimization of systems in other key fields.

title: Advancing Disease Prediction with Data Synthesis and Noise-tolerant Training
authors: Jiaxin Duan, Haifeng Jing, Fengyu Lu
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer Science, and Information Processing (AICSIP)
abstract: Disease prediction (DP) requires an automatic program to infer a patient's underlying diseases from his/her electronic medical records (EMRs), a long-standing focus in bioinformatics. Although deep learning approaches have achieved promising results on this task, the lack of sufficient clinical EMRs significantly prohibits further progress. In this paper, we exploit advanced technologies encompassing pre-trained language models (PLMs) to address this problem. We first reformulate DP as a masked language modeling problem, which facilitates BERT, with slight fine-tuning, to jointly predict the assertion words that indicate the diagnostic opinion of candidate diseases. We also leverage the excellent generation ability of GPT-4, reprocessing abundant online medical texts to augment the limited EMR data. For this, we ask GPT-4 to synthesize novel EMRs using medical cases and dialogues that contain partial clinical information by performing a Grouped Pointwise MixUp algorithm. To utilize the synthetic data without suffering potential biases, we establish criteria and prompt GPT-4 to filter out flawed samples through knowledge-based question-answering and use the remaining to fine-tune BERT through noise-tolerant training. Extensive evaluation results on two real datasets demonstrate substantial enhancements achieved by our methods. Our codes and data will be released to facilitate further studies.

title: A Microfluidic Chip for Rapid Colorimetric Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing at Point of Care
authors: Salimbabu Abdulla, Amin F. Majdalawieh, Mohamed Abdelgawad
publish: 2025 IEEE 18th International Conference on Nano/Molecular Medicine &amp; Engineering (NANOMED)
abstract: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is emerging as one of the most critical global health threats, with the potential to render current antibiotics ineffective. The excessive and often uncontrolled use of antibiotics in medicine and food production has accelerated the development of resistant pathogens, putting vulnerable and immunocompromised populations at serious risk. Further aggravating this issue, delays in conventional antibiotic susceptibility testing (AST) impede timely treatment, allowing infections to spread and resistance to intensify. In this study, we harnessed the precision of microfluidics, a technology that manipulates minute fluid volumes within microscale channels to revolutionize AST. We developed a novel microfluidic chip for rapid, colorimetric susceptibility testing using resazurin, a redox dye that shifts from blue to pink in the presence of metabolically active bacteria. This method enables susceptibility assessment within 2 hours, a significant improvement over the 18-24 hours required for traditional culture-based tests. To ensure accuracy and reproducibility, we optimized key parameters, including dye concentration, bacterial density, and incubation time. The optimized protocol was applied to two Gram-negative bacteria: Salmonella enterica (commercial strain) and Escherichia coli (clinical isolate). Our findings revealed a strong inverse correlation between the inhibition zone diameters obtained using the gold-standard disc diffusion method and the red intensity values measured in ImageJ on the microfluidic chip. This correlation shows that antibiotics with larger inhibition zones, such as Cefazolin (24 mm) and Piperacillin/Tazobactam (25 mm), yielded lower red-intensity values (98.54 a.u. and 97.00 a.u.), indicating potent bacterial inhibition consistent with CLSI standards. Levofloxacin and Cefepime demonstrated the highest inhibitory activity, consistent with their interpretive categories of intermediate (I) and resistant (R). Overall, our findings validate the reliability and speed of the microfluidic AST platform, showcasing its potential as a compact, point-of-care tool to accelerate antimicrobial testing and combat AMR.

title: Enterprise-Scale SSL/TLS Automation Using Let’s Encrypt: A Practical Framework for Certificate Lifecycle Management
authors: Usha Rani Sheri, VasanthRao Jadav
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Managing SSL/TLS certificates remains one of the most persistent operational challenges in enterprise security. Organizations typically rely on manual processes, spreadsheets, or ad-hoc renewal practices that introduce hidden risks, service outages, and inconsistent security posture. This paper presents an enterprise-oriented framework for automated certificate lifecycle management using Let’s Encrypt and the ACME protocol, supported by a practical deployment model. Unlike prior work that focuses mainly on protocol specifications, this paper emphasizes operational realities in enterprise environments—multi-team coordination, hybrid infrastructures, DNS limitations, and CI/CD-driven deployments. A mid-sized organization’s evaluation demonstrates measurable improvements in reliability, administrative efficiency, and outage prevention. The findings highlight that automated SSL/TLS certification is no longer optional but foundational to modern cyber-resilience and zero-trust architectures.

title: Performance Analysis of Credit Card Behavior Score Prediction Using Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Said Salloum, Khalaf Tahat, Ahmed Mansoori, Raghad Alfaisal, Chaker Mhamdi, Dina Tahat
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Communication, Networking and Services (ICCNS)
abstract: Credit scoring has an influential function in financial risk assessment which assists institutions make informed lending decisions. Classical credit scoring methods depend on historical financial data and rule-based models that tend generally not to capture non-linear relationships in consumer behaviour. Machine learning models propound an opportunity to augment risk prediction through data-driven cognisance. This study evaluates a CNN-based approach for predicting credit behaviour employing the Kaggle Credit Card Behaviour Score dataset. The model attained an accuracy of 98.79%, but the ROC AUC score (0.71) specifies challenges in discerning customers with high-risk. The outcomes designate that while credit risk assessment can be enhanced by CNNs, it is essential to address class imbalance and improve feature selection for practical application in financial decision-making.

title: Stress Monitoring Using Wearable Technology
authors: Sakshi Prasad, Vikas Upadhyaya
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: The topic of this research is stress detection and management using wearable technology with the aim of making stress detection and management accessible and user-friendly. It focuses on helping users to easily track their stress levels and overall health. The review includes wearable devices that can be used for real-time stress detection, including those recommended by healthcare practitioners.There are numerous key findings from the research. Sensors like PPG, ECG, EDA are effective in measuring the physiological data that is needed for stress detection. For example, elevated heart rate and blood pressure, reduced HRV, lower skin temperature, and increased skin conductivityThere are also some gaps and challenges identified. First being the non-standardized grading system. Second is the subjective nature of stress. Third is the need for rigorous ML training to create personalized models. Another important problem to tackle is contextual awareness. The model should be able to differentiate between bodily changes caused by stress or other factors like exercise, caffeine etc.The future directions involve advancement in the current methodology with AI/ML based biofeedback mechanism that provides real-time calming feedback and also collects post intervention data to analyze its effectiveness. This proposal will help develop a personalized and adaptive model.

title: Predicting Lithium-Ion Battery Degradation Modes Using a Machine Learning Approach Based on EIS Measurements
authors: Filippo Battaglia, Salvatore Gianluca Leonardi, Giovanni Lucà Trombetta, Davide Aloisio, Gioacchino Musicò, Giovanni Brunaccini, Giovanni Gugliandolo, Francesco Sergi, Nicola Donato, Giuseppe Campobello
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Green Technologies, Renewable Energy and Ecological Sustainability (MetroGREENST)
abstract: This study presents a machine learning (ML) framework that is able to predict the degradation modes of a lithiumion battery. Leveraging on some analytical models recently proposed in literature, the proposed algorithm estimates three main ageing indicators (Conductivity Loss (CL), Loss of Active Material (LAM) and Loss of Lithium Inventory (LLI)), using ML models based on battery-related features, i.e., Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), State of Charge (SoC), Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) and number of cycles. In order to test the accuracy of these ML models, two different datasets were created (for testing and validation), using data acquired from 8 different cells. Experimental results show that the proposed approach, based on ML, is able to determine the ageing status of a battery with high accuracy, thus offering a reliable solution for battery health diagnostics.

title: A Mini-Review on Pseudolite-Based User Positioning: Are Simpler Approaches Better?
authors: Chih-Chun Chen, Tim Reuscher, Heike Vallery
abstract: Pseudolites (PLs) transmit Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-like signals and provide effective solutions to replace or augment GNSS in environments where signals are denied or degraded. This review provides an overview of existing studies on PL-based user positioning, categorized into standalone methods and sensor fusion approaches. Further, it examines the algorithms, implementations, and performance of these approaches. Standalone PL and simple algorithms are popular and appear to outperform sensor fusion and more complex algorithms when considering positioning accuracy alone. However, this finding may be highly misleading, as simpler approaches also tend to use simpler evaluation scenarios. Studies that directly compare algorithms are scarce. This indicates the need for systematic benchmarking.

title: A novel unsupervised method for software defect prediction based on clustering assumption
authors: Shiqi Tang, Xinlan Tie, Minggui Song, Qiao Yang
abstract: Software defect prediction (SDP) is a very important technique used in the software testing. It helps to quickly predict the defects contained in software in the early stage of development. Currently, many SDP methods use machine learning algorithms to build defect prediction models based on historical data. However, due to the lack of historical data that can be used to train the models, unsupervised defect prediction (UDP) methods have been proposed. The instance-violation-score-based-clustering (IVSBC) method is an example of the UDP method. It is relatively effective but vulnerable to redundant features and rough split of the feature space. To reduce those shortcoming, this paper proposes an unsupervised defect prediction method named CLA-CA based on clustering assumption. The CLA-CA searches different regions of every feature space to find an appropriate classification threshold, and remove redundant features, so as to improve the prediction performance. Experiments show that the CLA-CA is not only better than other mainstream UDP methods, but also outperforms most supervised defect prediction methods.

title: A 14-bit 500-MS/s Synthesizable Current-Steering DAC for Cryogenic Applications
authors: Yi-Hsuan Chiu, Zih-Ting Weng, Shen-Iuan Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: A 14-bit 500-MS/s synthesizable current-steering digital-to-analog converter (CS-DAC) is presented by using the digital place-and-route (DPR) tools. The proposed unit current cells for the CS-DAC are presented to relax the issues due to the auto-routed wires by using DPR tools and the layout-dependent effects (LDEs). For the unit current cells, the clustered placement method is used to improve the timing skews induced by the auto-routed wires. To reduce the parasitic coupling capacitances, the bias rail is presented for the unit current cells. A plug-in layout method is used to meet the current density requirement and reduce the parasitic capacitances due to the limited width of the auto-routed wires. By using the DPR tools, the layout time is significantly reduced compared with the manual layout. This 14-bit 500-MS/s CS-DAC is fabricated in 40-nm CMOS technology. It consumes 12.42 mW from a 1.1-V supply at a rate of 500 MS/s. At the temperature of 300 K, it achieves >50-dBc spurious-free dynamic range (SFDR) and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lt -66$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-dBc third-order intermodulation distortion (IM3). At the temperature of 4 K, it achieves >43 dBc SFDR and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lt -66$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dBc IM3.

title: Fuzzy Dynamic Hypergraph Neural Network Based on Fused Granule
authors: Tao Yin, Yuepeng Chen, Jiashuang Huang, Hengrong Ju, Witold Pedrycz, Weiping Ding
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: The hypergraph neural network (HGNN) is designed to capture high-order correlations within hypergraph structures, demonstrating superior performance in both classification and regression tasks. However, the conventional HGNN model does not sufficiently address both feature redundancy and inherent ambiguity within the hypergraph structure, resulting in overfitting and data heterogeneity. To address the challenge, this paper proposes the fuzzy dynamic hypergraph neural network based on fused granule (FDHGNN). First, we develop the global fuzzy information-guided feature selection module. By utilizing weighted scores to extract the discriminative feature subset, we mitigate the issue of overfitting. Second, we employ the multi-granularity evidence fusion method to obtain the weight matrix of hyperedges. The fuzzy evidence hypergraph convolution is performed using the fuzzy hyperedge weights, which reduces the impact of heterogeneous hyperedges. Third, we construct the fuzzy dynamic hypergraph based on the fuzzy cluster method and sparse objective function. The dynamic hyperedge is generated from the fuzzy cluster granule and optimal k neighbors viewed through the lens of granular computing. During each iteration, modifications are applied to update the hypergraph topology. Extensive experiment results on Pubmed dataset confirm that the F1-score of FDHGNN improves by about 2% over existing approaches. The maximum p-value of Friedman test on four datasets is about $10^{-3}$, demonstrating that FDHGNN surpasses comparison approaches. Experimental analysis on Xiangya dataset reveals that the accuracy and F1-score of FDHGNN improve by 2.1% and 3.3%, respectively, compared to current models. The proposed method establishes a novel direction for optimizing HGNN.

title: Confidence-Aware Optimal Transport for Open Set Time Series Adaptation Under Non-Stationary Shifts_supp1-3661475.pdf
authors: Khushboo Mishra
abstract: Open Set Domain Adaptation (OSDA) in non-stationary time series remains a challenging problem, particularly when the target domain contains previously unseen classes and exhibits temporal drift due to changing behaviors, sensor noise, or environmental variability. Existing adaptation methods often fail in such settings, misaligning unknown target samples and resulting in negative transfer. We propose a Confidence-Aware Latent Optimal Transport framework, named as CALOT, that performs selective domain alignment in a learned latent space. CALOT filters out low-confidence target samples—those likely to belong to unknown classes—using entropy-based confidence scoring, and applies optimal transport only to the most reliable instances. A self-regularization loss further enhances alignment stability under temporal variability. Our work achieves 90% accuracy on known classes and 80% accuracy in rejecting unknown classes, resulting in a strong H-score of 85% on the challenging non-stationary HHAR dataset. Additionally, it obtains H-scores of 76% on WISDM, 73% on HAR, 74% on EDF, and 75% on the BOILER dataset.

title: Loss Tomography for Quantum Networks
authors: Jake Navas, Jaden Brewer, Jaime Diaz, Matheus Guedes de Andrade, Don Towsley, Inès Montaño
abstract: With steady progress in the development of quantum networks, the question on how to best provide end-to-end characterization of such networks (Quantum Network Tomography) is quickly becoming more pressing. Initial results demonstrated how we can utilize multipartite entanglement distribution to determine error probabilities of single-Pauli channels and depolarizing channels. In this work, we show how the analysis of quantum capacity regions can be used as a powerful new tool in quantum network tomography. As a first application of the proposed method, we demonstrate how we can characterize the loss on quantum channels in the network directly from quantum capacity region diagrams, even in the presence of bit-flip errors. Our results indicate that quantum capacity regions are not only valuable for network design, resource allocation, and protocol benchmarking, but also show promise for applications in quantum network tomography, particularly in loss tomography.

title: Andronov-Hopf Oscillator Based Harmonic Elimination Scheme for Grid-Connected Inverters
authors: Muhammad Noman Ashraf, Mohamed Shawky El Moursi, Khalifa Al Hosani, Ahmed Al Durra
abstract: Conventional harmonic elimination methods for Grid-Connected Inverters (GCI) rely on individual harmonic extraction and compensation, resulting in increased complexity and slow dynamic response. In this paper, the Virtual Oscillator based Harmonic Compensation (VO-HC) is proposed. It uses the Andronov–Hopf oscillator not for synchronization or grid-forming purposes, but to extract the fundamental component from the distorted grid current. The Hopf oscillator is inherently frequency-adaptive, allowing it to extract the fundamental component and attenuate harmonics with negligible phase delay compared to traditional filters. The extractor provides unity-gain fundamental tracking even under frequency deviations and weak-grid conditions. The extracted fundamental is then subtracted from the measured grid current to isolate the grid current harmonics. These harmonics are compensated using a simple proportional compensator loop in the stationary frame as a feedforward, operating in parallel with a conventional Synchronous Reference Frame (SRF) current controller. This results in fast low-order harmonic compensation of grid current, improving the Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) of the GCI system. Experimental results demonstrate THD reduction from up to 18.4% to below 2.5%, with a transient settling time of approximately 2–3 ms. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through both simulation and experimental results conducted on a laboratory two-inverter microgrid setup under distorted and weak-grid conditions.

title: Improved and Accelerated Text-to-Image Generation with Collect, Reflect, and Refine
authors: Shitong Shao, Zikai Zhou, Dian Xie, Yuetong Fang, Tian Ye, Lichen Bai, Bo Han, Zeke Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Recently, enhancing the generative capability of text-to-image (T2I) models has become a promising direction in both academia and industry. Prior studies often focused on either improving generative quality or reducing inference latency, but typically failed to improve both quality and speed simultaneously. Moreover, existing inference-enhancement methods do not achieve significant improvements simultaneously across both diffusion models (DMs) and autoregressive models (ARMs). In this paper, we introduce a general tuning-based inference-enhancement framework, named ${\bm {CoRe}^{2}}$, which is the first to simultaneously achieve significant generative quality and reduced inference overhead across DMs and ARMs, to the best of our knowledge. ${\bm {CoRe}^{2}}$ comprises three stages: ${\bm {Co}llect}$, ${\bm {Re}flect}$, and ${\bm {Re}fine}$. During the Collect stage, classifier-free guidance (CFG) trajectories are collected and subsequently used in the Reflect stage to train a weak model capable of reflecting the “easy-to-learn” content. Finally, during the Refine stage, CoRe$^{2}$ can utilize the trained weak model to achieve speedup and performance gain in inference. Specifically, in the early sampling steps, CoRe$^{2}$ employs weak-to-strong guidance to refine the “difficult-to-learn” and realistic content, thereby improving generative quality. In the later sampling steps, CoRe$^{2}$ can use the weak model to generate “easy-to-learn” content instead of CFG, dramatically reducing inference time. Experimental outcomes substantiates CoRe$^{2}$ achieve significant performance improvements on HPD v2, Pick-of-Pic, Drawbench, GenEval, and T2I-Compbench across SDXL, SD3.5, FLUX and LlamaGen. Notably, for SD3.5, CoRe$^{2}$ can be seamlessly integrated with the state-of-the-art inference-enhancement algorithm Z-Sampling, outperforming it with winning rates of 73% and 69% on PickScore and AES even with less time.

title: EMMA: Ephemeris-Assisted LEO Inter-Satellite Link Spoof Detection Dataset and ML Framework
authors: Varun Kohli, Arijit Bhattacharjee, Samar Shailendra, Biplab Sikdar
abstract: Inter-satellite link (ISL) spoofing in Low Earth Orbit (LEO) constellations poses a growing yet underexplored security challenge. This letter presents EMMA, a lightweight Multi-task Learning (MTL)-based framework for ISL authentication and spoof detection using simple physical behavior-based features. It also presents the first benchmark simulation dataset on ISL security. EMMA achieves detection rates of 94.75% and 94.46% on registered and spoofing satellite samples, respectively, at low inference latency (10−4 seconds) and low memory overhead (3.5 KB). We also compare EMMA with existing works to demonstrate its comparative effectiveness.

title: Evaluating the Effectiveness of Deep Learning Models for Foundational Program Analysis Tasks
authors: Qian Chen, Rouyi Chen, Chenyang Yu, Ruyan Liu, Chi Zhang, Yu Wang, Ke Wang, Linzhang Wang

title: Multiphysics Simulation of an Acoustic-Electric Chip for Multiscale Particle and Droplet Manipulation
authors: Hengchao Qu, Wenfeng Liang, Dingyi Wang, Yiwei Zhang, Si Tang, Huiyao Shi, Lianqing Liu, Jialin Shi
publish: 2025 IEEE 18th International Conference on Nano/Molecular Medicine &amp; Engineering (NANOMED)
abstract: As micromanipulation technologies advance toward higher integration and multifunctionality, achieving precise control of multiscale objects within a unified platform has become a pressing challenge. This study proposes and simulates an electroacoustic integrated microfluidic chip that combines surface acoustic wave (SAW) and electric field-based actuation to enable programmable manipulation of particles and droplets across nano- to submillimeter scales. A multiphysics model was developed using COMSOL Multiphysics to simulate the spatial distributions of acoustic pressure, electric field, and induced forces, incorporating both PDMS and water environments. Simulation results reveal various manipulation modes, including 1D and 2D SAW-based particle patterning, single-particle trajectory control via phase modulation, and droplet deformation, fusion, and splitting under different electric field configurations. The acoustic and electric modules can operate independently or synergistically, offering a versatile and reconfigurable platform. This work demonstrates the feasibility and flexibility of the hybrid electroacoustic integration for multiscale manipulation, providing theoretical foundations for next-generation lab-on-achip applications in synthetic biology, point-of-care diagnostics, and microfluidic automation.

title: Development of Active Disturbance Rejection Control for Mobile Robot Using Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Controller
authors: Usama Habib, Ashraf Elnagar, R. Fareh, Ahmad M. El-Nagar
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: Designing an effective control system is a cornerstone of robotics, but it's no easy task, especially when it comes to ensuring reliable performance and managing unexpected disturbances. Active Disturbance Rejection Control (ADRC) has earned notable attention for its strength in tackling system uncertainties and external disruptions. Despite its popularity, traditional ADRC systems often rely on fixed gain Proportional Derivative (PD) controller, which can struggle to adapt in dynamic or unpredictable environments. To address this limitation, this paper introduces an improved ADRC approach that swaps out the conventional PD controller with the Interval Type-2 Fuzzy PD (IT2FPD) controller. Unlike classical PD, the IT2FPD controller utilizes fuzzy logic to dynamically adjust the proportional gain based on the system's error, allowing for improved adaptability and disturbance rejection. The effectiveness of this technique is tested through trajectory tracking simulations on MATLAB. The results demonstrate that the ADRC based on IT2FPD (ADRC-IT2FPD) outperforms classical ADRC as well as Type-1 Fuzzy Logic PD (IT1FPD) controller, with enhanced stability, less trajectory tracking error and improved control on high magnitude disturbances.

title: Policy-as-Code Auto-Remediation with Human-Centered Explanations
authors: Pragya Keshap, Arpana Hosabettu, Akshay Mittal
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Cloud misconfigurations—such as publicly exposed storage buckets or overly permissive IAM policies—remain a leading cause of security incidents. This paper introduces a policy-as-code framework for automated remediation with human-centered explanations. The system detects violations, synthesizes minimal Terraform patches, and generates didactic diffs that show both the changes and the rationale. A safety checker enforces formal invariants to prevent over-remediation, while a rollback controller ensures reversibility. An observability layer records all actions into logs and metrics, supporting reproducibility and comparative evaluation.Evaluation across 100 synthetic environments demonstrates that the framework achieves an 8× improvement in remediation speed compared to manual intervention, eliminates over-remediation incidents through invariant checking, and scales sub-linearly from 50 to 200 resources. Explanations reduced violation recurrence by nearly half, transforming remediation events into opportunities for operator learning. These results show that remediation can be both automated and explainable.The framework’s modular design and benchmark harness provide a foundation for reproducible research in explainable cloud security, with future work focused on production-scale validation, adaptive explanations, and multi-cloud portability.

title: Predicting Regional Diffusion of EV Incentives Through Graph Neural Networks
authors: Said Almaqbali, Asmaa H. Marhoubi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: This research project explores the prediction of the electric vehicle (EV) adoption and sales revenue through a multi-algorithm machine learning model on a sample of 550 regional sales records sourced on Kaggle. The data set is made up of detailed attributes, that is, the region, type of vehicle, and customer segment, and also attributes that pertain to the policy, such as discounts and fast-charging arrangements. The idea was to improve on the predictive performance of EV revenue in various markets by comparing and refining the baseline models of Random Forest, XGBoost, and Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP). The use of advanced preprocessing methods, such as the one-hot encoding of categorical variables and the scaling of features of numerical variables, was used to guarantee model robustness. The model hyperparameter optimization with the use of the GridSearchCV contributed to the significant increase in the performance measures in relation to the initial experiments, during which the R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> scores were negative, and the models showed dismal generalization. Following the optimization, it was found that the Random Forest and XGBoost had significant improvement in lower values of RMSE and MAE, positive values of R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, which showed improved explanatory power. The diffusion model with optimized pipeline offered a solid base of EV market diffusion modeling and can be expanded with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to incorporate the effects of regional policies and diffusion of adoption. The above results illustrate that data-driven optimization methods are effective in enhancing the model precision and offer useful knowledge to policy-makers and manufacturers to plan EV incentive strategies.

title: Exploring Spiking-Vision Transformer Fusion for Speech Emotion Recognition
authors: Ismail Shahin, Mohamed Bader Alsabek, Abdelfatah Ahmed
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: Speech is an essential tool for articulating emotions and attitudes. The extraction and identification of emotions from speech signals is essential, making Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) a prominent field of research. This has resulted in the development of numerous systems and algorithms for identifying an individual's emotional content in speech. SER is crucial for improving Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), but it encounters obstacles such as data quality, model precision, and efficacy under varying settings. This research presents a unique architecture that integrates Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) with Vision Transformers (ViTs) to develop an SNN-ViT model, with the objective of enhancing emotion recognition from speech. Utilizing the extensive RAVDESS dataset, our approach utilizes a comprehensive set of speech features, including Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC), along with their first-order (delta MFCC) and second-order (delta-delta MFCC) derivatives, spectral centroid, spectral roll-off, and spectrograms. This hybrid model leverages the SNN's capacity to analyze temporal dynamics and the ViT's effectiveness in capturing contextual information, establishing a new standard in SER. Our findings illustrate the model's exceptional performance compared to existing approaches, attaining outstanding accuracy, recall, F1-score, and precision measures. Our proposed approach has surpassed the state-of-the-art methods by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$83.06 \%, 78.14 \%, 78.00 \%$</tex>, and 80.91 % in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, respectively.

title: Usage of the Large Action Model in Agentic AI
authors: Pradnya Desai, Ashwini Joshi, Mugdha Kulkarni, Aditya Chincholkar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Large Action Models (LAMs) are advanced AI systems built for function calling, reasoning, and autonomous planning. These models are developed to reduce the complexity often encountered with large language models (LLMs). LAMs enable seamless integration of AI into workflows by proactively managing tasks and making context-aware decisions without the need for explicit instructions. Unlike traditional LLMs which are good at generating text, LAMs take a more agentic approach like anticipating user needs, automating processes, and executing actions intelligently. Rooted in research areas such as robotics, autonomous systems, and gaming AI, LAMs bring a new level of real-time responsiveness and adaptability to enterprise applications.This session will cover the overview of LAM and survey of Salesforce AI Research’s xLAM and its utilization in Agentic AI for next-generation workflow automation.

title: A Simple Wall-Following Control Strategy for Differential-Drive Mobile Robots
authors: Zayed Tariq, Hamed Alhamed, Stefan Tomic, Türker Türker
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: This paper presents a simple yet effective wall-following control strategy for differential-drive autonomous mobile robots operating in indoor environments. The proposed method relies on two low-cost ultrasonic sensors mounted at a fixed angle on the robot's side, enabling both distance and approach angle estimation through basic trigonometric computations. The control architecture consists of two coordinated loops: an inner proportional (P) controller for correcting the robots orientation relative to the wall, and an outer proportional-integral (PI) controller for maintaining the desired lateral distance. This dual-loop design ensures stable and responsive wall-following behavior without the need for complex modeling or expensive hardware. The system is straightforward to implement, making it well-suited for educational platforms, low-budget research prototypes, and real-world applications where cost and simplicity are critical. Simulation results validate the accuracy of the approach, demonstrating its effectiveness in navigating along walls in cluttered or narrow indoor spaces.

title: Dual-Encoder Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network Based on Attribute Completion and Data Augmentation
authors: Lang Liu, Bing Kong
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer Science, and Information Processing (AICSIP)
abstract: Heterogeneous graphs (HGs), which are capable of representing multiple types of nodes and relations, have been widely applied in various domains such as social networks, recommender systems, and bioinformatics. Although numerous heterogeneous graph neural network (HGNN) approaches have been proposed to accommodate such complex structures, existing HGNNs still face two unresolved challenges in practical tasks. On the one hand, missing node attributes are prevalent, especially in scenarios lacking high-quality edge-type information or where attribute collection is costly, thereby interfering with the representation learning process. On the other hand, although graph Transformers (GTs) have been increasingly adopted in graph learning, their application to heterogeneous information networks (HINs) remains in its infancy. The inherent heterogeneity leads to significant attribute differences among nodes and necessitates cross-type interaction modeling, posing substantial technical obstacles for further advancement. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel model: Attribute Completion and Data Augmentation based Dual-Encoder Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network (ADDH). Specifically, the proposed model first optimizes the input data through heterogeneous information completion and data augmentation preprocessing techniques. It then leverages a dual-encoder architecture to effectively integrate heterogeneous information and high-level feature information within the graph, which is subsequently utilized for downstream tasks. Experimental results on four HIN benchmark datasets demonstrate that the ADDH model significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches.
