title: Beam Squint Analysis and Mitigation Using Spatial IDFT for Massive Wideband Arrays
authors: Hesham Beshary, Ali M. Niknejad
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of the beam-squint phenomenon in wideband massive phased arrays and proposes an architecture to achieve squint-free operation. In wideband phased arrays, the sources of beam squint are coherent bandwidth limitations and systematic delay spread, which degrade the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and introduce inter-symbol interference (ISI). The presented analysis quantifies these sources and derives the relationship between array parameters and performance degradation. In addition, this work demonstrates that orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) modulation significantly mitigates self-interference caused by delay spread. Building on this analysis, a spatial inverse discrete Fourier transform (IDFT) architecture is proposed that cancels the intrinsic DFT effect of phased arrays, enabling fully coherent combining across all subcarriers. Using error vector magnitude (EVM) as a performance metric, beam squint and the proposed OFDM and IDFT implementations are evaluated through numerical simulations.

title: DyTopK: Accelerating Top-K SpMV on Embedded FPGAs via Dynamic Floating-Point Quantization
authors: Shangshang Yao, Jingyu Liu, Li Shen, Zuoning Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Top-K sparse matrix–vector multiplication is the computational backbone of modern recommendation systems and graph neural networks (GNNs). While shifting these workloads to the edge offers the potential for ultralow latency and enhanced privacy, it presents significant architectural challenges. Recent HBM-based field-programmable gate array (FPGA) accelerators deployed in data centers have shown promise, but their designs are ill-suited for power-constrained embedded FPGAs, which suffer from the limited bandwidth of standard DDR interfaces and restricted on-chip logic resources. In this article, we propose DyTopK, a hardware–software codesigned accelerator tailored specifically for embedded edge devices. Unlike prior approaches that rely on coarse-grained blockwise quantization or complex filtering, DyTopK introduces a novel Dynamic FP4/FP8 Quantization scheme. This mechanism adapts the numerical format at the granularity of individual nonzero elements. To maximize the utility of restricted off-chip bandwidth, we further propose a bandwidth-maximal sparse matrix compression format named packet CSR (PCSR), perfectly aligning with the 64-bit data bus width of standard DDR4 interfaces. Complementing these data-centric optimizations, we design a high-efficiency hardware architecture featuring a scalable processing element array, allowing for flexible expansion based on available logic resources. Experimental results demonstrate that DyTopK achieves significant improvements, delivering <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$12.4\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> speedup in effective bandwidth utilization and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8.6\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> higher computational throughput compared to state-of-the-art CPU baselines. Furthermore, it outperforms prior FPGA implementations by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.3\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> in energy efficiency while maintaining negligible accuracy loss (less than 0.5% recall drop) at K = 100.

title: E-Band CMOS Frequency Tripler With Even-Harmonic Rejection Using a Switched Self-Biasing Technique
authors: Ui-Gyu Choi, Hyeon Jin Son, Jong-Ryul Yang
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: A CMOS frequency tripler is proposed to suppress unwanted harmonics located close to the desired output tone in the Eband. A high level of harmonic rejection (HR) is achieved without an additional passive filter network by using a self-biasing technique at the tail current source of the tripler core. The even-mode component is filtered through the push–push operation of the tail current source, driven by the dominant fundamental leakage fed back through the self-biasing path. In addition, the inductive source termination is used to boost the third harmonic signal to improve the conversion gain (CG) and efficiency. The proposed frequency tripler, fabricated in 65-nm CMOS technology, exhibits a maximum conversion efficiency (CE) of 6.6% at an input power of 5.5 dBm at 63 GHz. The proposed frequency tripler achieves the measured second and fourth HRs of 57.1 and 48.5 dB, respectively, with a core size of 0.36 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>.

title: Intelligent Reflecting Surfaces for THz Communications: Fundamentals, Key Solutions, and System Prototyping
authors: Qingqing Wu, Yanze Zhu, Qiaoyan Peng, Wanming Hao, Yanzhao Hou, Fengyuan Yang, Wencai Yan, Guoning Wang, Wen Chen, Chi Qiu
abstract: Intelligent reflecting surfaces (IRSs) have emerged as a cost-effective technology for terahertz (THz) communications by enabling programmable control of the wireless environment. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of IRSs-aided THz communications, covering hardware designs, advanced signal processing techniques, and practical deployment strategies. It first examines key THz reconfigurable metasurface architectures, including electronic, optical, phase-change material, and micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS)-based implementations, highlighting their reconfiguration mechanisms and challenges. Then, fundamental effects including near field and beam squint in wideband THz systems are analyzed, along with their impacts on system performance. The paper further explores conventional and beam-squint-assisted channel estimation methods, innovative beam management strategies, and deployment considerations across large- and small-scale scenarios. Practical experiments at 220 gigahertz (GHz) validate the effectiveness of IRS in improving signal strength and communication reliability for both single-user and multi-user setups.

title: Event-Triggered Distributed Formation Tracking of Cooperative Quadrotors Subject to Performance Constraints and Leader Uncertainty
authors: Jianhui Wang, Yancheng Yan, C. L. Philip Chen, Zhi Liu, Zhikai Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper addressed the event-triggered distributed formation tracking problem of cooperative quadrotors in the presence of preassigned performance constraints and leader uncertainty. First, a distributed event-triggered observer is designed to estimate the leader's output without requiring knowledge of the leader's uncertain dynamics, enabling preassigned transient and steady-state performance guarantees on the estimation error. Subsequently, decentralized preassigned performance controllers, integrating separate preassigned performance strategies for position and attitude, are devised for each quadrotor to track the generated reference signal, i.e., the observer's output. These controllers possess the salient feature of ensuring asymptotic tracking performance with arbitrarily predefined transient performance. By incorporating the proposed observer and decentralized controllers into a hierarchical control architecture, discontinuous communication among quadrotors is achieved, and formation tracking errors are assured to converge to a pre-specified arbitrarily small residual set. Finally, the simulation results demonstrate the theoretical findings.

title: Boosting Extrapolation Capabilities in Model-free Offline Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ziru Wang, Wanli Jiang, Zeyang Liu, Xuguang Lan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive and Developmental Systems
abstract: Model-free offline reinforcement learning learns policies from static, pre-collected datasets but often suffers from overestimating the values of out-of-distribution (OOD) data, which leads to biased value predictions and degraded performance. Existing methods mainly address this issue by penalizing OOD actions, but their extrapolation capabilities are limited by two key factors: (i) action-only penalties do not handle state-space extrapolation, causing cascading errors in unseen states; and (ii) uniform penalties suppress potentially beneficial extrapolation, biasing learning toward suboptimal dataset behavior. To overcome these limitations, we introduce ExtrapoBoost Offline Reinforcement Learning (EBORL), a method that improves extrapolation by jointly addressing both OOD states and actions. EBORL leverages an ensemble of Q-networks, each independently minimizing Temporal Difference error on in-distribution data, while maximizing the diversity of input gradients across the Q-networks for OOD state-action pairs. Theoretically, we provide a decomposition of the extrapolation error and prove that EBORL achieves a smaller upper bound on extrapolation error compared to baseline methods. Empirically, EBORL demonstrates strong performance on D4RL tasks with fewer ensemble heads and lower computational cost, and real-world evaluations further highlight its practical applicability.

title: Balancing Efficiency and Restoration: Lightweight Mamba-Based Model for CT Metal Artifact Reduction
authors: Weikai Qu, Sijun Liang, Xianfeng Li, Cheng Pan, An Yan, Ahmed Elazab, Shanzhou Niu, Dong Zeng, Xiang Wan, Changmiao Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: In computed tomography imaging, metal implants frequently generate severe artifacts that compromise image quality and hinder diagnostic accuracy. There are three main challenges in the existing methods: the deterioration of organ and tissue structures, dependence on sinogram data, and an imbalance between resource use and restoration efficiency. Addressing these issues, we introduce MARMamba, which effectively eliminates artifacts caused by metals of different sizes while maintaining the integrity of the original anatomical structures of the image. Furthermore, this model only focuses on CT images affected by metal artifacts, thus negating the requirement for additional input data. The model is a streamlined UNet architecture, which incorporates multi-scale Mamba (MS-Mamba) as its core module. Within MS-Mamba, a flip mamba block captures comprehensive contextual information by analyzing images from multiple orientations. Subsequently, the average maximum feed-forward network integrates critical features with average features to suppress the artifacts. This combination allows MARMamba to reduce artifacts efficiently. The experimental results demonstrate that our model excels in reducing metal artifacts, offering distinct advantages over other models. It also strikes an optimal balance between computational demands, memory usage, and the number of parameters, highlighting its practical utility in the real world. The code of the presented model is available at: https://github.com/RICKand-MORTY/MARMamba.

title:
Ti Metasurface-Integrated Mn
                    <sub>1.56</sub>
                    Co
                    <sub>0.96</sub>
                    Ni
                    <sub>0.48</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>4</sub>
                    MSM-structured Thermal Detector for High-Responsivity LWIR Sensing
authors: Zexin Feng, Yingying Dou, Xijun Yan, Bo Yang, Wenwen Kong, Aimin Chang, Jianping Guo, Changshui Chen
abstract: Infrared thermal detectors are indispensable for modern military surveillance, industrial automation, and civilian safety systems, yet achieving high sensitivity, stability, and spectral selectivity in the long-wave infrared (LWIR) regime remains a significant challenge. To address this, the metal-semiconductor-metal (MSM) structured infrared thermal detector based on a Mn<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1.56</sub>Co<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.96</sub>Ni<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"/>0.4O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub> (MCNO) thin film was designed and fabricated, leveraging finite-difference-time-domain (FDTD) simulations and micro-nano processing techniques. By integrating an optimized Ti metasurface on the MCNO film, the infrared absorption through localized surface plasmon resonance and interference effects were significantly enhanced, resulting in a high thermal sensitivity (temperature coefficient of resistance of -3.82%/K at 25 ° C), a high infrared absorptivity (exceeding 80% in the 8.7-12.1 μm) and a high blackbody responsivity (348.36 V/W at 1 Hz). This detector demonstrates strong potential for advanced low-frequency thermal imaging and sensing applications, particularly in next-generation infrared systems requiring high sensitivity in the long-wave infrared regime.

title: From the Editor
authors: N/A
abstract: This issue features three articles, the first of which is by Fofana et al., titled “Over 40 Years of Ester Dielectric Liquids for High-Voltage Transformers: A Retrospective on Evolution, Performance, and Future Prospects.” The article provides a comprehensive review of the development and application of ester-based insulating liquids in power transformers. The article reviews the history, key drivers, and barriers to the adoption of ester-based insulating liquids, and critical topics specific to their use in power transformers, while emphasizing the role of ester liquids in reducing environmental risks and supporting the transition to safer, more-resilient power systems. The article discusses key benefits of using ester-based insulating liquids, including biodegradability, fire safety, and moisture tolerance, making these liquids well-suited for urban and ecologically sensitive environments. A growing track record in field experience, including units rated up to 750 kV, demonstrates that previous technical limitations are being resolved.

title: Contactless Telemetry System for Condition Monitoring of Large Hydro and Wind Generators
authors: Marius Meiswinkel, Andreas Ridder, Amir Ebrahimi
abstract: Condition monitoring and predictive maintenance of large hydro and wind generators are essential for reducing operational costs and improving system availability. However, traditional monitoring approaches rely primarily on stator-side measurements and bearing vibrations, providing limited observability of rotor-side phenomena where many critical faults originate. This paper presents a comprehensive modular contactless rotor telemetry platform designed for direct rotor-side measurement of multiple physical quantities including magnetic flux density, temperatures, damper winding currents, and mechanical vibrations. The system architecture prioritizes flexibility and scalability, enabling easy integration of different sensor types with configurable sampling rates and resolutions. In a laboratory research environment, fixed sensor configurations and rigid sampling parameters often become limiting factors. The proposed modular design addresses these constraints by allowing users to dynamically add or remove acquisition modules, adapt sampling parameters for specific experiments, and perform on-rotor preprocessing to optimize data transmission bandwidth. Detailed descriptions of the hardware design, synchronization strategy, communication protocol, and software architecture are provided. Experimental validation is demonstrated on a 366 kVA research hydro generator, demonstrating the system’s capability for reliable, synchronized multichannel acquisition on a rotating platform with contactless data transmission. The prototype scales up to 64 simultaneous channels (8 acquisition modules) using 16-bit simultaneous-sampling ADCs and achieves about 28 h runtime at approximately 6.8Wtotal rotor-side power consumption using a 190Wh battery pack.

title: Plenty of Room at the Bottom and Top: Future Perspectives on GaN Power Devices and Applications
authors: Zineng Yang, Xin Yang, Jingcun Liu, Hongchang Cui, Kuokchi Lei, Yujun Guo, Teng Gao, Philip Chan, Ling Xia, Han Wang, Florin Udrea, Dong Dong, Qiang Li, Yuhao Zhang
abstract: GaN power devices have achieved commercial success primarily in the 30-900 V range, particularly in consumer electronics. Yet, to borrow from Richard Feynman's famous remark, there remains plenty of room at both the “bottom” and the “top”- not only in voltage classes, with opportunities at both lower and higher ends, but also in integration scale, spanning from fundamental device architectures to monolithic integration. This paper explores the untapped potential of GaN power technology across these domains, beginning with both ends of the voltage spectrum: low-voltage (<30-50 V) and high-voltage (>1 kV) applications. We highlight emerging GaN device structures under industrial development, analyze their performance limits, and examine transformative GaN applications enabled by these devices, including AI processor power delivery, humanoid robotics, light detection and ranging (LiDAR), electric vehicles (EVs), electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft, MVDC power supplies, and solid-state circuit breakers. Beyond voltage scaling, we explore the promise of monolithic bidirectional GaN switches for various AC power conversion applications, and evaluate GaN's applicability in cryogenic and high-temperature environments for quantum and aerospace systems. Finally, we discuss packaging advances essential to supporting these device capabilities. This work provides a comprehensive perspective on extending the frontier of GaN power electronics and coordinating progress in devices and circuits for next-generation applications.

title: PGD–PPM: A Hybrid Framework for Enhancing Adversarial Robustness in Traffic Sign Recognition System
authors: Raiyah Rub, Shaheena Noor, Irfan Ahmed Usmani, Zain Anwar Ali
abstract: Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR) models based on Deep Learning are highly vulnerable to adversarial perturbations where often imperceptible changes to the input can significantly mislead model predictions and posing serious safety concerns in autonomous driving. This paper presents PGD–PPM, a hybrid defense framework designed to enhance adversarial robustness of TSR models by combining Projected Gradient Descent–based Adversarial Training (PGD-AT) with Pyramid Pooling Module (PPM) integration. The proposed architecture improves multi-scale contextual feature aggregation to resist adversarial perturbations while maintaining high clean accuracy. Four Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures, VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50 and EfficientNetB0 were evaluated on two benchmark datasets GTSRB) and BelgiumTSC datasets under gradient based white-box attacks FGSM, IFGSM and PGD at various perturbation strengths (ϵ=0.1-2.0). The proposed models exhibit significant improvement in clean and adversarial accuracies. For instance, EfficientNetB0 with PGD-PPM achieves the most significant improvement in clean accuracy of 92.57% (up from 87.55%), VGG16 and VGG19 also increase by +4.29% and +3.10%, respectively, whereas EfficientNetB0 maintaining robustness with improved adversarial accuracy of 89% and 87% under strong adversarial condition (PGD attack at, ϵ = 0.1, 0.2). However, ResNet50 achieves the highest adversarial accuracy of 90% and 88% under PGD attack at ϵ = 0.1 and 0.2, which is significantly higher than the corresponding baseline model. The experimental results indicates that proposed framework not only possess a strong improvement in adversarial accuracy but it also improves the clean accuracy which mitigates the accuracy-robustness trade-off, contributing to safer and more reliable intelligent systems.

title: Enhanced Adaptive Duplexing and Robust Resource Allocation for Decentralized FD-NOMA V2X Systems
authors: Zaid Albataineh, Haythem Bany Salameh, Mohammad Al Bataineh, Mohammed Alsumady
abstract: Decentralized vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication enables direct device-to-device transmissions that can offload cellular infrastructure and reduce end-to-end latency. However, the benefits of full-duplex (FD) non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) are highly sensitive to residual self-interference (SI), fast channel fluctuations, and heterogeneous quality-of-service (QoS) requirements. In practice, fixed duplexing, fixed successive interference cancellation (SIC) ordering, and static power allocation can lead to unreliable decoding and inefficient operation across different SNR and SI regimes. This paper proposes an enhanced adaptive hybrid-duplex NOMA framework for decentralized V2X clusters that improves spectral efficiency while preserving QoS robustness under realistic fading and residual SI. The proposed framework integrates: (i) channel-disparity aware user scheduling and pairing to exploit near–far diversity; (ii) adaptive SIC decoding-order selection per channel realization to mitigate decoding failures; (iii) continuous time-splitting (partial-FD) control that smoothly trades FD multiplexing gains against residual SI instead of relying on a binary FD/HD switch; and (iv) outage-aware robust power allocation that enforces minimumrate constraints through deterministic safety margins. Extensive Monte-Carlo evaluations under Rayleigh (urban/crowded) and Rician (suburban/LoS) channels demonstrate that the proposed strategy consistently improves throughput across SNR and SI conditions while maintaining competitive Jain fairness (>0.85) and practical computational complexity of O(U log U) for greedy pairing.

title: Assume a Spherical Cube: Bounding the Force and Torque Induced on a Conductive Nonmagnetic Cube by a Rotating Magnetic Dipole
authors: Travis J. Allen, Levi M. Barker, Jake J. Abbott
abstract: A time-varying magnetic field induces electric currents in a conductive object, which in turn induces a force torque wrench on the object that is not due to ferromagnetism. Prior work empirically modeled the wrench induced in a solid sphere by a rotating magnetic dipole (RMD) field, and then used this model to perform contactless manipulation using multiple RMD field sources, motivated by application in on-orbit satellite servicing and space-debris capture. In this letter, we measure the induced wrench on a conductive, non-ferromagnetic cube—which, unlike on a sphere, is not invariant to orientation—in six canonical configurations, and compare it to that of a sphere with identical volume. Results show that such a spherical model provides a reasonable orientation-invariant approximation of a cube. Further, the induced wrench can be bounded by considering the induced wrenches on spheres with ±15% volume.

title: Optimising P2P Energy Trading Using Internet of Things and Agentic AI Cluster Zooming
authors: Kelvin Anoh, Benard Kipchumba, Dejan Vukobratovic, Augustine Ikpehai, Kennedy Chinedu Okafor, Sabita Maharjan, Bamidele Adebisi
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has become the game changer in smart grids-an enabler of network autonomy, self-healing, and reconfiguration. This study integrates AI and Internet of Things (IoT) to organise peer-to-peer (P2P) energy prosumers into virtual clusters without altering the physical topology of the power network. The aim is to enable an autonomous, scalable and dynamic virtual microgrids (VMG) by leveraging federated learning, agentic AI, AI agents, IoT, and cluster zooming to optimise P2P energy trading costs for prosumers and operational expenditure (OPEX) for network operators, depending on the number of prosumers available. The study employs a central controller AI to coordinate multiple local AI agents. Each AI agent resides in the network server and monitors energy trading traffic for each long-range wide-area network (LoRaWAN) gateway to optimise trading and OPEX costs via cluster zooming achieved by the spreading factor (SF) via adaptive data rate (ADR) mechanism of LoRaWAN. The agentic AI module in the cloud autonomously selects and adapts the network coverage based on SF, via the AI energy trading agent configured in the LoRaWAN access network server, to zoom the clusters (i.e., VMGs) in grid-connected and island modes. The study formulates an energy trading model connecting the physical (electrical) and virtual (telecom) distances and OPEX in the VMG. With agentic AI-assisted cluster zooming, over 70% of the energy is traded at lower SF. At the same time, the energy costs decrease by 40% in proportion to the network size and the number of prosumers. For the network operator, OPEX reduces by 21% and 38% in base-station power consumption. Ultimately, grid-connected prosumers pay higher charges than their off-grid counterparts. The agentic AI model in this study exemplifies a use case of the 3GPP model of the future 6G network.

title: Batch Assessment of Short-Term Voltage Stability Based on Proactive Searching of Critical Operating Point
authors: Zhe Ye, Yijiang Wu, Xin Wang, Yuzhong Gong, Guangchao Geng, Quanyuan Jiang, Shiyang Li
abstract: Short-term voltage stability assessment (STVSA) plays a critical role in ensuring the stable operation of power systems, yet it suffers from excessive time consumption and computational burdens. While existing data-driven approaches accelerate the assessment by replacing time-domain simulations, this letter proposes a proactive search method of critical operating point (OP) in extensive OP space to further enhance the efficiency of batch STVSA. First, by predicting the voltage stability index through a convolutional neural network (CNN) to proactively identify critical OPs, the assessment focuses on the OPs with the lowest stability margins. Then, leveraging Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) analysis, substantial OPs with elevated instability severity are proactively searched for targeted assessments. This methodology effectively enhances the efficiency of STVSA by eliminating redundant assessments of evidently stable OPs. Case studies on a realistic 10,000-bus test system in the China Southern Power Grid validate the accuracy and efficiency of the proposed approach.

title: Copyright Pages
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Green Technologies, Renewable Energy and Ecological Sustainability (MetroGREENST)

title: Image Encryption Based on 3D Rubik’s Cube, Arnold Scrambling, and Hyperchaos
authors: Ghada O. Mohamed, Wafaa S. Sayed, Lobna A. Said
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Most current Rubik’s Cube-based image encryption methods use elementary cyclic shifts or 2D pixel-surface mapping. However, the complexities of true 3D spatial transformations can provide notable security enhancement. In this paper, we propose a color image encryption scheme that integrates three core algorithms controlled by an integer-order 5D hyperchaotic system: a 3D Rubik’s cube scrambling algorithm that processes all image data, a double diffusion mechanism, and an enhanced plaintext-dependent Arnold transformation. To ensure complete pixel scrambling, the image passes through 3D spatial scrambling, driven by chaotic sequences that adapt to Rubik’s cube rotations. After scrambling, the image is combined with a double-diffusion mechanism to enhance security. This involves applying bit-level diffusion through binary rearrangement and incorporating chaotic diffusion with XNOR-XOR operations. Additionally, non-linear confusion is introduced via an S-box substitution, followed by the improved Arnold transformation. Security analysis indicates that encrypted outputs consistently pass the NIST SP 800-22 randomness tests and show strong resistance against various types of attacks.

title: Temperature-Adaptive Inference via Dynamic Switching of Pre-Pruned Models for Satellite Edge Computing
authors: KeHan Meng
abstract: The proliferation of deep learning has extended model deployment from ground-based servers to resource-constrained edge devices, with satellite edge computing emerging as a critical frontier. However, satellites face unique thermal management challenges due to the lack of passive cooling, leading to performance degradation through hardware frequency throttling when temperatures exceed operational thresholds. Conventional solutions, such as static model pruning, result in a permanent loss of accuracy and underutilization of available computational resources. This paper proposes a novel temperature-adaptive inference (TAI) method that dynamically balances computational workload and thermal constraints. We first generate a family of models with varying parameter counts by applying a structured pruning technique to a baseline deep neural network. An adaptive model selection algorithm is then introduced, which intelligently switches between these model versions in real-time. The selection is based on a holistic assessment of the current device temperature, its rate of change, and the hardware’s known frequency-throttling characteristics. This approach allows the system to operate within a safe thermal envelope while maximizing performance, effectively navigating the trade-off between inference speed and prediction accuracy. Our experiments, conducted on a high-resolution satellite imagery dataset for ship detection, demonstrate that our method maintains stable operating temperatures and achieves a superior balance of efficiency and performance compared to traditional throttling or static pruning approaches.

title: Conversational AI for Workday HCM Designing Ethical and Human-Centered Digital Assistants
authors: Abhishek Jain, Monu Sharma, Shivareddy Devarapalli
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: In the evolving digital workplace, human capital management (HCM) systems increasingly rely on artificial intelligence to enhance employee engagement, automate HR functions, and support decision-making. However, the integration of Conversational AI in HCM raises challenges related to ethics, transparency, and human-centric design. This study addresses the problem of developing an ethical and intelligent conversational framework for Workday HCM that balances automation efficiency with user trust and inclusivity. To major objective is to design a human-centered digital assistant capable of ethical decision-making, contextual understanding, and adaptive communication across diverse workforce scenarios. The Transformer architecture is used for high-dimensional feature extraction from textual and interactional data, followed by multi-objective swarm optimization (MOSO) to select optimal feature subsets. The fuzzy conversational deep neural network (F-CDNN) is used to capture uncertain and context-dependent user intents, ensuring adaptability and fairness in conversational flow. The study employs enterprise-level recruitment and financial data from the RESSET Chinese enterprise big data platform and the CSMAR database (2016–2023) to validate model performance and ethical robustness. Experimental findings demonstrate that the proposed Conversational AI significantly improves dialogue accuracy, ethical compliance, and user satisfaction while reducing bias and misinterpretation compared to existing HCM assistants. This work contributes to the emerging discourse on responsible AI in workplace systems by providing a scalable, interpretable, and human-aligned digital assistant framework for next-generation HCM.

title: Flight Fare Prediction Using Machine Learning
authors: Monika Singh, Niharika Singh, Priyansh Aggarwal, Madhur Panwar, Parth Gupta
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: Airline ticket price forecasting remains a challenging task due to dynamic revenue management strategies, competitive market behavior, and temporal demand variations. In this paper, the authors have proposed a machine learning-based framework for the prediction of airfare, which is based on traditional regression models, further complemented with systematic feature engineering in order to capture demand and competitive patterns. Constructed features include days to departure, holiday/weekend indicators, route distance, and rolling fare statistics. Normalization, categorical encoding, outlier filtering, and temporally aware train-test splits are proposed in data preprocessing. The results are presented showing that, among the compared techniques, the best performance relates to ensemble methods-particularly Random Forests-with an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> of 0.81 and MAPE at 13.3%, outperforming single regressors and naïve baselines. SHAP-based analysis enhances interpretability by underlining key drivers like booking windows and holiday effects. The proposed approach demonstrates an effective, interpretable, and computationally efficient solution suitable for deployment in consumer-centric airfare forecasting systems.

title: Power System Operating Mode Extraction Method Based on Chronological Production Simulation and Expert Experience Indices
authors: Songtao Zhang, Dian Chen, Runzhao Lu, Zhenjun Gao, Xiaoqi Zhang, Xuetao Dong, Xin Ma, Guoqiang Lu
publish: 2025 4th Asia Power and Electrical Technology Conference (APET)
abstract: Power system operating modes employed for security and stability analysis should accurately capture the critical challenges encountered in power system operation. With the growing penetration of new energy, operating modes have become increasingly complex, while manually constructed scenarios suffer from subjectivity and insufficient scientific rigor. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel method for extracting operating modes based on chronological production simulation and expert experience indices. Key factors, including load demand, AC/DC transmission power, new energy generation output and the committed capacity of synchronous units, are utilized to establish expert indices for evaluating power system stability, identifying heavy-load conditions, and characterizing high new energy generation scenarios. The chronological production simulation results are then utilized to compute indicator values for all annual operating modes. Seasonal rankings of these indices are further applied to extract representative operating modes. The case study on the Northwest China power grid demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: Blockchain and Fog Computing Based Smart E-Voting System with Edge Integration
authors: Tarun Kumar, Tanu, Kadugula Joel, Ashish Kumar
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: As digitalisation transforms the delivery of public services, the cry for secure and transparent voting systems has never been louder! However, blockchain solution has experienced problems in latency, scalability and energy-consuming consensus mechanism, despite its promising ability to provide integrity and immutability of voting record. To overcome these disadvantages, some recent studies have considered hybrid architectures of fog and edge computing and its integration with blockchain. In this paper, a systematic review and comparison of 20 cutting- edge models is conducted with regard to architectural evolution, cryptographic mechanisms and system performance in the context of blockchain-fog-edge convergence. As compared with previous surveys, this survey aggregates the scattered papers into one unified framework and presents an original comparative table that charts technologies with technical limitations. The gap analysis matrix and future directions for a deployable, voters- centric and regulation-compliant e-voting system are also given in the study

title: SWOT-Based Assessment of Solar Energy Integration into Power Systems in the GCC Region
authors: Addalla Mahmoud Salim, Saleh Abu Dabous
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: The Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) region is undergoing a strategic energy transition, driven by the need to reduce fossil fuel dependence and enhance sustainability through solar energy integration. This study presents a strategic SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) analysis to systematically assess the internal and external factors influencing solar energy integration in the GCC. These factors are categorized across five domains: technical, industrial, economic, legal, and environmental. A mixed-methods approach was adopted, integrating expert interviews and secondary data analysis from national and international energy sources. The analysis identifies key factors such as abundant solar irradiance, cost-competitive utility-scale photovoltaic (PV), and sovereignbacked infrastructure investment. At the same time, the study highlights persistent challenges, including underdeveloped local manufacturing, grid inflexibility, and inconsistent policy implementation. Opportunities from global climate finance and falling PV costs are weighed against threats such as supply chain disruptions, regulatory delays, and environmental risks. The findings provide strategic recommendations for enhancing institutional readiness, accelerating grid modernization, and promoting regional collaboration. This paper contributes to energy policy and planning by offering a comprehensive, regionspecific framework to support evidence-based solar energy deployment across GCC power systems.

title: Automated Hydroponic Plant Growth Chamber
authors: Snehal Shukla, Sanyam Agarwal, Tanya Chaudhary, Vashu Paliwal, Neeraj Sharma
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: Water shortage, limited urban space, and manual inefficiencies are some of the major problems in agriculture that this study aims to solve by introducing an automated hydroponic plant growth chamber. The system automates environmental modifications to enhance plant growth by integrating sensors (DHT22, pH, EC, and water level), an ESP32 microcontroller, and Internet of Things (IoT) connections. This environmentally friendly and scalable method guarantees exact nutrient delivery and environmental control while reducing water usage by 30% compared to soil-based farming. This technique offers a solution for sustainable agriculture in urban areas with limited resources.

title: A Comprehensive Review on Image Processing Methods for Identifying and Classifying Glaucoma Using Retinal Images
authors: Amit Kumar Mourya, Usha Chauhan
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: Eyes are precious organ for human. Human cannot imagine his life without eyes. Glaucoma affect a tremendous amount of people's vision and, in fact, it is main leading cause of visual impairment in the humans. Glaucoma stands the second main cause of blindness in the people. It is accountable for loss of vision of more than 80 million people in worldwide. In early stage of Glaucoma, it does not show any symptoms. So people are not aware of it and it becomes severe. The cost of the treatment is also high. Treatment for damage that has already been done is not possible, hence, it is important one take preventive measures to protect from Glaucoma, which can only be done through early detection. If early detection is achieved, it is possible to control the advancement of destruction of the optic nerve, which is the key element in Glaucoma. This paper will give brief introduction of Glaucoma detection and its classification.

title: Emotion Recognition in Autistic Children Using Convolution Neural Network
authors: Surendra Kumar Shukla, Vishan Kumar Gupta, Priyank Pandey, Harish Tiwari, Paras Jain, Atul Kumar Dwivedi
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: Facial emotion recognition (FER) is a vital element of perceiving and reacting to emotional information especially when using it in children with autism spectrum disorders. In this project, the authors investigate the application of Convolutional Neural Networks, with the transfer learning approach based on the Xception model, to classify six fundamental emotions: anger, fear, joy, sadness, surprise, and neutral expressions. The images that are in the dataset are labeled with these emotions and they were preprocessed and augmented to improve the training process. An analysis of the data showed that there were imbalances in the distribution of emotions, especially the fact that the sample emotions such as fear and anger were less, and the technique of augmentation was used to correct the situation. The model was trained with the help of the TensorFlow framework and resulted in an acceptable degree of accuracy on the test set. Furthermore, a further feature extractor (an autoencoder) was provided to get knowledge about the representation of data. Analysis statistics like accuracy, confusion matrix, and F1score, were employed to determine the performance of the model. The outcome indicates that the model is effective in detecting certain emotions such as joy and sadness, but some such as anger resulted in difficulties because they had delicate facial expressions.

title: Towards Secure Smart-Home Environments: Machine Learning for IoT Device Classification, Segmentation, and Botnet Detection
authors: Amjad Qashlan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Numerous threats, such as identity theft and the exploitation of IoT devices as botnets in DDoS assaults, might expose residential Wi-Fi due to the growing usage of IoT devices and their connectivity to home networks. To address this issue, this paper has developed ’Maaman’, a web-based application that automatically groups similar IoT devices into a separate section using machine-learning algorithms. By using Maaman, Traffic is tracked, botnet activity is identified, infected devices are isolated, and the user is notified. This makes residential Wi-Fi more secure by stopping hackers from propagating throughout the network.

title: Beyond The Desk: Organisational Deficits And The Necessity Of Administrative Controls For MSK Risk In Indian Higher Education
authors: Shaik Balkhis Banu, Ali Ateeq, Yogawallie R Rengasamy, Hasan Ali Razzaqi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: This quantitative study investigated the multifactorial drivers of Musculoskeletal Disorder (MSD) symptoms among higher education faculty in the YSR Kadapa Region, Andhra Pradesh, India, focusing on the interplay between physical, psychosocial, and health factors. Data were collected from N=200 faculty members across private and public institutions, with the sample size determined by Cohran's formula and analysed using SPSS. The primary structural equation modelling revealed that MSD symptomatology is a function of both Ergonomic Exposure and Job Satisfaction (JS). Ergonomic Exposure was the most significant direct predictor of MSD Symptoms (β= .650, p < .001), explaining 42.2% of the variance. However, a potent protective mechanism was identified: Job Satisfaction was strongly negatively related to Ergonomic Exposure itself (β = -.738, p < .001), confirming that psychosocial factors are critical precursors to physical risk mitigation. Organisational vulnerabilities were severe, with 100% of the sample reporting an absence of Occupational Health Programs and 64% reporting High Physical Workload, both of which were significant predictors of poor JS and high MSD symptoms. The findings necessitate a paradigm shift from purely physical interventions to an integrated organisational health strategy. Controlling MSD risk requires mandatory, systemic change, including implementing Administrative Controls such as establishing a compulsory OHS program, standardising work-hour limits, and mandating scheduled micro-breaks to utilise the protective effect of JS and reduce systemic MSK vulnerability rooted in organisational and demographic deficits.

title: Creative Technologies & Augmented Reality for Business Sustainability - A Literature Review for Impacts, Opportunities and Benefits
authors: Ari Happonen, Amitav M. Swapnil, Inna Sosunova, Zeph M. C. Van Berlo, Manuel B. Garcia
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Innovations in Engineering, Science and Technology for Sustainable Development (ICEST)
abstract: Creative technologies and specifically Augmented Reality (AR) technology's role in helping to solve technological challenges, push forward new novel business models, boost circularity, and enhance sustainability, which are all part of the core focus areas of this study. Currently, the widespread adoption is still limited due to high implementation costs, technical complexity, and a shortage of skilled professionals. Literature review on AR & creative technology solutions, covering sectors like teaching & education, industrial maintenance & operations, asset manufacturing, e-commerce, and health & wellbeing, was conducted. Largest academic publications index databases, inc. IEEE Xplore, Google Scholar, and Scopus were used. The study clarifies the impacts and benefits of AR and Createch from a sustainability perspective. We specifically focused on the effectiveness and usability of augmented reality in problems reduction, enhancing productivity, and improving the engagement of end-users in industrial services. AR was found to facilitate immersive training, to reduce product return rates through virtual previews, and to personalize user interactions, leading to increased operational agility and innovations. AR supports sustainability by promoting eco-friendly behavior & optimizing resource usage, and it enables lean production practices. Targeted recommendations for adding to adoption and mitigating growth barriers include investing in scalable, cost-effective AR solutions, improving technical infrastructure, developing industry-specific AR applications, and increasing specialized training for AR expertise. These strategies are essential for full AR's potential realization for sustainability enhancements and business practices transformations, for strategic asset management, and to support long-term innovations too.

title: A Vision Transformer with Shift Encoding for Early Screening of Oral Cancer in Clinical Images
authors: Maher Alrahhal, Mohammad Ahmad Qasim Al-Shabi, Talal Bonny, Nabeel Al Yateem, Abir Jaafar Hussain, Mawieh Hamad
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: ShiftViT-XAI (Shift Vision Transformer with Explainable AI) is a novel deep learning framework designed for early detection of oral cancer from clinical images. The framework leverages the ShiftViT architecture, which integrates patch-wise token embedding with stacked shift-encoding transformer blocks to efficiently capture both local lesion textures and global spatial dependencies. During training, stochastic depth regularization and a cosine learning rate schedule are employed to improve generalization and ensure stable convergence. Data augmentation techniques, including resizing, random cropping, horizontal flipping, and pixel normalization, enhance robustness to variability in clinical imaging conditions. Evaluated on the Oral Cancer Detection L dataset comprising 1,152 high-resolution annotated images, ShiftViT-XAI achieved a 97.97% accuracy, demonstrating balanced performance across cancerous and noncancerous classes. To support interpretability and clinical trust, Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (GradCAM) is integrated to generate saliency maps that localize diagnostically significant regions within each image, aligning closely with expert clinical assessments. The proposed ShiftViT-XAI framework combines high diagnostic accuracy, efficient transformer design, and explainable predictions, making it a promising tool for deployment in computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) systems, telemedicine, mobile health units, and community screening programs to facilitate early oral cancer detection and improve patient outcomes.

title: Design and Simulation of a Helmholtz Cage for CubeSat Attitude Determination and Control System Validation
authors: Aysha Almazrouei, Shamma Jamali, Abdalla Elshaal, Mohamed Okasha, Haitham Elshimy, Kresna Aji
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: The Attitude Determination and Control System (ADCS) is a critical subsystem in CubeSat missions. It is responsible for maintaining the satellite's orientation and stability in orbit. Reliable ground testing of ADCS units is essential to prevent mission failures caused by pointing errors or loss of control. This paper presents the design and simulation of a three-axis Helmholtz cage for validating ADCS performance in CubeSat missions. The system uses three orthogonal pairs of square coils to generate uniform magnetic fields within a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$40 \text{cm} \times 40 \text{cm} \times 40 \text{cm}$</tex> test volume. Such volume is large enough to test CubeSats up to 12U. MATLAB simulations showed that the cage can generate fields up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$166,000 \text{nT}$</tex> with about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 1 \%$</tex> uniformity. A closed-loop control scheme based on the International Geomagnetic Reference Field (IGRF) model was developed to compensate for ambient magnetic fields and replicate orbital magnetic conditions. Structural analysis confirmed that the aluminum frame supports subjected loads without coil misalignment. This system offers a low-cost and reliable platform for ground-based CubeSat ADCS testing.

title: AI for Equity (AI4E): A Capability-Oriented Framework for Digital Learning Under Extreme Constraint
authors: Constantine Andoniou
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: The worldwide community backs digital education expansion as it provides universal learning access although these advantages do not reach students at equal levels. Learning through digital methods remains an unattainable goal for numerous students who live in rural areas, isolated locations and those who face economic limitations. The learning environment in these settings remains defined by ongoing challenges which include unstable power supply, no internet connectivity, insufficient educational facilities and unprepared teaching staff. The fast development of artificial intelligence (AI) technology threatens to make educational gaps between students worse because it will create two separate educational systems for people who already have digital access and those who do not. The research explores fundamental, yet frequently disregarded, matters regarding AI system development for underserved populations who lack internet access, share devices or need strong learning approaches instead of technological resources. The paper develops the AI for Equity (AI4E) model as a specific framework which exists to function in extreme-constraint environments. AI4E operates based on the belief that AI functions as an independent tool which supports operations through local access, minimal bandwidth requirements using low-cost hardware and community-driven educational systems. The system consists of three interconnected elements which include adaptive access for internet-free AI learning, community-centered pedagogy for cultural and linguistic adaptation, and equity analytics for non-invasive participation gap detection. The paper shows how AI4E maintains learning operations through an example from a rural Sub-Saharan African context which standard AI systems fail to address. The framework supports the equity goals of SDG 4 (Quality Education), SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure) and SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities). The method reveals that AI education systems encounter a challenge because designers need to work with current limitations instead of designing for future digital inclusion infrastructure advancements. AI4E provides an operational method to establish digital education access for all students who face the highest risk of exclusion from AI-based learning systems.

title: DIVIAC: Library of Input Data Aware Approximate Dividers with Partial Exact Minimization
authors: Chandan Kumar Jha, Sallar Ahmadi-Pour, Sajjad Parvin, Rolf Drechsler
publish: 2026 39th International Conference on VLSI Design &amp; 25th International Conference on Embedded Systems (VLSID)
abstract: Approximate divider designs have been extensively explored as they are widely used in image processing applications. The design of approximate dividers is predominantly accomplished through the use of functional approximation, where the Boolean functions of the subtractor sub-blocks in the dividers are approximated by replacing them with a different Boolean function. However, the prior works have explored only a few Boolean approximations and evaluated the error metrics using uniform distributions. This does not effectively explore the design space and leads to suboptimal approximate divider designs. In this work, we alleviate the limitations of prior works as follows: Firstly, we perform an extensive and systematic design space exploration to identify the Pareto-optimal approximate divider designs, where each sub-block has been reduced through exact minimization. Secondly, we do this for three input distributions, namely uniform, normal, and exponential distributions. Lastly, we also evaluate the design on the widely used image processing applications for approximate dividers, namely background removal and change detection. We aim to make the Pareto-optimal approximate divider designs available as open-source to stimulate further research.

title: Quantum-Resilient Zero-Trust Architectures in Edge Computing Networks: Theoretical Foundations and Complexity Bounds
authors: Varshitha Manjunath, Sooraj Sathyanarayanan, Rajesh Purushothaman, Arun Kumar Elengovan, Jeevan Shanbhag, Rakesh Keshava
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: In the era of advancing quantum computing, edge computing networks face unprecedented security risks due to expanded attack surfaces and resource limitations. We develop a rigorous theoretical frame-work for quantum-resilient zero-trust architectures (ZTA) tailored to these environments. Our contributions encompass: (i) security reductions demonstrating that ZTA derives quantum resistance from lattice-based hardness assumptions; (ii) precise complexity analyses establishing Θ(log n) as the tight communication bound for n-node networks; (iii) fundamental tradeoffs between differential privacy and anomaly detection utility; and (iv) impossibility theorems delineating limits under quantum adversaries. Our work is the first to provide tight complexity bounds and impossibility theorems for quantum-resilient ZTA in edge networks.

title: A Systematic Review of Generative AI and Uncertainty Quantification Techniques in Predictive Maintenance
authors: Lasandi Randini, Lahiru Ubesingha, Dinesh Asanka, Thilini Mahanama, Chathura Rajapakse
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Innovations in Engineering, Science and Technology for Sustainable Development (ICEST)
abstract: This study presents a systematic literature review of generative artificial intelligence (AI) techniques in predictive maintenance (PdM), with a focus on uncertainty quantification (UQ). Analyzing over 40 publications from 2020 to 2024, addressing four key aspects: the role of generative AI in PdM with UQ, model types used and application domains. The findings reveal that generative models, such as Bayesian neural networks, variational autoencoders, and Monte Carlo methods, are increasingly applied to enhance model reliability, augment failure data, and quantify uncertainty in RUL predictions. These approaches are prominent across manufacturing, energy, and aerospace domains. Common datasets include NASA's C-MAPSS and PRONOSTIA. While interest in this integration is rapidly growing, challenges remain in adopting newer generative architectures and validating UQ outputs. This highlights emerging trends and proposes future directions to support the development of robust, uncertainty-aware PdM systems.

title: Vanadium Redox Flow Batteries: Sustainable Energy Storage Solutions for a Green Future
authors: Hafsa Anwar, Roberta Farina, Giuseppe E. Capuano, Maurizio Sacco, Maria Rosaria Plutino, Sebania Libertino, Fabio Matera
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Green Technologies, Renewable Energy and Ecological Sustainability (MetroGREENST)
abstract: The increasing reliance on intermittent renewable energy sources such as solar and wind power necessitates the development of robust, scalable, and safe energy storage technologies. Vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFBs) are promising candidates for grid-scale storage due to their long cycle life, decoupled energy and power capacity, and operational safety. This study reports the synthesis and characterization of a 1.5 M vanadium(V) electrolyte in 3 M sulfuric acid, optimized for VRFB application. The correct electrolyte preparation was confirmed visually and via pH analysis. A laboratory-scale VRFB was assembled using graphite felt electrodes and a Nafion 115 membrane to assess structural and electrochemical compatibility. The battery design enabled effective electrolyte circulation and demonstrated the suitability of the prepared electrolyte for redox reactions. These findings establish a foundational protocol for electrolyte formulation and battery assembly, supporting the advancement of small-scale VRFBs.

title: An Improved Multimodal Biometric Authentication System with Secured Templates
authors: Swimpy Pahuja, Navdeep Goel
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: There is a widespread use of biometric authentication systems for safe and reliable use verification. Among such systems, unimodal systems face the challenges of intra-class variability, noise and susceptibility of templates stored. This paper proposes an improved multimodal biometric authentication system that utilizes fingerprint and palmprint images, and a perturbation-based template protection system for better security. Series of operations including binarization, thinning, minutiae extraction, bifurcation, removal of spurious minutiae, etc. are utilized to obtain fingerprint characteristics, and Gabor filter is used to obtain palmprint characteristics. To achieve the revocability or non-invertibility, the characteristics of the two modalities are combined and then converted to safe templates through a simple hashing-based mechanism with perturbation. The experimental results on a publicly accessible dataset demonstrated that unimodal fingerprint systems can achieve high accuracy (98.63 %), but unimodal palmprint systems on its own are significantly advantaged by the perturbation strategy. The suggested system has superior performance (96.66 %) when perturbed, and small false acceptance and false rejection, which highlights the tradeoff between security and recognition performance.

title: Advanced Process Control (APC) Overview - How to Find and Justify Opportunities
authors: Richard J Van Fleet
publish: 2025 IEEE IAS Pulp and Paper Industry Conference (PPIC)
abstract: Advanced Process Control (APC) encompasses a suite of control strategies and technologies designed to optimize the paper-making process, enhancing efficiency, product quality, and resource utilization. While APC offers significant potential benefits, its implementation challenges include model development (dynamic, multi-variable, nonlinearity), data quality, and operational resistance. Furthermore, there is sometimes the unforeseen cost of integration with existing control systems and investment in instrumentation and analyzers. Lastly, the evolving nature of processes and external disturbances necessitates adaptive control strategies. Case studies demonstrate the successful application of APCs to deliver substantial economic benefits and operational improvements by overcoming traditional control limitations and achieving sustainable economic benefit.

title: A Systematic Review of Machine Learning and Deep Learning Approaches for Credit Card Fraud Detection
authors: GannaTullah Gouda, Abdelrahman Mahmoud, Ahmed Khaled Abdulfatah, Ali Mohamed, Shady A.Abdelmoneim, Asmaa F. Hassan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: The increasing sophistication of payment card fraud needs enhanced artificial intelligence (AI) treatments. This systematic review critically assesses the landscape of Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) applications in credit card fraud detection from 2021 to 2024 (77 studies), focusing on unsolved AI obstacles and potential for advancement. While popular algorithms such as Random Forest and Logistic Regression continue to thrive, and deep learning methods such as CNNs and LSTMs gain traction, our analysis reveals critical gaps in addressing fundamental AI problems such as robustness against adversarial attacks, effective concept drift management, comprehensive missing data imputation strategies, and the untapped potential of emerging AI paradigms such as Large Language Models (LLMs) and advanced optimization techniques. This paper is more than just a method review; it lays out a strategic AI research roadmap for future advances in fraud detection, highlighting the importance of explainable, adaptive, and scalable AI solutions to manage shifting risks and practical implementation challenges.

title: Development and Evaluation of Culturally Contextualized Speech Recognition Engine for Reading Assessment
authors: Angie C. Almarza, Ramil G. Lumauag
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Reading fluency, particularly in terms of reading speed and pronunciation accuracy, remains a persistent challenge among Filipino primary learners. Conventional speech recognition systems often lack cultural adaptation to Filipino-accented English, limiting their effectiveness in evaluating oral reading proficiency. This study presents the development and evaluation of an AI-powered speech recognition engine tailored for Filipino primary learners. Using Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs) for acoustic feature extraction from localized English speech and Fuzzy Support Vector Machine (FSVM) classifier enhanced with ADASYN-Tomek Links balancing, the model effectively distinguished between correctly and mispronounced words. Through the systematic training and hyperparameter tuning, the optimized model-at a regularization parameter C=10, achieved 88% accuracy, 87% precision, 85% recall, and an F1 score of 86% on unseen data. Further validation using confusion matrix and ROC/Precision-Recall curves confirmed its strong discriminative power and robustness under class imbalance. By integrating local phonetic characteristics, the study delivers a culturally contextualized assessment framework that strengthens data driven reading instruction and promotes inclusive literacy development in Philippine education.

title: Enhancing Pilgrim Crowd Management in Mina: From Digital Corridor Labels to Physical Coloured Streets
authors: Samar Ali Aseeri
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: The annual Hajj pilgrimage in Mina<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>, Saudi Arabia, is one of the world’s largest mass gatherings, drawing millions of pilgrims into a confined geographic area [1]. Managing these crowds is essential for safety, efficiency and the spiritual well-being of participants. Nearly 25 years ago Dr. Ali Aseeri<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> proposed a visionary idea: painting Mina’s streets in distinct colours to aid wayfinding for pilgrims who cannot read Arabic or do not have access to technology. Building on this inspiration—and on personal experiences during the 2015 Hajj where crowd collisions and confusion were observed—this paper advances two complementary components. First, we present a near-term, device-agnostic digital guidance system built around a Color-Aware Routing Algorithm (CARA) and its predictive extension (AI-CARA). The algorithms treat corridors as coloured categories within a mobile application, optimise routes based on crowd density and can be deployed immediately on existing pedestrian networks. Second, we outline a future physical coloured-street system, including materials, patterns and coding schemes, that provides intuitive guidance for pilgrims without smartphones. By distinguishing the digital and physical layers, integrating real-time data streams and commercial map services, and providing a phased roadmap, the framework aims to reduce disorientation, minimise collisions and support the Kingdom’s Vision 2030 for smart Hajj operations. We also review related work on AI platforms and digital crowd-management tools and present prototype demonstrations and figures to illustrate the architecture and proposed corridors.

title: Harmonic Source Localization in Distribution Networks Based on Data–Mechanism Fusion and Spatiotemporal Graph Modeling
authors: Fang Chen, Minghao Zou, Ming Yang, Zhengyu Wang, Zhuhua Chen, Yanyi Fu, Meng Liu
publish: 2025 4th Asia Power and Electrical Technology Conference (APET)
abstract: Harmonic pollution has become a critical concern in modern distribution networks with the increasing penetration of nonlinear loads and power electronic converters. Traditional model-based localization methods rely on precise network parameters, while purely data-driven approaches suffer from limited measurement coverage and the absence of synchronized harmonic phasor data. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a data-mechanism fusion method for harmonic source localization based on a spatio-temporal graph convolutional network (STGCN). A hybrid adjacency matrix is constructed by combining line impedance magnitude and nodal short-circuit capacity to represent both electrical coupling strength and energy-flow similarity among nodes. This fusion framework enables the STGCN to capture spatio-temporal correlations of voltage and current features consistent with physical energy-flow behaviors in the distribution network. Case studies on a modified IEEE 33-bus distribution system validate that the proposed method significantly enhances localization accuracy and robustness compared with conventional data-driven models.

title: Thermal Runaway Analysis of Analog PIM Accelerators
authors: Huatao Wu, Sandip Kundu
publish: 2026 39th International Conference on VLSI Design &amp; 25th International Conference on Embedded Systems (VLSID)
abstract: Processing-in-Memory (PIM) is a technology that integrates computing units directly into memory, enabling data processing at its source. This approach significantly reduces data movement and enhances computational efficiency, making PIM a promising solution for tasks that involve large volumes of data movement, such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). However, when computing units are deployed in 3D memory, the additional power consumed by computing units can significantly increase the temperature of the DRAM die, accelerating the current leakage of DRAM cells. As a result, DRAM needs to refresh more frequently to ensure data integrity. This extra refresh power consumption further increases the die temperature, creating a positive feedback loop leading to thermal runaway conditions. Consequently, DRAM die has to operate under a critical temperature to avoid thermal runaway through throttling or dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS). In this paper, we investigate the thermal runaway problem in PIM accelerators. We begin by proposing a novel analog PIM accelerator architecture. We then conduct a detailed power analysis and thermal simulation of a 3D DRAM to determine the system's operating temperature. This temperature is used to adjust the DRAM refresh cycle, which in turn influences power and thermal characteristics in an iterative loop. The process continues until the system reaches either a thermal steady state or experiences thermal runaway. Through this analysis, we identify the critical temperature threshold at which thermal runaway occurs for the proposed accelerator, under varying 3D DRAM stack heights and cooling solutions in in-memory computing environments.

title: Digital Humans for Lingnan Cultural Heritage: A Study on Integration of Fine-Tuned LLMs and AI 3D Reconstruction
authors: Weihui Zhang, Rongyi Wang, Jiongjie Wang, Cheng Liao, Feiling Chen, Shaobin Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer Science, and Information Processing (AICSIP)
abstract: This paper fine-tunes a large language model and embeds it into a digital human application, creating a digital human specialized in the historical development of Lingnan cities. The main work includes fine-tuning and evaluating the model, comparing it with other models to verify its effectiveness; using multiple AI-based 3D reconstruction algorithms to model the Nanyue King statue, assessing the model quality, and analyzing similarity through feature point matching; finally, identifying the optimal 3D reconstruction algorithm. The fine-tuned model' s API is then integrated with the best 3D reconstruction model, resulting in a digital human knowledgeable about the historical development of Lingnan cities.

title: Multimodal Emotion Detection and Classification of Audio-Visual Using Deep Learning
authors: Monika Srivastava, Pancham Singh, Manoj Kumar Sharma
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: Multimodal emotion recognition is a field of a human-computer interaction (HCI). It relies on various resources, which include text, voice, and facial expressions, to identify and comprehend the feelings of people. With the new developments in the field of deep learning and presence of various datasets, the field has grown a lot. This research paper focuses on how the field of medicine and learning may use deep learning algorithms to extract emotions on multimodal data. Some of the methodologies used to arrive at the state of emotions of individuals include gestures, physiological signals, facial expressions, neuroimaging technology, among others through textual analysis. Emotions and features extracted, the strategy used to classify, and the consistency of a database have an impact on the effectiveness of multimodal emotion detection. The article explains the special method of applying both the visual and auditory languages to differentiate between the speech or non-speaking messages within a frame and judge the feelings of the speaker. The research tries to elucidate the physiological indicators relating to scientific understanding as well as awareness of feelings.

title: Balanced Cascade: Free-Flowing Microfluidic Lab-On-Chip for Generating Serial Dilutions
authors: Tapalina Banerjee, Sudip Poddar, Bhargab B. Bhattacharya
publish: 2026 39th International Conference on VLSI Design &amp; 25th International Conference on Embedded Systems (VLSID)
abstract: Generating dilution gradients, i.e., fluid samples with systematically varying concentration factors, is a key function in automating biochemical assays using microfluidic lab-on-chips. Among these, serial (or exponential) gradients, where concentrations follow a geometric progression, are essential for applications such as sample preparation, yet their implementation remains a challenge with flow-based microfluidics, especially in passive systems without integrated control valves. In this work, we introduce the Balanced Cascade, a freeflowing microfluidic architecture designed to generate continuous and accurate serial dilutions using a planar, single-layer chip without the need for external control mechanisms. Our design leverages principles from electrical circuit theory, modeling the fluidic network as an equivalent resistive network to ensure that hydraulic resistances across all input-output paths are balanced so that the concentration profile is produced accurately. We provide a comprehensive physical design of the system, including channel dimensions, and interior obstacle geometries. The architecture is simple, cost-effective, devoid of any control valves, and thus amenable to mass fabrication using standard soft lithography techniques. Performance evaluation through COMSOL Multiphysics simulations confirms close agreement between theoretical and simulated concentration factors, demonstrating the accuracy and robustness of the generated gradient profiles.

title: Semantic Segmentation Guided Diffusion for Pose-Guided Person Image Synthesis
authors: Yunhan Dou, Jianjun Li
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer Science, and Information Processing (AICSIP)
abstract: The diffusion model is currently widely used in Pose-Guided Person Image Synthesis (PGPIS) and has achieved good results. However, existing methods simply extract image features from complete human images through encoders. Although this method can obtain human appearance, it ignores the relationships between human body part attributes and cannot obtain more accurate local features. In this paper, we propose a novel Semantic Segmentation Guided Diffusion (SSGD) method for PGPIS. To preserve the complex appearance of the reference image and enable more precise control and adjustment, we utilize semantic segmentation maps of the human body to obtain feature information for each body part, which complements the features extracted from the complete reference image. To ensure that every detail of the reference image is equally considered, including the face and clothing components of the task, the segmentation map is also used to refine the attention of the feature map. Additionally, we modified the skip connection method of the UNet in the diffusion model to better integrate semantic information from high-level features and details from low-level features. We evaluated our method on the Deep Fashion benchmark, where it achieves new state-of-the-art results. For instance, at 512×352 resolution, our SSGD lowers the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) score to 6.857 and increases the PSNR to 17.913, outperforming the previous leading method across all key reported metrics. By enabling fine-grained local modeling and relational reasoning of the human body, SSGD generates images that are more structurally consistent and realistic in detail, demonstrating exceptional capabilities in handling occlusions and complex poses.

title: Ontology-Based Disease Diagnosis in Root and Tuber Crops
authors: Safiatou Sore, Pengwendé Zongo, Ouedraogo Tounwendyam Frédéric
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Roots and Tubers such as cassava, sweet potato, potato, yam and taro play a crucial role in the African diet, with an estimated annual production of 240 million tons on 23 million hectares. Cultivated in a wide variety of agroecological environments and under various production systems, they are characterized by their underground storage organs rich in water, starch, sugars, and essential nutrients. However, these crops are highly susceptible to fungal, bacterial, viral, and nematode diseases, as well as abiotic stresses (drought, heat, low soil fertility), leading to yield losses of up to 30–60%. Cultivation practices, such as crop rotation, intercropping, and moisture management, directly influence their resilience to these stresses. To address these challenges, we propose an ontology dedicated to root and tuber diseases, developed using Protégé 5.6.8 in OWL. The ontology covers crops, pathogens, symptoms, treatments, and cultural practices and allows automatic inference of prob- able diseases from combination of symptoms, while suggesting appropriate treatment and management measures. Compared to existing ontologies (PDO, PSO), our approach stands out due to its specialization in African tubers, its capacity for symptomatic reasoning via Description Logic, its extensibility, and its adaptability to farmers’ needs through SPARQL queries. The validation was carried out by two plant pathologists and an entomologist, which confirmed the relevance of the modeled concepts and the logical consistency of the ontology. The main challenges concern integration with real-time data (imagery, IoT, machine learning), climate variability, and the standardization of local terminologies. The future lies in multimodal integration (CNN for visual detection, IoT for soil monitoring, ML for epidemic prediction) to strengthen the relevance of this ontology for smart agriculture and food resilience in Africa.

title: Topology Construction Algorithms for Communication and Localization Services in Multi-UAVs IIoT Networks
authors: Chuyu Li, Kwan-Wu Chin
abstract: This article studies a novel network or topology construction problem using uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs). Unlike prior works, it aims to construct a topology that jointly optimizes communications between ground users operating in an Industrial Internet of things network as well as provide localization services to users. It first outlines a mixed integer linear program (MILP) that maximizes the minimum flow-rate of ground users subject to localization accuracy. Its key decision variables relate to, first, the position of UAVs, second, routing of traffic between users, and third, link schedule. It then presents a heuristic called localization-aware UAV deployment and communication-aware relay deployment (LAUD-CARD) to position UAVs. The results show that LAUD-CARD is able to achieve 90% of the optimal or MILP result.
