title: MAPformer: A Multiscale Adaptive Patch Transformer with Expert Selection for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
authors: Zonggao Wang, Xiuli Xiang, Jingdong Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer Science, and Information Processing (AICSIP)
abstract: Multivariate time series forecasting remains a persistent challenge due to intrinsic multiscale temporal patterns and complex inter-variable dependencies. Existing methodologies often struggle to simultaneously capture hierarchical temporal structures and heterogeneous variable interactions. To address these limitations, we propose MAPformer, a novel framework for multiscale spatiotemporal modeling. The architecture begins by generating hierarchical multiscale representations via recursive downsampling, followed by bidirectional trend-seasonality decomposition to explicitly isolate dynamic structural components at each temporal scale. To further enhance representation learning, MAPformer incorporates (1) an adaptive patching strategy and (2) a lightweight routing-based attention mechanism, enabling efficient, structure-aware integration of localized cross-variable features. A dynamic expert selection module then aggregates multiscale predictions through weighted fusion, yielding optimized final outputs with improved accuracy and generalization. Extensive experiments across seven public benchmark datasets demonstrate MAPformer's consistent superiority over state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3. 4 7 \%}$</tex> average reduction in MSE. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each component, while sensitivity analyses underscore the framework's robustness across diverse configurations.

title: Involution Neural Networks for Fundus Image Classification: A Multi-Class Analysis of Retinal and Anterior Segment Diseases
authors: Maher Alrahhal, Mawieh Hamad, Abir Jaafar Hussain, Nabeel Al Yateem, Talal Bonny, Mohammad Ahmad Qasim Al-Shabi
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: Early and accurate detection of ophthalmic diseases is essential for preventing vision loss and improving patient outcomes. This paper presents a deep learning-based framework-InvoFundusNet (Involution-Based Network for Fundus Disease Classification)-for multi-class classification of retinal and anterior segment images. The framework integrates conventional convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with the advanced involution operator for spatially adaptive filtering. The Eye Disease Image Dataset, comprising 5,335 highresolution images from ten diagnostic categories, was utilized and augmented to a total of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 6, 2 4 2}$</tex> images to address class imbalance. Two model architectures-a baseline CNN and the proposed InvoFundusNet-were evaluated. Experimental results demonstrate that InvoFundusNet achieves an average accuracy of 99.47 %, F1-score of 89.38 %, and AUC of 0.9521 across all classes. Confusion matrix analysis and ROC curves confirm the model's strong discriminative ability, particularly in major classes such as diabetic retinopathy, glaucoma, and macular scar. The study highlights the potential of involutionbased models in ophthalmic image analysis and lays the groundwork for future integration into real-world clinical screening systems.

title: Human Heart Arterial Blood Flow Wave Propagation Analysis by SolidWorks
authors: Dalal Alawadhi, Fawaz Mustafa, Abbas Alshehabi
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: This research focuses on simulating arterial blood pressure and wave propagation through different arterial geometries using SolidWorks Flow Simulation. The study models three artery types: Pulmonary Trunk Artery for male and female patients (based on anatomical variance), and a simplified cylindrical model as a baseline. Simulations were conducted under pulsatile pressure conditions to study deformation, wave speed, and mechanical behavior. Results confirm that female arteries exhibit higher stiffness, lower wave propagation velocities, and increased risk for clot formation, whereas cylindrical models showed smooth pressure decline due to their idealized geometry. SolidWorks proved to be an effective educational tool for visualizing cardiovascular dynamics and supports the potential for CT-based, patientspecific modeling. In addition to comparing geometrical behavior, the study highlights the significance of pressure and radius relationships and how they vary between real and ideal arteries. The simulation output, including pressure graphs, wave propagation velocity, and normalization trends, provides a clearer understanding of how blood vessels react to internal loads. While the simulation did not include material properties or blood viscosity in this phase, the findings still reflect physiological principles supported by existing biomedical studies. This project serves as a foundation for developing accessible biomedical simulation tools in mechanical engineering education. It also encourages future research that incorporates CT-derived arterial geometry, pulsatile blood flow, and compliant material properties to enhance realism and clinical relevance.

title: A Federated Edge-AI and Fog-Cloud Framework for Real-Time Monitoring of Waterborne Disease Risks in Smart Agriculture
authors: Babacar Mbaye Faye, Ibrahima Niang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Communication, Networking and Services (ICCNS)
abstract: Ensuring access to clean water is critical for sustainable agriculture, yet conventional monitoring systems remain ill-suited for remote and resource-limited environments. This paper introduces a novel, multi-layered Edge-Fog-Cloud architecture that integrates Edge-AI with Federated Learning to enable real-time, privacy-preserving detection of waterborne disease risks in smart farming ecosystems. The system decentralizes intelligence across sensor nodes, edge devices, fog coordinators, and cloud servers each with distinct yet collaborative roles in anomaly detection, model training, and predictive analytics. Unlike traditional centralized systems, our approach significantly reduces latency, optimizes bandwidth, and ensures compliance with data privacy constraints by keeping raw sensor data local. Extensive simulations and real-world datasets validate the framework's ability to deliver high detection accuracy, low energy consumption, and rapid responsiveness making it highly applicable to the next generation of intelligent, resilient, and scalable agricultural infrastructures.

title: Physics-Informed Machine Learning for Service Life Prediction of Reinforced Concrete Under Real Environmental Actions
authors: Hala Al Moubayed, Houda Halawani, Baraa Altayabrrafei, Nancy Chendeb
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: We present a physics-informed, data-driven framework to predict the service life of reinforced concrete exposed to humidity, chloride corrosion, and freeze-thaw conditions. The approach combines performance-based descriptors (cover, apparent diffusion, chloride boundary and threshold, carbonation coefficient, freeze-thaw intensity) with in-situ sensing proxies (corrosion current, time of wetness) and trains regression and machine learning models to estimate service life. A synthetic but physics-consistent dataset (N=800) spanning urban, marine, and cold climates is released to enable reproducible evaluation. Gradient boosting delivers the best accuracy on a held-out test set, while feature importance confirms the dominance of physically meaningful variables. An ablation study shows that adding sensing features improves accuracy over physics-only inputs. Design curves illustrate actionable trade-offs: benefits of increasing cover saturate beyond about 60-70 mm, and service life decreases monotonically with freeze-thaw intensity. The framework supports optimizing design and material selection to meet target service life while reducing clinker content and embodied CO2. Beyond this contribution, future work will validate the model on multi-site field datasets and integrate it into digital twin platforms for continuous, uncertainty-aware monitoring of reinforced concrete structures.

title: A Systematic Review of Chronic Kidney Disease Using Machine Learning
authors: Ashish Kumar, Ram Kinkar Pandey, Prabhat Kumar Srivastava
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is a progressive condition affecting millions worldwide and ranks among the major global health challenges, causing millions of deaths each year. Early prediction and detection of CKD can significantly improve patient outcomes by enabling timely interventions before irreversible kidney damage occurs. In recent years (2018-2025), machine learning (ML) techniques have shown great promise in predicting CKD onset, progression to endstage renal disease (ESRD), and related outcomes. This review provides a comprehensive overview of state-of-the-art ML algorithms applied to CKD prediction, covering both clinical applications and technical methodologies. We discuss how ML models - from traditional classifiers (e.g., logistic regression, decision trees, support vector machines) to advanced ensemble methods and deep learning - are being utilized to identify CKD risk factors, forecast disease progression, and assist in clinical decision-making. Notably, ML models can leverage complex, high-dimensional healthcare data (electronic health records, lab results, etc.) to uncover non-linear patterns that traditional risk scoring methods might miss. We summarize recent studies and their outcomes, highlight the methodologies used, and identify research gaps. Key challenges such as data quality, model interpretability, and integration into clinical practice are discussed, and future directions are outlined for developing robust, explainable, and generalizable ML-based CKD prediction tools.

title: Robust Onboard DNNs under Single Event Upsets
authors: Qiyang Zhang, Hao Zhao, Tianshi Wang, Chen Yang, Fei Teng
abstract: The deployment of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) for onboard satellite applications, such as raw data processing and Earth observation, is rapidly expanding. However, Single Event Upsets (SEUs) caused by high-energy particles induce bit flips in memory-stored model parameters, compromising system reliability. Hardware redundancy and radiation-hardened components incur substantial cost and resource overhead. To address this, we develop a simulation tool to evaluate DNN robustness under SEUs and conduct extensive measurements across various bit-flip scenarios. Our results reveal that DNNs are particularly vulnerable to multi-bit upsets. We propose a lightweight, software-based method that combines 10-bit weight normalization with BCH encoding, correcting up to 3-bit errors with only 21.44 MB overhead. Experimental results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 using multiple models demonstrate that our method reduces vulnerable SEU events by over 100× compared with the baselines, with less than 3% accuracy loss, making it highly suitable for resource-constrained satellite platforms.

title: Time Series Forecasting of Electrical Load Using LSTM Networks with Comparative Analysis of Traditional and Deep Learning Methods
authors: Mohamed Shili, Salah Hammedi, Amjad Gawanmeh
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: Reliable grid and effective energy management require accurate forecasts of electrical loads; therefore, in this study, we proposed a deep learning method based on Long ShortTerm Memory (LSTM) networks to forecast electrical loads time series. The LSTM model is capable of capturing temporal dependencies and nonlinearities demonstrated in the patterns of power consumption. This proposed method was superior to traditional methods based on model performance in forecasting using a public dataset. The results show how to potentially use LSTM-based models to estimate load capacity in real time to improve demand response and smart grid capabilities.

title: Operationalizing AI in Cloud-Native Systems: A DevSecOps Framework for Secure, Scalable, and Cost-Efficient Engineering
authors: Manan Agrawal, Shalini Sudarsan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Cloud-native architectures and artificial intelligence (AI) have reshaped software delivery, but their convergence creates challenges in security, scalability, and cost control. Current practices in MLOps, container orchestration, and DevSecOps address these concerns independently, leaving gaps when AI workloads must meet production requirements simultaneously. We present an AI-augmented DevSecOps framework integrating four pillars: AI-driven security in Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines, reinforcement learning–based autoscaling, cost-aware orchestration, and explainable compliance. Evaluation on Kubernetes with NVIDIA A100 GPUs demonstrates 23% latency reduction, 35% faster vulnerability detection, and 22% cost savings versus rule-based baselines. Contributions include: (i) joint optimization of security, performance, and cost; (ii) explainable decision mechanisms; (iii) minimal adoption pathway for resource-constrained teams. The framework addresses operationalization—moving AI from prototypes to governed production deployments—by bridging MLOps reproducibility, DevSecOps threat mitigation, and cloud-native elasticity.

title: SecureSight: A Smart CCTV Surveillance Model Leveraging Computer Vision for Real-Time Threat Detection and Analysis
authors: Rajani Singh, Pranav Paliwal, Pratank Vardhan, Prajwal Kumar Sahu, Birendra Kumar Saraswat
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: The new GUI application based on Python presented in this report is called Smart CCTV. This project is an effort to develop an innovation to solve problems posed in the present Closed-Circuit Television. In this project, Computer Vision technology has been used, it is the technology of computer regarding visual information, this change will always bring the change in surveillance and security practices. The project can operate on many Operating Systems and it uses a common webcam with many advanced features such as an Anti-thief mechanism, a Noise Detection algorithm, a Visitor Counting capability function, a normal recording function, and a Face Identification technique among others. The project involves the latest computer advancement and python programming or simply it reflects how much computer vision can influence our lives in this era of today. Along with that, Smart CCTV makes life safer as well. Smart CCTV not only signifies the use of more safety measures but also points to the evolution from unintelligent surveillance systems to the smarter ones. The technical detail that supports it, design consideration, implementation approaches, and finally the future prospects for development are presented around this software package, accenting the importance of this package in contemporary instances of computerized monitoring for the protecting of lives of persons and property.

title: Multi-Dimensional Tail Classification in Procurement Spend Analysis: A Data-Driven Approach to Strategic Sourcing Optimization
authors: Magzhan Kairanbay, Najma Taqi, Basaveswar Reddy Mora Reddy, Ahmed Bahar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Effective procurement tail spend management rep-resents a significant opportunity for organizational cost optimization, yet identifying tail spend categories remains challenging due to the multi-dimensional nature of procurement data. This study presents a comprehensive analytical framework for classifying procurement tail spend using three distinct methodologies applied to a large industrial dataset of 24,983 transactions. Our analysis reveals that 88.5% of transactions exhibit tail characteristics under at least one classification method, with significant overlap across multiple dimensions. By employing F1-score evaluation, we identify 21 critical material groups representing 38.59% of transactions but only 9.56% of total spend value, achieving an F1-score of 0.65 and IoU of 0.48 against domain expert classifications. These findings demonstrate the importance of multi-dimensional analysis in procurement optimization and provide actionable insights for strategic sourcing initiatives in the aluminum manufacturing industry.

title: Machine Learning-Aided Design and Ansys Simulation of a High-Performance PTFE/Al Triboelectric Nanogenerator for Healthcare Applications
authors: Amaresh Kumar Sahu, Manoj Kumar Nigam, Susanta Kumar Patro, Pruthwijeet Patnayak
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: In this paper, we have analysed how Transformative energy-harvesting technologies in the triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) have emerged as self-powered, flexible, and miniaturised solutions for personalised healthcare. TENGs facilitate the battery less operation of therapeutic systems, implantable devices, and sensors by converting biomechanical and acoustic energy into electricity. New developments include textile-based TENGs with high output for biosensing that can be used, within iTENG-powered pacemakers which interact with heart movement, and biodegradable ultrasound-responsive devices for short-term medical uses. To make the suitable design of a revolutionary TENG device using polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and aluminium (Al), this study assesses existing TENG technologies. Ansys was used to model and simulate the TENG architecture, using mechanical pressures ranging from 100 to 300 kPa. With peak open-circuit voltages of 120 V and short-circuit currents of 15 micro ampere, the simulation results showed a strong electrical output. The acquired design and performance data were then thoroughly analysed using machine learning techniques.

title: Wearable Smart Slippers: Integrating Fall Detection, Heart Rate, and Blood Pressure Monitoring for Elderly Care
authors: Mukesh Kumar, Noor Mohd, Hina Singh, Vivudh Fore, Dev Baloni, Madhulika Mittal
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Advanced Computing and Emerging Technologies (ACET)
abstract: People who are elderly often have difficulty performing daily tasks due to diminished cognitive, physical, and sensory abilities. Reduced functions raise the possibility of falls, which are catastrophic. It is desirable to develop fall detection technology to detect falls. Here an innovative solution is presented in form of Smart Slippers for detecting falls and altered heart rate and blood pressure using machine learning. A system is evaluated based on a variety of parameters, including datasets, ML algorithms, sensors, and sensor placement. This concept can be put into practice by identifying or monitoring the typical behaviours of elderly persons, such as walking, running, heart rate, blood pressure, and falls. Slippers will be the most effective use because they may be always worn by each and everyone. Force and perspiration sensors used in the slippers will enable to detect falls and changes in heart rate or blood pressure. And in such critical situations, a warning message will be delivered to the connected device utilizing a cloud-based algorithm. In the case of injury, this may help to minimize significant loss.

title: Fairness-Driven Resource Allocation for RIS-Assisted NOMA in Massive MIMO Systems
authors: Zaid Albataineh, Haythem Bany Salameh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Communication, Networking and Services (ICCNS)
abstract: Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) have emerged as a promising solution to enhance wireless communications by intelligently manipulating the wireless propagation environment. However, RIS-assisted massive Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) systems employing Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) are prone to inter- and intra-beam interference. This paper proposes a fairness-driven resource allocation framework for RIS-based NOMA systems. The proposed framework utilizes an alternating optimization strategy to dynamically allocate resources, including the power distribution and the RIS reflection coefficients, to achieve maximum-minimum fairness. The presented method effectively mitigates inter-beam spectral interference and improves fairness while maintaining computational efficiency by iteratively optimizing power allocation and digital precoding vectors. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms conventional techniques in terms of the minimum achievable rate, demonstrating its potential for deployment in next-generation wireless networks.

title: Silicon Particles for Improved Energy Density Anodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries
authors: Dominika Capkova, Hugh Geaney, David McNulty, Kevin M Ryan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Green Technologies, Renewable Energy and Ecological Sustainability (MetroGREENST)
abstract: With the escalating demand for efficient energy storage solutions driven by global energy challenges, lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries have emerged as critical components across diverse sectors, including electric mobility and aerospace applications. To address limitations in energy density and cycle life, numerous enhancement strategies have been explored. Among these, the incorporation of silicon into the anode material has garnered significant attention due to its high theoretical specific capacity and potential to substantially improve overall battery performance. Therefore, in this study, the focus is on silicon anode material and its optimisation by varying the amount of silicon and electrolyte composition. Anodes containing 20% and 30% silicon microparticles were prepared. The cells were analysed using three different electrolytes to enhance the battery lifetime. The electrolyte has a considerable impact on the overall cell performance. The optimised electrolyte with additives prolonged the cycle life up to 300 cycles, with the capacity of 629 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{mAhg}^{-1}$</tex> and 487 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{mAh}{\mathrm{g}}^{-1}$</tex> for 20% and 30% silicon anodes, respectively, corresponding to capacity retentions of 56% and 36%.

title: Optimized Energy Interconnection Dispatch of Multi-Feeder Systems for Low-Carbon Economic Operation
authors: Sihao Zhu, Yongjun Zhou, Yirui Zeng, Mingyi Guo, Lijia Zheng, Yandan Xiao, Ziang Zhu, Yun Zhou
publish: 2025 4th Asia Power and Electrical Technology Conference (APET)
abstract: Against the backdrop of vigorous advancement of the dual-carbon goals, distributed energy resources are poised for a phase of rapid growth. Their inherent intermittency and volatility pose challenges to the operation and dispatch of traditional distribution feeder areas. As relatively independent systems, distribution feeder areas can mitigate the output fluctuations of distributed generation through smart interaction of source-grid-load-storage, facilitating the grid-friendly integration and local consumption of distributed generation, thus holding broad prospects. To achieve effective management of distributed generation within feeder areas and better leverage their capacity to accommodate renewable energy and adjustable resources, an optimized dispatch strategy for interconnected multi-feeder areas is proposed. By interconnecting feeder areas via tie-lines, a multi-timescale coordinated dispatch model for source-load interactions in multi-feeder area systems incorporating electric vehicles and energy storage is constructed, with the objective of minimizing the total system operating cost. The model is subsequently solved using the CPLEX solver. A case study based on an actual test zone is conducted, analyzing the interconnection of several typical feeder areas in the region. Under the premise of satisfying load demand, day-ahead scheduling and intraday adjustments are employed to rationally dispatch resources within the system, enabling energy transfer and sharing among feeder areas, enhancing their operational economy, and verifying the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Influence of Irrigant Viscosity and Canal Curvature on Root Canal Irrigation Dynamics: A CFD Study
authors: Akram Joda, Amir I O Ibrahim, Bahaa Mourad
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: This study aims to investigate the effect of root canal irrigant viscosity and root canal curvature on irrigation fluid dynamics using computational fluid dynamics (CFD). Three root canal geometries were generated with different curvatures of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0^{\circ}, 40^{\circ}$</tex>, and 60°, and for each canal curvature, five different irrigant viscosities were used. The dynamics of the irrigant flow were evaluated using COMSOL Multiphysics. The results showed that using higher-viscosity irrigant fluids produced higher local flow velocities near the needle outlet but limited flow movement in narrow and curved canals, as well as towards the apical zone. Wall shear stress was mainly concentrated near the needle outlet and outer canal walls. Irrigant fluids with higher viscosities produce higher shear stress magnitudes and distributions, which can improve cleaning, but they can restrict flow in narrow and curved canals. Apical pressure decreased with reduced viscosity for all models. It was found that both canal curvature and irrigant viscosity can significantly affect root canal irrigation dynamics.

title: Spectrum Sensing and Planning in Cognitive Radio Networks Using TD3 Deep Reinforcement Learning Algorithm
authors: Sree Vaishnavi Yalakaturi, T. Ramashri
publish: 2025 Advances in Science and Engineering Technology International Conferences (ASET)
abstract: Spectrum planning is crucial for the development and deployment of future generations of wireless communication networks, including 5G and potential 6G networks, as they are expected to support significantly higher data rates and increased network capacity. Effective spectrum planning is crucial for allocating frequency bands that can accommodate the increasing demand for high-speed data services. Cognitive Radio (CR) plays a vital role in spectrum planning by addressing the challenge of spectrum scarcity and inefficiency. This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning-based algorithm for efficient CR spectrum planning in future 5G/6G Networks. For this, the Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) Algorithm is incorporated, where the TD3 agents identify whether the spectrum is vacant or non-vacant based on the mother node (MN) aggregated information. Furthermore, the users are directed to the available spectrum with the utilisation of CR, thus enhancing spectrum planning and spectrum sensing. Then the threshold optimization of the algorithm is studied to achieve a trade-off between the Probability of detection and the probability of false alarms to reduce the total error rate.

title: Snooker-Based Optimization Algorithm for Optimal FACTS Allocation in a Practical 30-Bus Iraqi Distribution Network
authors: Khawlah Yahya Rebat
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Radial distribution systems (RDS) commonly experience voltage drops, elevated active and reactive power losses, and instability due to long feeders and fluctuating load conditions. These issues become more severe in practical public networks, where infrastructure upgrades are limited by economic and technical constraints. In this study, the Snooker-Based Optimization Algorithm (SBOA) is utilized as a modern and computationally efficient metaheuristic to determine the optimal placement and sizing of two FACTS devices— Static VAR Compensator (SVC) and Thyristor-Controlled Series Capacitor (TCSC)—in a real 30-bus radial distribution feeder located in the Al-Jamia area of Al-Diwaniyah, Iraq.Load flow analysis is conducted using the Primitive Impedance-Based Distribution Load Flow (PIDLF) method, while both Normal and ZIP load models are incorporated to ensure realistic representation of load behavior .The main objective is to demonstrate the superior effectiveness of SBOA in minimizing active and reactive power losses and improving voltage stability.Results demonstrate that SBOA markedly reduces power losses and improves VSI and voltage profiles, confirming its high efficiency for optimal FACTS allocation in radial distribution networks

title: Enhancing Low-Frequency Trading with Rule-Guided Reinforcement Learning
authors: Mohammed El-Hajj
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: The increasing integration of Machine Learning (ML) into financial trading has predominantly focused on high-frequency strategies reliant on raw data processing, often at the cost of computational efficiency and interpretability. This work explores an alternative paradigm for low-frequency trading by combining rule-based structural features—such as pivot points and support/resistance levels—with adaptive Reinforcement Learning (RL). While many RL-driven trading systems operate as opaque "black boxes," struggling with generalization and regulatory scrutiny, this approach embeds domain-specific knowledge directly into the agent’s state representation and action space, enhancing transparency without sacrificing adaptability. A Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent was trained on EUR/USD forex data (2015–2022) and evaluated on out-of-sample data (2023–2024), leveraging constrained action mechanics and dynamic reward shaping aligned with market structure. The agent achieved a Sharpe ratio of 4.41, a Calmar ratio of 6.69, and a maximum drawdown of 1.53%, demonstrating robust risk-adjusted performance. Key innovations include pivot-driven state encoding, limit-order execution logic, and asymmetric payoff profiles that balance frequent small losses from stop-loss triggers with infrequent but substantial gains. By reducing computational costs by 70–80% compared to high-frequency systems, this framework offers a practical solution for real-world deployment, bridging the divide between rigid rule-based strategies and purely data-driven models. Results validate that structural priors improve generalization in volatile markets while maintaining profitability, suggesting a path toward more resilient and auditable trading systems for low-frequency regimes.

title: RTL Information Tracker and its Applications in Quantifying the Security Impact of HLS Optimizations
authors: Nilotpola Sarma, Sunny Priyadarshi, Chandan Karfa
publish: 2026 39th International Conference on VLSI Design &amp; 25th International Conference on Embedded Systems (VLSID)
abstract: High-Level Synthesis (HLS) offers several optimization strategies that enable performance optimization of generated circuits. However a few recent works highlight the security vulnerabilities introduced by such optimizations, threatening the integrity and confidentiality of security-critical hardware designs generated by HLS. In this work, we first develop an automated method that uses model checking in the background to track the information flow in Register Transfer Level (RTL) design. We then evaluate the trustworthiness of HLS-generated cryptographic hardware at the RTL using this tool. Specifically, we systematically investigate how different HLS pragma optimizations affect information leakage in cryptographic hardware. The tool takes a cryptographic algorithm in software and applies various pragma-directives (for optimization) and synthesizes them using HLS tool to generate the corresponding Verilog implementations at RTL. By analyzing the propagation of sensitive inputs in each design generated with a set of pragmas, it quantifies the information leakage associated with each set of optimizations corresponding to the pragmas. Our results provide insights into methods for securing HLS-generated cryptographic hardware against information leakage. The tool is fully automated, enabling scalable leakage detection across large RTL designs.

title: Edutainment Games in English Language Learning: An Empirical Study of Usage and Impact at AOU Bahrain
authors: Rupa Supriya Garnipudi, Hilda Jeyakumari, Meera Ramadas
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: The study on the usage of edutainment games in the English language classroom examines students’ perceptions, motivation, and self-reported learning outcomes associated with the use of edutainment games integrated into immersive super-game environments for English language learning. The research builds on the premise that integrating educational mini-games, quests, and narrative-driven tasks into interconnected ecosystems enhances learner engagement and supports the use of authentic language. A quantitative, descriptive survey design was adopted with undergraduate students at the Arab Open University in 2025 across Foundation, Level 1, and Level 2. The results show that students across all levels hold varied levels of perceptions toward edutainment platforms, reporting high motivation and strong perceived effectiveness in vocabulary development, grammar practice, and overall proficiency enhancement. The study concludes that edutainment games can enhance language learning when integrated within adaptive, developmentally responsive instructional frameworks that support engagement, autonomy, and academic progress.

title: A Novel Infrared-Based Non-Invasive Brain Stimulation Approach for Neurological Disorders
authors: Kanchan Verandani
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Communication, Networking and Services (ICCNS)
abstract: Non-invasive neuromodulation techniques are gaining attention as alternatives to traditional deep brain stimulation for treating neurological disorders. This study explores the use of short-wave infrared light to stimulate deep-brain regions without surgical intervention. By leveraging advanced photon modeling and optical simulations, the proposed system optimizes light pen-etration for effective neural activation. Experimental evaluations demonstrate its feasibility in delivering targeted stimulation while maintaining safety and efficiency. These findings highlight the potential of infrared-based neuromodulation for future clinical applications in neurotherapy and cognitive enhancement.

title: Application of Genetic Algorithm Optimization Neural Network in Predicting the Development Level of Regional Digital Economy
authors: Ziqing Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer Science, and Information Processing (AICSIP)
abstract: As a key driver for high - quality regional economic development, digital economy's development level forecasting is crucial for policy - making. Yet, current studies, mostly relying on econometric models, fail to capture the multi - dimensionality and non - linearity of digital economy. Hence, this paper proposes a GA - BP - based prediction model for regional digital economy development level, and takes Tai'an as a case. First, based on the connotation of digital economy, an evaluation system with 11 indicators from four aspects: hardware input, software input, digital transaction and digital life, is built. Data from Tai'an's 2000-2022 statistical yearbooks, government announcements and internet platforms are integrated, and the digital economy index is calculated via the entropy weight TOPSIS method. Next, a GA - BP hybrid algorithm framework is designed. Real - number encoding maps the initial weights, thresholds and hidden - layer nodes of BP neural network to chromosomes, with MSE as the fitness function. Parameters are optimized through operations to output the best network structure. The model's input layer has the historical digital economy index and four - aspect indicators, and the output layer gives the next-year prediction. Comparison results show that the GA - BP model can effectively and accurately predict the regional digital economy development level.

title: Generalizing Forecasts for Hospital Supplies with LLMs: A Comparative Study of Zero-Shot and Fine-Tuned TimesFM
authors: Nicolas Haxaire, Farah Mourad-Chehade, Alice Yalaoui, Patrick Nader, Hicham Chehade
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: Effective inventory forecasting is critical for hospital supply chain efficiency, especially when managing large catalogs of products with varying demand patterns. This work investigates the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) pretrained for time series forecasting, focusing on TimesFM in a univariate setting. A structured evaluation is conducted over ~5,900 hospital supply products classified into ABC-XYZ segments. The study compares zero-shot forecasting performance to fine-tuned variants trained on historical data, using a rolling forecasting setup with consistent context and horizon windows. Results show that fine-tuning improves accuracy in 8 out of 9 classes, with substantial nRMSE reductions (up to 75%) in high-variability demand segments. In contrast, zero-shot performance remains competitive in low-variability classes. These findings highlight the complementary strengths of zero-shot and fine-tuned LLM forecasters, offering both scalability and adaptability for hospital inventory management.

title: AI-Driven 3D Molecular Reconstruction and Generative Modeling for Property-Aware Molecule Design
authors: Anas Refaat Sabry, Alaa Adel Zaki, Mazen Ebrahim, Tamer M. Nassef
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer and Applications (ICCA)
abstract: The 3D molecular structure is one of the most important pillars in understanding chemical behavior, drug interactions, and material properties. However, obtaining 3D geometries using quantum simulations is computationally very costly, and many machine learning approaches still rely on reconstructing molecular structures from 2D representations such as SMILES and molecular graphs. This research presents an AI-driven framework that performs accurate 3D molecular reconstruction from 2D input and expands its capabilities in generation. It can generate candidates with known properties. The system integrates traditional methods such as GNNs to achieve spatial coordinate prediction with a generative latent model, allowing users to obtain controlled examples of valid chemical structures with specified properties. Experiments were conducted on the QM7b data set and demonstrated competitive reconstruction, achieving RMSD errors below 1.2 Å for stable molecules and bond-length deviations below 0.08 Å, while maintaining valid chemical connectivity. The predictive model accurately estimated quantum-derived molecular properties, attaining a test RMSE of 0.6433 for the HOMO–LUMO gap and showcasing stable learning behavior across epochs. The module achieved 82% chemical validity, 91% uniqueness, and 69% novelty in producing new molecular candidates. In addition, optimization of the latent-space allowed orientation of target properties, reducing the predicted molecular energy from 1592.80 kcal/mol to 1614.46 kcal/mol through gradient-based refinement. These results highlight the potential of unifying 3D reconstruction and generative modeling into a single, scalable framework for rapid molecular design.

title: Contents
authors: N/A
publish: 1999 Symposium on VLSI Technology. Digest of Technical Papers (IEEE Cat. No.99CH36325)
abstract: Presents the table of contents of the proceedings.

title: Boosting Active Defense Persistence: A Two-Stage Defense Framework Combining Interruption and Poisoning Against Deepfake
authors: Hongrui Zheng, Yuezun Li, Liejun Wang, Yunfeng Diao, Zhiqing Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Active defense strategies have been developed to counter the threat of deepfake technology. However, a primary challenge is their lack of persistence, as their effectiveness is often short-lived. Attackers can bypass these defenses by simply collecting protected samples and retraining their models. This means that static defenses inevitably fail when attackers retrain their models, which severely limits practical use. We argue that an effective defense not only distorts forged content but also blocks the model’s ability to adapt, which occurs when attackers retrain their models on protected images. To achieve this, we propose an innovative Two-Stage Defense Framework (TSDF). Benefiting from the intensity separation mechanism designed in this paper, the framework uses dual-function adversarial perturbations to perform two roles. First, it can directly distort the forged results. Second, it acts as a poisoning vehicle that disrupts the data preparation process essential for an attacker’s retraining pipeline. By poisoning the data source, TSDF aims to prevent the attacker’s model from adapting to the defensive perturbations, thus ensuring the defense remains effective long-term. Comprehensive experiments show that the performance of traditional interruption methods degrades sharply when these methods are subjected to adversarial retraining. However, our framework shows a strong dual defense capability, which can improve the persistence of active defense. Our code will be available at https://github.com/vpsg-research/TSDF.

title: A Review of 4H-SiC Detector Monitoring Applications in Fission and Fusion Reactors
authors: Frank H. Ruddy, Abdallah Lyoussi, Christophe Destouches, Laurent Ottaviani, Christelle Reynard-Carette
abstract: Semiconductor radiation detectors based on the 4H polytype of Silicon Carbide (4H-SiC) have many advantages for high-temperature, high-radiation and mixed-radiation applications. The wide band gap of 4H-SiC (3.27 eV) allows measurements at temperatures up to 800 °C and probably much higher. Conventional lower band-width semiconductors such as silicon or germanium are limited by thermally generated noise to measurements at lower temperatures and can require external cooling. 4H-SiC has a high thermal conductivity, high breakdown voltage, and high electron saturation velocity. Furthermore, 4H-SiC detectors have also been demonstrated to have outstanding radiation hardness. These properties make 4H-SiC Schottky barrier and p-n diode detectors compatible with radiation monitoring applications in fission and fusion reactors where measurements of the neutron fluence rate and energy spectrum characteristics are required and monitoring environments with temperatures up to and exceeding 800 °C can be encountered. In such environments, the detector service lifetime is a prime consideration. 4H-SiC Schottky barrier detectors have been demonstrated to be operational after <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">137</sup>Cs gamma ray doses up to 22.7 MGy and fast-neutron (E> 1 MeV) fluences up to 1.7 x 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">17</sup> cm-2. 4H-SiC detectors have been demonstrated to provide linear count-rate responses as a function of fluence-rate for thermal, epithermal and fast fission neutrons as well as gamma rays. In addition, a linear fluence-rate response has been demonstrated for deuterium-tritium (D-T) and deuterium-deuterium (D-D) fusion neutrons. For D-T neutrons, the 14-MeV energy exceeds the thresholds for <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">12</sup>C(n,α)<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">9</sup>Be and <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">28</sup>Si(n,α)<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">25</sup>Mg reactions and allows peaks to be observed from neutron reactions within the SiC detector. The energy widths and areas of these reaction peaks can be used to deduce information on the plasma temperature as well as neutron yield for fusion devices. 4H-SiC detectors with lithium neutron convertor foils can be used to monitor <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sup>Li(n,α)<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>H and <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</sup>Li(n,n’α)<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>H reaction rates to directly measure tritium breeding rates in fusion reactors. In this paper we will review relevant developments in 4H-SiC radiation detectors and discuss their potential and planned applications in test and research reactors and nuclear power reactors as well as fusion reactors and test facilities.

title: QGMNet: Question-type Guided Multi-view Network for Remote Sensing Image Question Answering
authors: Zhengying Zhao, Xiangtao Zheng, Ting Cao, Junhuai Li, Xiaoqiang Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote Sensing Image Question Answering (RSIQA) is a task that searches remote sensing images (RSIs) based on questions to generate accurate answers. These questions are categorized into different types, with each question type highlighting distinct semantics of the RSIs. Answering different types of questions helps non-expert users better understand the rich semantic content within RSIs. However, existing methods have not fully exploited question type to guide cross-modal interaction. To overcome this limitation, a Question-type Guided Multi-view Network (QGMNet) is proposed. The proposed network: 1) employs a dual-branch structure to extract global and local image features for comprehensive representations; 2) applies a question-type multi-view attention method to guide image-question interaction; and 3) adopts a question-type stage-wise training strategy with a joint loss integrating cross-entropy, focal, and contrastive losses for cross-modal learning. Overall, the QGMNet effectively leverages question type to achieve more fine-grained cross-modal interaction. Experiments on RSVQA-LR, RSVQA-HR, and RSIVQA datasets demonstrate that the proposed network achieves state-of-the-art performance.

title: Ising Placer: A Novel Framework for Placement Based on Ising Machine with Error-Correcting Multi-Stage Annealing
authors: Peng Wei, Hongyu Zhao, Wei Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: As conventional placement algorithms face growing challenges, Ising machines offer a promising path for accelerating electronic design automation (EDA). This brief proposes a novel Ising model-based placement framework utilizing a Sequence-Pair (SP) to spin-matrix mapping, a custom Hamiltonian for constraints and wirelength, and an Error-Correcting Flipping with Multi-Stage Annealing (ECF-MSA) mechanism to ensure solution validity. Validated on an Ising machine simulator, our framework, when compared to state-of-the-art commercial and academic PCB placement tools, reduces routed wirelength by 8-18% with an estimated 37x to over 431x speedup on future hardware. This work demonstrates the significant potential of physics-based computing for complex EDA problems.

title: Overview of Free-Space Laser Communications Technology State-of-the-Art: Challenges, Solutions, and Standards
authors: Don Boroson, Hamid Hemmati
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: Since it was realized that laser light could be used to transmit large volumes of data over long distances through free space, researchers have proposed very many options for each of the required subsystems. In this report, we try to briefly survey the major approaches for many of these subsystems and then list how recent standards have selected options from this list.

title: Unleashing the Potential of Coverage Representation in Deep Learning-Based Fault Localization
authors: Jinping Wang, Yan Lei, Huan Xie, Chunyan Liu
abstract:
Fault localization (FL) aims to identify suspicious statements in faulty programs that may lead to program failure. Coverage representation, which includes code coverage trace and test outcomes, has proven effective in FL and is widely adopted in deep learning-based fault localization (DLFL) methods. However, we find that the potential of coverage representation in deep learning-based fault localization (DLFL) methods is inadequately unleashed (i.e., in the case of using pure coverage representation) since DLFL has exhibited inferior performance compared to traditional non-deep learning-based fault localization (Non-DLFL).

Thus, we conduct systematic analyses by the three key steps of the DLFL task process: data-model-strategy. These analyses reveal that existing methodologies fail to handle three key scale variations in DLFL: sample scale of data, feature scale of model, and task scale of strategy. To address these challenges, we propose Muser, a multi-scale-aware deep learning-based fault localization method based on the data-model-strategy framework to unleash the potential of coverage representation in DLFL. Specifically, Muser addresses the three scale variations through a hierarchical design: for the sample scale, Muser employs a sample-scale-aware data augmentation method that dynamically selects optimal methods based on the sample size to mitigate class imbalance; for the feature scale, Muser uses a proposed feature-scale-aware adaptive LSTM backbone with model sizing adjustments to handle varying feature dimensionality in coverage representations; for the task scale, Muser further employs task-scale-aware modeling strategies to enhance robustness across diverse fault localization scenarios, thereby systematically improving model adaptability and performance. We conduct large-scale experiments to evaluate Muser, and the results show that Muser significantly improves pure coverage-based DLFL performance, e.g., the data augmentation method and the backbone neural network outperform the state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines (i.e., PRAM and RNN-FL) by 12.41% and 94.44% on average in Top-1 metrics, respectively.

title: Channel-Adaptive Secure Noise Modulation for IoT Networks
authors: Serdar Prencuva, Yusuf Islam Tek, Ertugrul Basar
abstract: This letter introduces channel-adaptive secure noise modulation (CAS-NoiseMod), a novel physical layer security (PLS) scheme for future Internet of Things (IoT) networks. Our approach combines noise modulation with channel-adaptive prescaling, where information bits are encoded through variance power switching of Gaussian waveforms and signals are scaled by the magnitude of the channel between legitimate partners before transmission. This creates an asymmetric advantage where the legitimate receivers benefit from knowledge of pre-scaling and reciprocal channels, while eavesdroppers experience unintelligible noise signals. Theoretical bit error probability (BEP) expressions are derived for the proposed scheme under Rayleigh fading. Our analysis reveals that secure communication is possible with eavesdroppers exhibiting near-random guessing performance, making CAS-NoiseMod suitable for secure IoT networks.

title: Mamba-Based Prototypical Contrastive Learning With Augmented Feature Separation for Common and Rare Arrhythmia Classification_supp1-3669893.pdf
authors: Jianxin Wang
abstract: Early diagnosis of arrhythmia, a common cardiovascular condition, is crucial for improving prognosis. Electrocardiogram (ECG) is widely used as a non-invasive diagnostic tool. However, Computer-Aided Diagnosis of rare arrhythmias faces significant challenges due to the severe scarcity of samples for these rare disease classes. To tackle this, we propose a Mamba-based Prototypical Contrastive Learning framework, which can simultaneously identify both common and rare classes under the setting of generalized Few-Shot Learning (FSL). It primarily consists of: (1) the Mamba-based Spatio-Temporal Feature Fusion Network (MST), which integrates spatial features from multi-scale convolutions and temporal dynamics from bidirectional Mamba for ECG modeling; (2) the Prototypical Contrastive Learning framework with Augmented Feature Separation (PCAS), which employs a prototype augmentation strategy with an Augmented Prototype Consistency Loss to optimize prototype representations, and an Separation-Tuned Contrastive Loss to enhance intra-class compactness and inter-class distinctnessy, mitigating the risk of class collapse. Extensive experiments on publicly available datasets PTBXL and Chapman demonstrate the effectiveness of MST-PCAS, achieving superior rare-class recognition accuracies of 79.13% and 50.72%, respectively, for ECG arrhythmia classification.

title: Rethinking Pansharpening via Correspondence-Aware Complementary Fusion
authors: Jiangyun Li, Xiaochen Zhang, Tong Gu, Hao Wang, Hao Du, Peixian Zhuang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Pansharpening aims to fuse a high spatial resolution of panchromatic (PAN) images with a rich spectral information of multispectral (MS) images for generating a high resolution multispectral output. However, most existing methods adopt unconstrained cross-modal fusion strategies, neglecting the inherent modality-specific characteristics of PAN and MS data. This usually results in inefficient interactions and an unsatisfactory trade-off between spatial detail enhancement and spectral fidelity preservation. To overcome this limitation, we propose CCFNet, a Correspondence-Aware Complementary Fusion Network that reformulates pansharpening as a pixel aware and modality-consistent information exchange process. The cross-modal interaction is explicitly constrained to spatially corresponding features, with a Cross-Modal Complementary Attention (CCA) module further facilitating efficient and localized bidirectional information exchange. To enhance unimodal representations before fusion, CCFNet incorporates a Semantic Guided Enhancement Module (SGEM) to selectively strengthen spatially salient structures in the PAN branch and a Sparse Token Attention Module (STAM) to capture critical spectral dependencies in the MS branch. Extensive experiments on WorldView-3, QuickBird, and GaoFen-2 benchmark datasets demonstrate that CCFNet achieves the state-of-the-art performance against existing approaches. Our code will be available at https://github.com/zhangxc0105/CCFNet.

title: Active Mode-locking All-fiber Laser with Switchable Multi-transverse-mode Operation
authors: Lei Gui, Siyuan Zhang, Zhaosheng Chu, Qiwen Zhan, Anting Wang
abstract: An active mode-locking all-fiber laser capable of transverse-mode switching is experimentally demonstrated using a dual few-mode fiber Bragg grating (FM-FBG) configuration. The laser system achieves stable mode-locking operation across five linearly polarized modes and eight orbital angular momentum modes with topological charges of l = 0, ±1, ±2, and ±3, enabling arbitrary switching among these transverse modes. Transverse-mode switching is achieved by cooperatively tuning a tilted few-mode fiber Bragg grating (TFM-FBG) and a non-tilted few-mode fiber Bragg grating (NFM-FBG) with a LiNbO3 Mach-Zehnder intensity modulator providing active mode-locked pulse generation. The TFM-FBG achieves highly efficient coupling from the fundamental mode to different transverse modes by breaking the cylindrical symmetry of the refractive index modulation in the fiber core, while the NFM-FBG acts as a mode-selective cavity mirror. Mode-locked pulses are generated with repetition rates of 7.71 MHz (fundamental mode) and 7.02 MHz (high-order modes), exhibiting signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 67 dB for all modes. This all-fiber mode-locked laser with switchable transverse-mode has potential for applications in material processing and nonlinear optics, and related fields.

title: Efficient Context-Aware Traffic Trajectory Monitoring From Roadside LiDAR for Occlusion Handling_supp1-3672508.pdf
authors: ZHIHUI CHEN
abstract: Frequent occlusions in complex urban environments pose a major challenge for roadside LiDAR-based traffic monitoring, often leading to fragmented trajectories and kinematic errors. To address this, we propose a context-aware framework that shifts the sensing paradigm from individual object tracking to lane-level platoon modeling. By encoding raw LiDAR data into Lane Occupancy State, we employ an encoder–decoder LSTM architecture to capture collective traffic dynamics, enabling robust trajectory reconstruction even during prolonged occlusions. To ensure real-time scalability, a hardware-aware embedding scheme is designed to directly parse data packets from mechanical LiDAR, achieving an inference efficiency of 18.75 ms/frame. Evaluations on real-world datasets demonstrate a 95.3% occlusion reconstruction rate with low kinematic errors (0.0055 m/s speed deviation), while an ablation study confirms that integrating an attention mechanism further boosts the reconstruction rate to 97.9%. This work establishes a robust, traffic-context-centric foundation for high-fidelity digital twins and intelligent transportation systems.

title: CoLDM: Component‑Wise Lead–Lag‑Aware Dependency Modeling for Multivariate Time-Series Anomaly Detection in IoT
authors: Yaqi Fan, Wenhui Lin, Kexin Chen, Xiangchao Wang, Jie Yang, Yuanyuan Qiao
abstract: In Internet of Things (IoT) and industrial IoT (IIoT) systems, interconnected sensors continuously generate nonstationary multivariate time series (MTS), where inter-variable dependencies vary across operating regimes. Consequently, anomalies may manifest not only as point-wise deviations but also as abnormal changes in dependency patterns, especially lead–lag interactions induced by propagation delays and system responses. Reliably detecting such anomalies requires accurately modeling time-varying inter-variable dependencies. However, existing methods mainly focus on instantaneous correlations or lagagnostic dependency learning in the feature space, thereby over-looking lead–lag-aware dependency structures which are crucial for detecting anomalies in nonstationary environments. We propose CoLDM, an unsupervised reconstruction-based framework that learns lead-lag-aware inter-variable dependency representations for MTS anomaly detection. CoLDM first extracts statistical lead–lag cues and converts them into a soft structural prior, which is injected via Structural Preference Modulation to guide dependency learning. Moreover, CoLDM adopts a componentwise dual-branch architecture to capture complementary lead–lag dependency information, and introduces a component-adaptive latent fusion module that adaptively integrates the two representations across regimes, reducing redundant overlap and improving robustness. Experiments on real-world benchmarks demonstrate that CoLDM generalizes well and remains robust under nonstationary regimes.

title: Action Recognition in RGB-D Videos via an End-To-End Cross-Modal Attention Transformer
authors: Yujun Ma, Benjia Zhou, Hong Zhang, Xizheng Zhang, Xiangjian He, Ruili Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Recognizing actions in RGB-D videos necessitates an in-depth understanding of spatial and temporal information from both RGB and depth modalities, as well as an efficient fusion of these data streams. Existing late fusion approaches often suffer from modality collapse due to high semantic similarity between modalities, where the fusion diminishes the distinct contributions of each modality and compromises overall effectiveness. In this context, we propose a novel End-to-end Cross-Modal Attention Transformer (E-CMAT) model. Our model processes RGB and depth inputs through two distinct expert encoders that utilize factorized spatio-temporal representations to capture dimension-independent features, enhancing motion understanding. The extracted RGB and depth tokens are subsequently fused via our innovative cross-modal attention, which is applied iteratively to ensure a robust integration that accentuates critical patterns and discrepancies between the modalities, thereby facilitating more precise action recognition. Our cross-modal attention utilizes one class token per expert for inter-expert information exchange, focusing attention on salient features and fostering synergistic predictions while reducing computational complexity from quadratic to linear. Furthermore, extensive experiments conducted on widely used benchmark datasets, such as NTU RGB-D 60, NTU RGB-D 120, and THU-READ, demonstrate the favorable performance of E-CMAT compared to state-of-the-art models.

title: Variable Switching Frequency Control Strategies for the SAB
authors: Alexis A. Gómez, Alberto Rodríguez, Aitor Vázquez, Javier Sebastián
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: This paper explores control strategies based on variable switching frequency for the Single Active Bridge converter to expand its soft-switching capabilities without additional components. While the conduction mode and the soft-switching features of this converter can be maintained across the entire operational range using variable switching frequency with a constant duty cycle, this work demonstrates that the use of both as control variables provides additional degrees of freedom to optimize performance. Specifically, two control trajectory selection criteria are proposed: one focused on minimizing the switching frequency range while reducing rms currents, and another aimed at maintaining constant recirculating current to ensure adequate ZVS across all operating points. Additionally, comprehensive design guidelines that integrate both control variables are provided. The proposed strategies and design methodologies are validated through a comparative experimental analysis of four 800 V to 400 V, 2.2 kW prototypes, each designed for the same specifications and utilizing a different control strategy.

title: Dynamic and Conditional Identity-based Broadcast Proxy Re-encryption with Autonomous Paths
authors: Liqing Chen, Shiyu Chen, Hao Zhang, Jian Weng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Cloud computing is commonly used by both individual and business users due to its powerful computing and storage capabilities. In recent years, identity-based broadcast proxy re-encryption (IBPRE) has been designed to realize more convenient and faster data sharing while guaranteeing the confidentiality of the data. IBPRE is capable of realizing one-to-many data sharing by re-encrypting the initial ciphertext, and then sharing the data with more users. In many practical application scenarios, it needs to satisfy the autonomous conversion of users' decryption privileges, support real-time dynamic management of users, and realize fine-grained access control. However, no existing IBPRE approaches realize the above three functions simultaneously. To this end, dynamic and conditional identity-based broadcast proxy re-encryption with autonomous paths (DC-IBPRE-AP) is proposed. We present the system model, formal definition, and security model of DC-IBPRE-AP, as well as a concrete DC-IBPRE-AP scheme. Compared to the existing IBPRE schemes, our scheme not only realizes the autonomous conversion of decryption privileges among the set of broadcast receivers, but also supports the dynamic user management and fine-grained access control of users. The proposed scheme achieves chosen ciphertext attack security. The performance analysis shows that the new scheme also has significant advantages.
